## Data Structures: Interview Questions

**Data structures are inherently coding-problem territory — understanding them well means writing efficient data processing code and choosing the right organization for a given problem.**

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
## Key Concepts

Data structures matter to a data engineer for three reasons: writing efficient processing code, optimizing memory usage in pipelines, and choosing the right organization for a given problem. Arrays offer fast, indexed access but are expensive to resize; linked lists trade that random access for cheap insertion and deletion by chaining nodes with pointers; stacks and queues layer LIFO/FIFO ordering on top of either; and more complex structures (hash tables, trees, graphs) each trade off differently on lookup, insert, and memory cost. A recurring interview thread is memory management — how allocation, free pools, and garbage collection reclaim space, and what happens when code mismanages a pointer after freeing it. Understanding these trade-offs is less about memorizing defi

description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.

nitions and more about being able to justify why a particular structure fits a particular pipeline problem.

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Data Structures" storagePrefix="data-structure">

<QuestionCard id="ds-01" number={1} question="What is a data structure?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
A way of organizing data that considers not only the items stored, but also their relationship to each other. Knowing that relationship in advance allows designing efficient algorithms for manipulating the data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-02" number={2} question="What are the goals of a data structure?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
It must be rich enough in structure to reflect the actual relationships of data in the real world, while staying simple enough for efficient processing.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-03" number={3} question="What does abstract d

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
ata type mean?" difficulty="medium" topic="ADTs">
A data type is a collection of values plus a set of operations on those values. An abstract data type (ADT) is the mathematical concept defining a data type's logical properties, independent of implementation. An ADT has two parts: a **value definition** (e.g. the ADT `RATIONAL` consists of two integers, the second non-zero) and an **operation definition** (creation, addition, multiplication, equality test).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-04" number={4} question="What is the difference between a stack and an array?" difficulty="medium" topic="Stacks & Arrays">
**Stack:** an ordered collection whose size changes dynamically as items are pushed/popped, may hold mixed types conceptually, and is typically implemented as a structure conta

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
ining an array plus an integer tracking the current top.

**Array:** an ordered, static collection — size is fixed at declaration, and it holds a single data type. An array can back a stack's implementation (declared large enough for the stack's maximum size).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-05" number={5} question="What do you mean by recursive definition?" difficulty="easy" topic="Recursion">
A definition that defines an object in terms of simpler cases of itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-06" number={6} question="What is sequential search?" difficulty="easy" topic="Searching">
Comparing each item in the array (or linked list) against the target until a match is found.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-07" number={7} question="What actions are performed when a funct

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
ion is called?" difficulty="medium" topic="Functions">
Arguments are passed, local variables are allocated and initialized, and control transfers to the function.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-08" number={8} question="What actions are performed when a function returns?" difficulty="medium" topic="Functions">
The return address is retrieved, the function's data area is freed, and execution branches back to the return address.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-09" number={9} question="What is a linked list?" difficulty="easy" topic="Linked Lists">
A linear collection of nodes where order is given by pointers rather than physical placement. Each node stores the element's data plus the address of the next node.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-10" number={10} question="What ar

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
e the advantages of a linked list over an array?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
Arrays are expensive to insert into or delete from, and can't be resized without reallocating a new memory block. In a linked list, each element carries a pointer to the next, so successive elements need not occupy adjacent memory — insertion and deletion just rewire pointers.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-11" number={11} question="Why can't you apply binary search to a sorted linked list?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
Binary search needs random access to the middle element by index; a linked list has no way to index directly to the middle, so binary search isn't applicable — a key drawback of using linked lists.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-12" number={12} question="Wh

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
at do you mean by a free pool?" difficulty="medium" topic="Memory Management">
A list of unused memory cells, maintained with its own pointer, from which new nodes are allocated.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-13" number={13} question="What do you mean by garbage collection?" difficulty="medium" topic="Memory Management">
An operating system technique that periodically reclaims deleted space back onto the free storage list — triggered when free space runs low or the CPU is idle. The alternative, immediately reinserting freed space into the list, is more time-consuming.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-14" number={14} question="What do you mean by overflow and underflow?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
**Overflow:** inserting into a data structure when no space is avail

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
able (the free storage list is empty).

**Underflow:** attempting to delete from a data structure that is already empty.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-15" number={15} question="What are the disadvantages of an array implementation of a linked list?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
The number of nodes needed can't be predicted at compile time, and the declared nodes remain allocated for the program's entire execution.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-16" number={16} question="What is a queue?" difficulty="easy" topic="Queues">
An ordered collection where items are inserted at the rear and removed from the front — FIFO (first in, first out) — with no inherent limit on the number of elements.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-17" number={17} question="What is a prio

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
rity queue?" difficulty="medium" topic="Queues">
A data structure where the intrinsic ordering of elements determines removal order rather than insertion order.

**Ascending priority queue:** smallest item is removed first. **Descending priority queue:** largest item is removed first.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-18" number={18} question="What are the disadvantages of sequential storage?" difficulty="medium" topic="Arrays">
A fixed amount of storage stays allocated even when the structure holds fewer elements, and once that fixed allocation is exhausted, overflow occurs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-19" number={19} question="What are the disadvantages of representing a stack or queue with a linked list?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
Each node's info + next-po

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
inter fields occupy more storage than the equivalent array element, and extra time is spent managing the available-node list.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-20" number={20} question="What is a dangling pointer, and how do you avoid it?" difficulty="hard" topic="Memory Management">
After `free(p)`, a subsequent reference to `*p` is illegal — the storage is freed but `p`'s address value is unchanged, so it may still be dereferenced (with no way to detect the illegality). This is a dangling pointer. Avoid it by setting `p` to `NULL` immediately after `free(p)`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-21" number={21} question="What are the disadvantages of a linear list?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
You can't reach nodes preceding a given node `p`, and to traverse the list a

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
gain, an external pointer to its head must be preserved.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-22" number={22} question="What is a circular list?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
A linear list where the last node's next field, instead of holding a null pointer, points back to the first node. Advantage: from any point in the list, every other point is reachable.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-23" number={23} question="What are the disadvantages of a circular list?" difficulty="medium" topic="Linked Lists">
You can't traverse it backward, and given only a pointer to a node, you can't delete that node (you'd need the predecessor).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ds-24" number={24} question="What is a doubly linked list?" difficulty="easy" topic="Linked Lists">
A collection

title: Data Structures
description: Data structure interview questions for data engineers — arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, and the trade-offs behind efficient pipeline code.
content:
of nodes where each node has three parts: an info field holding the data, a left pointer to the previous node, and a right pointer to the next node.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Implement a queue using two stacks."** A classic coding prompt that tests whether you understand the amortized cost of shuffling elements between the two structures.
- **"You need O(1) lookups but also need to preserve insertion order — what do you reach for?"** Tests whether the candidate knows the trade-offs behind ordered hash structures (e.g. Python's `dict`).
- **"A pipeline job keeps running out of memory while reversing a huge linked list in place — how would you debug it?"** Probes understanding of pointer manipulation, free pools, and garbage collection under memory pressure.
- **"Walk through what happens in memory when a recursive function calls itself too many times."** Tests understanding of stack frames and why deep recursion causes a stack

## Language Fundamentals in Practice

Why Python shows up everywhere in data platforms, and how its concurrency model plays out in real pipelines.

<QuestionList topic="Language Fundamentals in Practice" storagePrefix="py-prac-fundamentals">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-01"
  number={15}
  question="Why is Python widely used in data engineering?"
  difficulty="easy"
  topic="Fundamentals"
>
Python is popular because it's productive, readable, and has a strong ecosystem for data processing, orchestration, and integration.

- **Glue code** — connecting APIs, databases, and file systems with minimal boilerplate
- **Rich libraries** — pandas, PyArrow, SQLAlchemy, boto3 cover most pipeline needs out of the box
- **Orchestration native** — Airflow, Dagster, and Prefect are all Python-first
- **I

title: Python Practice | Core Concepts
description: Practice Python interview questions for data engineering with real-world scenarios. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
nterop layer** — even when heavy compute runs on Spark or a warehouse engine, Python is the language stitching the pipeline together

It's rarely the fastest language for raw compute, but it's usually the fastest to build and maintain a correct pipeline in.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-02"
  number={16}
  question="How do iterators and generators help with large data processing?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="Fundamentals"
>
Iterators and generators let you stream data instead of loading everything into memory — processing row-by-row or batch-by-batch keeps memory flat regardless of input size.

```python
def batched(iterable, size):
    """Yield successive chunks without materializing the full list."""
    batch = []
    for item in iterable:
        batch.append(item)

title: Python Practice | Core Concepts
description: Practice Python interview questions for data engineering with real-world scenarios. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
if len(batch) == size:
            yield batch
            batch = []
    if batch:
        yield batch

def process_events(event_stream):
    for chunk in batched(event_stream, size=500):
        write_to_sink(chunk)  # one bounded batch at a time
```

This pattern — pull, don't load — is what lets a single job process files far larger than available RAM.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-03"
  number={17}
  question="What is the difference between threads, multiprocessing, and async IO in Python?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="Concurrency"
>
| Approach | Good for | Limited by |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| `threading` | I/O-bound work (network, disk) | GIL — no CPU parallelism |
| `multiprocessing` | CPU-bound work (parsing, transforms) | Process spawn/IPC over

title: Python Practice | Core Concepts
description: Practice Python interview questions for data engineering with real-world scenarios. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
head |
| `asyncio` | Many concurrent I/O waits (API calls) | Single-threaded event loop |

**Rule of thumb:** if the job spends most of its time *waiting* (network, disk), reach for threads or `asyncio`. If it spends its time *computing*, reach for `multiprocessing` or push the work to a native/distributed engine.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-04"
  number={18}
  question="What is the GIL and why does it matter for a data pipeline you're building?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="Concurrency"
>
The **Global Interpreter Lock** allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time in CPython, even on a multi-core machine.

In practice, most data engineering jobs are I/O-bound (waiting on S3, a database, or an API), so the GIL rarely matters — threads still help because th

title: Python Practice | Core Concepts
description: Practice Python interview questions for data engineering with real-world scenarios. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
e GIL is released during I/O. It only becomes a bottleneck when a job is CPU-bound (row-by-row transforms in pure Python), at which point `multiprocessing` or vectorized libraries (pandas/NumPy, which release the GIL in their C internals) are the fix.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

## Working with Data at Scale

Practical patterns for reading, writing, and moving data reliably in production pipelines.

<QuestionList topic="Working with Data at Scale" storagePrefix="py-prac-data">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-05"
  number={19}
  question="How do you read and write Parquet efficiently in Python?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="File Formats"
>
`pyarrow` is the standard choice — it supports columnar reads, predicate pushdown, and explicit schema control.

```python
import pyarrow.parquet as pq

title: Python Practice | Core Concepts
description: Practice Python interview questions for data engineering with real-world scenarios. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# Read only the columns you need, filtered at the file level
table = pq.read_table(
    "s3://bucket/events/",
    columns=["user_id", "event_type", "ts"],
    filters=[("ts", ">=", "2024-01-01")],
)
```

Guidelines: select only needed columns, keep row groups/files at a reasonable size (avoid the "many tiny files" problem), and hand large datasets off to a distributed engine rather than processing them purely locally.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-06"
  number={20}
  question="How do you process a large CSV file without running out of memory?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="File Formats"
>
Stream the file in chunks instead of loading it whole:

```python
import pandas as pd

total = 0
for chunk in pd.read_csv("huge_file.csv", chunksize=100_000):
    total += chunk["amount"]

                    if attempt == max_attempts:

                    delay = base_delay * (2 ** (attempt - 1))
                    time.sleep(delay + random.uniform(0, delay * 0.1))  # jitter

```

Only retry **transient** errors (timeouts, rate limits) — never retry a schema mismatch or a validation failure. Cap total retry time, and make the operation idempotent so a retried write can't duplicate data.
</QuestionCard>

>
```python
import logging, json

logger = logging.getLogger("pipeline")

def log

Structured (JSON) logs make pipeline runs searchable and correlatable across systems. Always include a run/pipeline id, log errors with enough context to debug without re-running the job, and never log sensitive values (PII, credentials).

## Project Structure & Testing

Organizing and validating pipeline code so it's maintainable beyond the first script.

<QuestionList topic="Project Structure & Testing" storagePrefix="py-prac-structure">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-09"
  number={23}
  question="How do you structure a Python project for data pipelines?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="Project Structure"
>
```
my_pipeline/
├── src/
│   ├── extract.py
│   ├── transform.py
│   ├── load.py
│   └── config.py        # env-driven settings, no hardcoded secrets
├── jobs/
│   └── run_daily.py      # thin CLI entrypoint
├── tests/
│   └── test_transform.py
├── pyproject.toml
```

Keep reusable library code separate from job entrypoints, inject external services (DB clients, S3 clients) rather than constructing them deep in the call s

title: Python Practice | Core Concepts
description: Practice Python interview questions for data engineering with real-world scenarios. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
tack, and standardize logging/error handling in one place so every job behaves consistently.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-10"
  number={24}
  question="How do you manage dependencies and reproducible environments?"
  difficulty="easy"
  topic="Project Structure"
>
Pin dependencies with a lockfile (`poetry.lock`, `uv.lock`, or `requirements.txt` with hashes), and isolate environments per project with a virtualenv or container.

Reproducibility also means pinning the Python version itself and building deterministic images — CI should build and test against the exact same environment definition that runs in production, not a looser "close enough" one.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-11"

    input_df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1], "revenue_cents": [1050]})
    result = normalize_revenue(input_df)
    assert result["revenue"].iloc[0] == 10.50

@given(revenue_cents=st.integers(min_value=0))
def test_revenue_is_never_negative(revenue_cents):
    df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1], "revenue_cents": [revenue_cents]})
    result = normalize_revenue(df)
    assert (result["revenue"] >= 0).all()
```

Combine small **golden-case** fixtures (known input → known output) with **property-based tests** (`hypothesis`) for invariants that must hold across all inputs, like non-negative amounts or no duplicate keys. Keep integration tests against real connectors separate and isolated, since they're slower and l

<QuestionCard
  id="py-prac-12"
  number={26}
  question="How do you profile and optimize slow Python code?"
  difficulty="hard"
  topic="Performance"
>
Profile before optimizing — guessing at bottlenecks wastes time:

```python
import cProfile
cProfile.run("process_data(df)", sort="cumulative")
```

Common fixes, in order of how often they apply:
1. **Vectorize** — replace `iterrows()`/Python loops with pandas/NumPy operations
2. **Reduce copies** — avoid chained `.loc`/`.copy()` calls that duplicate large frames
3. **Offload CPU work** — `multiprocessing`, or push to a native/distributed engine
4. **Cache repeated I/O** — `functools.lru_cache`, or batch reads instead of per-row calls

`py-spy` is worth knowing for production debugging — it's a sampling profil

content:
## Python Fundamentals

Core language concepts that come up in every data engineering interview.

<QuestionList topic="Python Fundamentals" storagePrefix="py-fundamentals">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-fund-01"
  number={1}
  question="What are the key features of Python?"
  difficulty="easy"
  topic="Fundamentals"
>
- **Simple syntax** — readable, English-like code reduces cognitive overhead
- **Interpreted** — runs directly without a compilation step
- **Dynamically typed** — no explicit type declarations required
- **High-level** — memory management is handled by the runtime
- **Cross-platform** — runs on Windows, Linux, macOS without changes
- **Rich ecosystem** — pandas, NumPy, requests, and thousands of other libraries
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-fund-02"
  number={2}
  questi

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
on="What is the difference between a mutable and an immutable object? Give examples of each."
  difficulty="easy"
  topic="Fundamentals"
>
**Mutable** objects can be changed after creation:
- `list` — `my_list.append(4)` modifies in place
- `dict` — `my_dict["key"] = "value"` modifies in place
- `set` — `my_set.add(5)` modifies in place

**Immutable** objects cannot be changed:
- `int`, `float`, `bool` — reassignment creates a new object
- `str` — `"hello" + "!"` returns a new string
- `tuple` — elements cannot be added, removed, or changed
- `frozenset` — immutable version of set

**Why it matters for data engineering:** immutable objects are safe as dict keys or set members; sharing mutable objects across threads causes subtle bugs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-fund-03"
  numb

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
er={3}
  question="Explain the difference between == and is in Python."
  difficulty="easy"
  topic="Fundamentals"
>
| Operator | Checks | Example |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `==` | **Value equality** — same value? | `[1,2] == [1,2]` → `True` |
| `is` | **Identity** — same object in memory? | `[1,2] is [1,2]` → `False` |

```python
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a           # same reference
c = [1, 2, 3]   # new object, same value

print(a == c)   # True  — same value
print(a is b)   # True  — same object
print(a is c)   # False — different objects
```

> Use `is` only for singleton checks (`if x is None`). Use `==` for all value comparisons.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-fund-04"
  number={4}
  question="How does memory management and garbage collection work in Python?"
  difficulty=

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
"medium"
  topic="Fundamentals"
>
Python uses two mechanisms:

**1. Reference Counting**
Every object tracks how many references point to it. When the count hits 0 the memory is freed immediately.

```python
import sys
a = [1, 2, 3]
print(sys.getrefcount(a))  # 2 (a + getrefcount arg itself)
del a  # refcount → 0, memory freed
```

**2. Cyclic Garbage Collector**
Reference counting cannot handle circular references (A → B → A). Python's `gc` module runs periodically to detect and clean these cycles.

```python
import gc
gc.collect()  # manually trigger GC
```

**Memory pools:** CPython uses `pymalloc`, a private heap for small objects (≤ 512 bytes), reducing `malloc` overhead.

**For data engineering:** use `del df` + `gc.collect()` after processing large DataFrames, or prefer chunked proc

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
essing to avoid OOM.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-fund-05"
  number={5}
  question="What is the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) and how does it affect data engineering workloads?"
  difficulty="hard"
  topic="Concurrency"
>
The **GIL** is a mutex in CPython that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, even on multi-core machines.

**Impact by workload type:**

| Workload | GIL Impact | Solution |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| I/O-bound (network, disk) | Minimal — GIL released during I/O | `threading` or `asyncio` |
| CPU-bound (parsing, transforms) | Full — only one core used | `multiprocessing` |
| NumPy / pandas ops | Minimal — C extensions release GIL | Can use threads |

```python
from multiprocessing import Pool

def process_partition(chunk):
    r

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
eturn chunk.apply(some_transform)

with Pool(processes=4) as pool:
    results = pool.map(process_partition, chunks)
```

> Python 3.13 introduced an experimental **no-GIL** build (PEP 703). This will change concurrent Python data pipeline design.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

## Functions & Generators

Essential Python patterns for building efficient, composable data pipelines.

<QuestionList topic="Functions & Generators" storagePrefix="py-functions">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-fn-01"
  number={6}
  question="What are generators and why are they useful in data engineering?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="Generators"
>
A **generator** uses `yield` instead of `return` — it produces values lazily, one at a time, without loading everything into memory.

```python

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# ❌ Loads all 1M rows into memory at once
def read_all_rows(file):
    rows = []
    with open(file) as f:
        for line in f:
            rows.append(line)
    return rows

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# ✅ Yields one row at a time — memory stays flat
def stream_rows(file):
    with open(file) as f:
        for line in f:
            yield line.strip()

for row in stream_rows("huge_file.csv"):
    process(row)
```

**Why critical for data engineering:**
- Process multi-GB files without OOM errors
- Build composable, lazy pipeline stages
- Power `chunksize` patterns in pandas

**Generator expression** (inline):
```python
total = sum(len(line) for line in open("file.txt"))
```
</QuestionCard>

## Object-Oriented Programming

OOP patterns used in data engineering frameworks and libraries.

<QuestionList topic="OOP in Python" storagePrefix="py-oop">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-oop-01"
  number={9}
  question="What is the difference between class methods, static methods, and instance methods?"
  difficulty="medium"
  topic="OOP"
>
| Method type | Decorator | First arg | Can access |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Instance | *(none)* | `self` | instance + class |
| Class | `@classmethod` | `cls` | class only |
| Static | `@staticmethod` | *(none)* | neither |

```python
class DatabaseConnector:
    _default_port = 5432

    def __init__(self, host, port=None):
        self.host = host
        self.port = port or self._default_port

    def connect(self):

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# Instance method — uses self
        return f"Connecting to {self.host}:{self.port}"

    @classmethod
    def from_url(cls, url: str):
        # Class method — factory pattern
        host, port = url.split(":")
        return cls(host, int(port))

    @staticmethod
    def validate_port(port: int) -> bool:
        # Static — pure utility, no state needed
        return 1 <= port <= 65535

db = DatabaseConnector.from_url("localhost:5432")
print(DatabaseConnector.validate_port(5432))  # True
```
</QuestionCard>

content:
## Concurrency & Performance

Threading, async, and profiling patterns for high-throughput pipelines.

<QuestionList topic="Concurrency & Performance" storagePrefix="py-concurrency">

<QuestionCard
  id="py-conc-01"
  number={11}
  question="What is the difference between threading, multiprocessing, and asyncio?"
  difficulty="hard"
  topic="Concurrency"
>
| Approach | Best for | GIL affected? | Overhead |
|----------|----------|--------------|---------|
| `threading` | I/O-bound (HTTP, DB queries) | Yes | Low |
| `multiprocessing` | CPU-bound (data transforms) | No | High (process spawn) |
| `asyncio` | Many concurrent I/O ops | Yes | Very low |

```python

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# asyncio — ideal for many concurrent API calls
import asyncio, httpx

async def fetch(url, client):
    r = await client.get(url)
    return r.json()

async def fetch_all(urls):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        return await asyncio.gather(*[fetch(u, client) for u in urls])

results = asyncio.run(fetch_all(["https://api.example.com/data"] * 100))
```

**Rule of thumb for data engineering:**
- Reading 100 S3 files in parallel → `asyncio` or `ThreadPoolExecutor`
- Transforming 10 million rows → `multiprocessing.Pool`
- Single background task → `threading.Thread`
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard
  id="py-conc-02"
  number={12}
  question="How do you profile and optimize a slow Python data pipeline?"
  difficulty="hard"
  topic="Performance"
>
**Step 1 — Profile first, optim

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ize second:**
```python
import cProfile
cProfile.run('process_data(df)', sort='cumulative')
```

**Step 2 — Common bottlenecks and fixes:**

| Bottleneck | Symptom | Fix |
|------------|---------|-----|
| Python loops over rows | Slow `iterrows()` | Vectorize with pandas / NumPy |
| Repeated I/O | High disk/net read time | Batch reads, cache with `functools.lru_cache` |
| Single-threaded CPU | One core at 100% | `multiprocessing.Pool` |
| Memory pressure | OOM / swap thrashing | Generator / chunked processing |

```python

description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.

```

**Useful profiling tools:**
- `cProfile` / `snakeviz` — function-level timings
- `memory_profiler` — line-by-line memory usage
- `py-spy` — sampling profiler, zero overhead on production

```python
try:
    result = load_from_db(query)
except ConnectionError as e:
    logger.error("DB connection failed: %s", e)
    raise  # re-raise after logging
except ValueError as e:
    logger.warning("Bad data, skipping: %s", e)
    result = default_value
finally:
    cleanup()  # always runs
```

**Data pipeline best practices:**
- Catch **specific** exceptions — never bare `except:`
- Log with context (row id, file name, batch id)
- Distinguish **re

```python

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# transform.py
import pandas as pd

def normalize_revenue(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Convert revenue from cents to dollars, drop nulls."""
    df = df.dropna(subset=["revenue_cents"])
    df["revenue"] = df["revenue_cents"] / 100
    return df[["id", "revenue"]]
```

```python

title: Python Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master Python fundamentals for data engineering. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
# test_transform.py
import pandas as pd
import pytest
from transform import normalize_revenue

def test_converts_cents_to_dollars():
    input_df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1], "revenue_cents": [1050]})
    result = normalize_revenue(input_df)
    assert result["revenue"].iloc[0] == 10.50

def test_drops_null_revenue():
    input_df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1, 2], "revenue_cents": [500, None]})
    result = normalize_revenue(input_df)
    assert len(result) == 1

def test_output_columns():
    input_df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1], "revenue_cents": [100]})
    result = normalize_revenue(input_df)
    assert list(result.columns) == ["id", "revenue"]
```

Run with: `pytest test_transform.py -v`

**Key principle:** test the data contract (shape, types, values), not implementation details.

## SQL: Fundamentals & Interview Questions

SQL is the language every data engineer queries, transforms, and models data with. This section covers the concepts and query challenges interviewers actually ask about.

- **Practice**: Hands-on query-writing challenges with worked solutions — the format interviewers use to test whether you can actually write SQL

For a from-scratch walkthrough of SQL, see the [SQL Roadmap](/roadmaps/sql/) — this section focuses on what interviewers ask, not how to learn SQL from zero.

content:
## Ranking & Salary Queries

The classic "Nth highest value" family of problems, and the different ways to answer them.

<QuestionList topic="Ranking & Salary Queries" storagePrefix="sql-prac-rank">

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-rank-01" number={100} question="How would you find the second-highest salary in an Employee table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Ranking">
Given an `Employee` table (`ID`, `Salary`), there are several ways to do this.

**Option 1 — subquery with `NOT IN`:** find the max, exclude it, find the new max.
```sql
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
WHERE Salary NOT IN (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);
```

**Option 2 — not-equal operator**, efficient when excluding a single top value:
```sql
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
WHERE

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
Salary <> (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);
```

**Option 3 — `LIMIT`/`OFFSET`** (best for row ordering); `DISTINCT` avoids ties breaking the logic:
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT Salary AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
ORDER BY Salary DESC
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;
```
*(SQL Server: `OFFSET 1 ROWS FETCH NEXT 1 ROWS ONLY`.)*
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-rank-02" number={101} question="How would you find the maximum salary by department, including departments with no employees?" difficulty="medium" topic="Ranking">
Given `Employee(ID, Salary, DeptID)` and `Department(ID, DeptName)` — **this is a trick question**: ask the interviewer whether departments with no employees should still appear. If yes, use `LEFT OUTER JOIN` (`NULL` salary for empty departments); if no, `INNER JOIN` is enou

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
gh.

```sql
SELECT d.DeptName, MAX(e.Salary) AS MaxSalary
FROM Department d
LEFT OUTER JOIN Employee e ON d.ID = e.DeptID
GROUP BY d.DeptName;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-rank-03" number={102} question="How would you find the maximum salary per department (without a Department table)?" difficulty="easy" topic="Ranking">
```sql
SELECT DeptID, MAX(Salary) AS MaxSalary
FROM Employee
GROUP BY DeptID;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-rank-04" number={103} question="How would you find the Nth highest salary, for an arbitrary N?" difficulty="hard" topic="Ranking">
**Correlated subquery** — counts how many distinct salaries are higher; when that count is `N-1`, you've found rank N:
```sql
SELECT * FROM Employee emp1
WHERE (N - 1) = (
    SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT emp

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
2.Salary) FROM Employee emp2 WHERE emp2.Salary > emp1.Salary
);
```

**`ROW_NUMBER()` window function** (recommended — works across Oracle, SQL Server, PostgreSQL, MySQL):
```sql
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS rnum FROM Employee
) AS Subquery WHERE rnum = N;
```
*(Use `DENSE_RANK()` instead if duplicate salaries should share a rank.)*

**`LIMIT`/`OFFSET`** (MySQL/PostgreSQL/SQLite — shortest if supported):
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT Salary FROM Employee ORDER BY Salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET (N - 1);
```
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts

content:
## Filtering, Sets & Duplicates

Set comparisons between tables, and finding rows that don't belong.

<QuestionList topic="Filtering, Sets & Duplicates" storagePrefix="sql-prac-filter">

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-filter-01" number={104} question="How would you select every other (alternate odd/even) record from a table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Filtering">
Assign a row number with `ROW_NUMBER()`, then filter with modulo:

```sql
-- Even-numbered records
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY (SELECT NULL)) AS row_num
    FROM Employee
) AS Subquery
WHERE MOD(row_num, 2) = 0;

-- Odd-numbered: WHERE MOD(row_num, 2) = 1
```
*(No `MOD()` support? Use `WHERE row_num % 2 = 0`.)*
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-filter-02" number={105} question="How would you fi

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
nd records in Table A that don't exist in Table B, without using NOT IN?" difficulty="medium" topic="Filtering">
**Set operators** — standard SQL/SQL Server/PostgreSQL/MySQL use `EXCEPT`, Oracle uses `MINUS`:
```sql
SELECT * FROM Table_A EXCEPT SELECT * FROM Table_B;   -- Oracle: MINUS instead of EXCEPT
```

**`LEFT JOIN`** (most universal — works across all SQL databases):
```sql
SELECT Table_A.* FROM Table_A
LEFT JOIN Table_B ON Table_A.ID = Table_B.ID
WHERE Table_B.ID IS NULL;
```
*(`NOT IN` is avoided here because it silently returns no rows if the subquery contains even one `NULL`.)*
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-filter-03" number={106} question="How would you find employees who share both the same name and email?" difficulty="easy" topic="Filtering">
Group by both colum

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ns together — `HAVING COUNT(*) > 1` isolates rows where both fields match exactly:
```sql
SELECT name, email, COUNT(*) AS duplicate_count
FROM Employee
GROUP BY name, email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-filter-04" number={107} question="How would you find duplicate email addresses in an Employee table?" difficulty="easy" topic="Filtering">
```sql
SELECT EMAIL, COUNT(EMAIL) AS occurrence_count
FROM Employee
GROUP BY EMAIL
HAVING COUNT(EMAIL) > 1;
```
Note: with `GROUP BY`, only grouped or aggregated columns may appear in `SELECT`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-filter-05" number={108} question="How would you select 10 employees with odd employee IDs?" difficulty="easy" topic="Filtering">
```sql
-- Standard SQL / Oracle 12c+ / PostgreSQL /

description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
MySQL
SELECT ID FROM Employee WHERE ID % 2 = 1 FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY;

-- SQL Server
SELECT TOP 10 ID FROM Employee WHERE ID % 2 = 1;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-filter-06" number={109} question="How would you do a case-insensitive name search?" difficulty="easy" topic="Filtering">
```sql
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE UPPER(emp_name) LIKE '%RICH%';
-- Equivalent: WHERE LOWER(emp_name) LIKE '%rich%'
```

content:
## Dates & Result Shaping

Date arithmetic and controlling how many rows come back.

<QuestionList topic="Dates & Result Shaping" storagePrefix="sql-prac-dates">

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-dates-01" number={110} question="How would you select employees born between two dates?" difficulty="easy" topic="Dates">
`BETWEEN` is inclusive of both endpoints:
```sql
SELECT EmpName FROM Employees WHERE birth_date BETWEEN '1990-01-01' AND '2000-12-31';
-- Equivalent: WHERE birth_date >= '1990-01-01' AND birth_date <= '2000-12-31'
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-dates-02" number={111} question="How would you extract the quarter from a date?" difficulty="easy" topic="Dates">
```sql
-- Standard SQL (most portable)
SELECT EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM DATE '2016-03-31') AS quarter;

-- Oracle
SEL

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ECT TO_CHAR(TO_DATE('03/31/2016', 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'Q') AS quarter FROM DUAL;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-dates-03" number={112} question="How would you calculate the number of days between two dates?" difficulty="easy" topic="Dates">
```sql
-- MySQL
SELECT DATEDIFF('2016-12-31', '2015-01-01') AS DaysDifference;

-- SQL Server
SELECT DATEDIFF(day, '2015-01-01', '2016-12-31') AS DaysDifference;

-- PostgreSQL / Oracle
SELECT '2016-12-31'::date - '2015-01-01'::date AS DaysDifference;
```
Note: in MySQL the larger date goes first for a positive result; in SQL Server/PostgreSQL, second.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-dates-04" number={113} question="How do you get the current date and time in SQL?" difficulty="easy" topic="Dates">
```sql
SELECT CURRENT_DATE;

title: SQL Practice | Core Concepts
description: Hands-on SQL query-writing challenges with worked solutions for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
-- ANSI standard; MySQL: CURDATE()
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;  -- ANSI standard; MySQL: NOW()
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-prac-dates-05" number={114} question="How do you limit the number of rows returned by a query, and how does pagination build on that?" difficulty="easy" topic="Result Shaping">
```sql
-- Standard SQL
SELECT * FROM table_name FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY;
-- MySQL
SELECT * FROM table_name LIMIT 10;

-- Pagination: skip 3, take next 6
SELECT * FROM table_name OFFSET 3 ROWS FETCH NEXT 6 ROWS ONLY;   -- MySQL: LIMIT 3, 6
```
Always pair `LIMIT`/`FETCH` with `ORDER BY` — without it, row order (and your results) are unpredictable.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## Quick Reference

A refresher on the syntax the questions below assume. For a from-scratch walkthrough of SQL, see the [SQL Roadmap](/roadmaps/sql/) — this page focuses on what interviewers ask, not how to learn SQL from zero.

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
### Reading data
`SELECT` retrieves columns, `WHERE` filters rows, `ORDER BY` sorts, `GROUP BY`/`HAVING` aggregate and filter groups, `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` paginate:

```sql
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS employee_count, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date >= '2020-01-01'
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
### Joins
`INNER JOIN` returns only matching rows; `LEFT`/`RIGHT JOIN` keep all rows from one side (NULL where unmatched); `FULL OUTER JOIN` keeps all rows from both; `CROSS JOIN` produces a Cartesian product; a self join joins a table to itself via an alias.

```sql
SELECT e.employee_name, d.department_name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id;
```

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
### Modifying data & schema
`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` (DML) manipulate rows; `CREATE TABLE`, `ALTER TABLE`, `DROP TABLE` (DDL) manage schema. Always scope `UPDATE`/`DELETE` with `WHERE` — omitting it applies to every row.

```sql
CREATE TABLE employees (
    employee_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    salary DECIMAL(10, 2),
    department_id INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (department_id) REFERENCES departments(id)
);

UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.10 WHERE department_id = 2;
```

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
### Constraints & data types
`PRIMARY KEY` (unique row identifier), `FOREIGN KEY` (referential integrity), `UNIQUE`, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK` (validates values), `DEFAULT`. Common types: `INT`, `VARCHAR(n)`, `DECIMAL(precision, scale)`, `DATE`, `DATETIME`, `BOOLEAN`, `TEXT`.

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
### Common mistakes

| Mistake | Problem | Fix |
|---------|---------|-----|
| `WHERE col = NULL` | Always returns no rows | Use `WHERE col IS NULL` |
| Missing `WHERE` on `UPDATE`/`DELETE` | Modifies/deletes every row | Always scope with `WHERE` |
| `SELECT *` on large tables | Slow, excess data transfer | Select only needed columns |
| Using a column alias in `WHERE` | Alias isn't resolved yet at that stage | Only usable in `ORDER BY`/`SELECT` |

---

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts

content:
## SQL Fundamentals

The core vocabulary and commands every SQL interview starts with.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Fundamentals" storagePrefix="sql-fundamentals">

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-01" number={1} question="What is SQL and what is it used for?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard programming language for managing relational databases. It enables data professionals to create, read, update, and delete data (CRUD), manage schemas, and perform data analysis.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-02" number={2} question="Describe the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- **SQL** — relational, structured schema, ACID compliance, tables with rows/columns
- **NoSQL** — non-relational,

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
flexible schema, eventual consistency, document/key-value/graph models
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-03" number={3} question="What are the different types of SQL commands?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- **DDL** — Data Definition (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`)
- **DML** — Data Manipulation (`SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`)
- **DCL** — Data Control (`GRANT`, `REVOKE`)
- **TCL** — Transaction Control (`COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-04" number={4} question="Explain the purpose of the SELECT statement." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Retrieves data from tables.

```sql
SELECT columns FROM table WHERE conditions ORDER BY columns;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-05" number={5} question="What is

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
the difference between WHERE and HAVING clauses?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- **`WHERE`** — filters rows *before* `GROUP BY`
- **`HAVING`** — filters groups *after* aggregation
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-06" number={6} question="Define what a JOIN is in SQL and list its types." difficulty="easy" topic="Joins">
JOINs combine rows based on related columns.

- **`INNER JOIN`** — matching rows only
- **`LEFT`/`RIGHT JOIN`** — all rows from one side + matching from the other
- **`FULL OUTER JOIN`** — all rows from both tables
- **`CROSS JOIN`** — Cartesian product
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-07" number={7} question="What is a primary key in a database?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
The unique identifier for each row. Cannot be `NULL`, mu

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
st be unique — enforces entity integrity.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-08" number={8} question="Explain what a foreign key is and how it is used." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
A column referencing another table's primary key. Enforces referential integrity and creates relationships between tables.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-09" number={9} question="How can you prevent SQL injections?" difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
Parameterized queries (prepared statements), input validation, least-privilege database accounts, and stored procedures used cautiously — never build queries by concatenating raw user input.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-10" number={10} question="What is normalization? Explain with examples." difficulty="medium" top

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ic="Data Modeling">
Organizing data to reduce redundancy:

- **1NF** — atomic values, no repeating groups
- **2NF** — every non-key column fully depends on the whole primary key
- **3NF** — no transitive dependencies (non-key columns depend only on the key)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-11" number={11} question="Describe the concept of denormalization and when you would use it." difficulty="medium" topic="Data Modeling">
Deliberately adding redundant data to improve read performance, at the cost of write complexity and storage. Common in data warehouses and read-heavy applications where join cost outweighs storage cost.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-12" number={12} question="What are indexes and how can they improve query performance?" difficulty="medium" topic=

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
"Performance">
B-tree structures that enable fast lookups. They speed up `WHERE`/`JOIN`/`ORDER BY`, but slow down `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` since the index has to be maintained on every write.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-13" number={13} question="Explain the purpose of the GROUP BY clause." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Groups rows into summary rows so aggregate functions (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, ...) can be applied per group instead of across the whole table.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-14" number={14} question="What is a subquery, and when would you use one?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
A query nested inside `SELECT`, `WHERE`, or `FROM`. Used for complex filtering that depends on the result of another query — for example, filtering

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
rows above an aggregate computed elsewhere.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-15" number={15} question="Describe the functions of the ORDER BY clause." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Sorts the result set in ascending (`ASC`, default) or descending (`DESC`) order, by one or more columns.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-16" number={16} question="What are aggregate functions in SQL?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Functions that compute a single value from multiple rows: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-17" number={17} question="Explain the differences between INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL JOIN." difficulty="easy" topic="Joins">
- **`INNER`** — only matching rows
- **`LEFT`** — all left rows

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
+ matching right rows (`NULL` if no match)
- **`RIGHT`** — all right rows + matching left rows (`NULL` if no match)
- **`FULL`** — all rows from both tables
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-18" number={18} question="How do you insert a new row into a database table?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
```sql
INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2);
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-19" number={19} question="Explain how to update records in a database table." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
```sql
UPDATE table SET col1 = val1 WHERE condition;
```
Always scope with `WHERE` — omitting it updates every row.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-fund-20" number={20} question="What is a SQL View and what are its advantages?" difficulty="medium" t

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
opic="Fundamentals">
A virtual table based on a stored `SELECT` query. Advantages: simplifies repeated complex queries, provides a security layer (expose only certain columns/rows), and centralizes reusable logic.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL Data Types and Operators

Types, comparison operators, and NULL handling that show up in almost every query.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Data Types and Operators" storagePrefix="sql-types">

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-01" number={21} question="List the different data types available in SQL." difficulty="easy" topic="Data Types">
- **Numeric** — `INT`, `DECIMAL`
- **String** — `CHAR`, `VARCHAR`, `TEXT`
- **Date/Time** — `DATE`, `TIMESTAMP`
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-02" number={22} question="What are the differences between CHAR, VARCHAR, and TEXT data types?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Types">
- **`CHAR`** — fixed length, padded with spaces
- **`VARCHAR`** — variable length, up to a defined limit
- **`TEXT`** — large variable-length text, typically no practical l

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
imit
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-03" number={23} question="How do you use the BETWEEN operator in SQL?" difficulty="easy" topic="Operators">
```sql
SELECT * FROM table WHERE column BETWEEN 10 AND 20;
```
Inclusive of both endpoints.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-04" number={24} question="Describe the use of the IN operator." difficulty="easy" topic="Operators">
```sql
SELECT * FROM table WHERE column IN (val1, val2, val3);
```
Shorthand for chaining several `OR` conditions on the same column.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-05" number={25} question="Explain the use of wildcard characters in SQL." difficulty="easy" topic="Operators">
`%` matches any sequence of characters, `_` matches exactly one character. Used with `LIKE`.
</QuestionCard>

<Que

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
stionCard id="sql-types-06" number={26} question="What is the purpose of the LIKE operator?" difficulty="easy" topic="Operators">
Pattern matching on strings:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE 'J%';
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-07" number={27} question="How do you handle NULL values in SQL?" difficulty="easy" topic="NULL Handling">
Use `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` for comparisons — `= NULL` never matches. Functions like `COALESCE` and `ISNULL` substitute a default when a value is `NULL`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-08" number={28} question="What does the COALESCE function do?" difficulty="easy" topic="NULL Handling">
Returns the first non-`NULL` value in its argument list:

```sql
SELECT COALESCE(phone, email, 'N/A');
```
</QuestionCard>

<Questi

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
onCard id="sql-types-09" number={29} question="What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Operators">
- **`UNION`** — combines result sets, removes duplicates (extra sort/dedup cost)
- **`UNION ALL`** — combines result sets, keeps duplicates (faster, no dedup pass)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-types-10" number={30} question="Describe the use of arithmetic operators in SQL queries." difficulty="easy" topic="Operators">
Standard operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) can be used directly in `SELECT` and `WHERE` clauses to compute or filter on derived numeric values.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL Advanced Queries

Patterns that go beyond basic filtering — conditional logic, self joins, CTEs, and window functions.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Advanced Queries" storagePrefix="sql-advanced">

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-01" number={31} question="Explain how to use the CASE statement in SQL." difficulty="medium" topic="Advanced Queries">
```sql
SELECT CASE WHEN age < 18 THEN 'Minor' ELSE 'Adult' END FROM users;
```
Works like an inline if/else — usable in `SELECT`, `WHERE`, and `ORDER BY`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-02" number={32} question="How would you perform a self JOIN?" difficulty="medium" topic="Joins">
```sql
SELECT e1.name, e2.name
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2 ON e1.manager_id = e2.id;
```
Joins a table to itself via two aliases — common for hierarchi

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
cal relationships like employee/manager.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-03" number={33} question="What is a cross JOIN and when would you use it?" difficulty="medium" topic="Joins">
Produces the Cartesian product of two tables (every row of A paired with every row of B). Used for generating test data or combining independent dimensions (e.g. all product/color combinations).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-04" number={34} question="How to implement pagination in SQL queries?" difficulty="medium" topic="Advanced Queries">
```sql
-- MySQL / PostgreSQL
SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY id LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;

-- SQL Server
SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY id OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;
```
Always pair with `ORDER BY` — without it, page boundaries aren't guaranteed to

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
be stable.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-05" number={35} question="Explain the concept of Common Table Expressions (CTEs) and recursive CTEs." difficulty="medium" topic="Advanced Queries">
A CTE (`WITH` clause) creates a temporary named result set scoped to one query, improving readability over nested subqueries. A **recursive CTE** references itself to walk hierarchical data (org charts, category trees) level by level.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-06" number={36} question="What are window functions and how are they used?" difficulty="medium" topic="Window Functions">
Functions that compute a value across a "window" of related rows without collapsing them into one row, unlike `GROUP BY`.

```sql
SELECT name, salary,
       RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORD

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank
FROM employees;
```
Common ones: `ROW_NUMBER()`, `RANK()`, `LAG()`, `LEAD()`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-07" number={37} question="How can you concatenate column values in SQL?" difficulty="easy" topic="Advanced Queries">
- **Standard SQL / PostgreSQL** — `||`
- **MySQL** — `CONCAT(a, b)`
- **SQL Server** — `+`
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-08" number={38} question="What is the PIVOT operation and how would you apply it?" difficulty="medium" topic="Advanced Queries">
Transforms distinct row values into columns. SQL Server has a native `PIVOT` keyword; most other databases emulate it with conditional aggregation:

```sql
SELECT
    department,
    SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q1' THEN revenue END) AS q1,
    SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
2' THEN revenue END) AS q2
FROM sales
GROUP BY department;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-09" number={39} question="Explain combining GROUP BY with ORDER BY." difficulty="easy" topic="Advanced Queries">
`ORDER BY` runs after `GROUP BY` (and after `HAVING`), so it sorts the already-aggregated result rows — you can order by an aggregate expression like `COUNT(*)`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-adv-10" number={40} question="How would you find duplicate records in a table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Advanced Queries">
```sql
SELECT col, COUNT(*)
FROM table
GROUP BY col
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
```
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## Database Design & Architecture

Modeling, schema strategy, and the objects that support them.

<QuestionList topic="Database Design & Architecture" storagePrefix="sql-design">

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-01" number={41} question="What is the Entity-Relationship Model?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Modeling">
A conceptual model using entities (tables), attributes (columns), and relationships between entities to design a database before implementation.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-02" number={42} question="Explain the different types of database schema." difficulty="medium" topic="Data Modeling">
- **Conceptual** — high-level business view, no implementation detail
- **Logical** — structure (entities, relationships, attributes) without DBMS-specific detail
- **Physical

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
** — the actual implementation: tables, indexes, storage
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-03" number={43} question="What are Stored Procedures and how are they beneficial?" difficulty="medium" topic="Database Objects">
Precompiled, named blocks of SQL stored in the database. Benefits: reduced network round trips, centralized/reusable logic, tighter access control, and execution plan caching.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-04" number={44} question="What is a trigger in SQL and when should it be used?" difficulty="medium" topic="Database Objects">
Code that runs automatically in response to a DML event (`INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`) on a table. Used for audit trails, enforcing complex validation, or keeping denormalized data in sync.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard i

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
d="sql-design-05" number={45} question="Describe the concept of ACID in databases." difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
**Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — the guarantees that make a transaction reliable: it fully completes or fully rolls back, leaves the database in a valid state, is isolated from concurrent transactions, and survives a crash once committed.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-06" number={46} question="What is database sharding?" difficulty="hard" topic="Scalability">
Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple database instances (shards), typically by a key like customer ID or region, to scale writes and storage beyond a single machine.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-07" number={47} question="How do database indexes work

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
and what types are there?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Most commonly a **B-tree** structure that keeps sorted keys for fast range/equality lookups. Other types: **Hash** (equality-only, O(1) lookup), **Bitmap** (low-cardinality columns), **GiST**/**GIN** (PostgreSQL — full-text search, geometric data).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-08" number={48} question="Describe the process of data warehousing." difficulty="medium" topic="Data Warehousing">
ETL processes move data from operational systems into a warehouse, modeled dimensionally (star/snowflake schema), and queried through OLAP cubes or SQL for reporting and analysis.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-09" number={49} question="Explain the difference between OLTP and OLAP systems." difficulty="me

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
dium" topic="Data Warehousing">
- **OLTP** — transactional, frequent small writes, operational (e.g. order processing)
- **OLAP** — analytical, read-heavy, large scans over historical data (e.g. reporting)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-design-10" number={50} question="What are materialized views and how do they differ from standard views?" difficulty="medium" topic="Database Objects">
A materialized view stores the query result physically and must be refreshed to stay current, trading staleness for read speed. A standard (virtual) view re-runs its underlying query on every access.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL Optimization and Performance

Reading execution plans and making queries faster.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Optimization and Performance" storagePrefix="sql-optimization">

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-01" number={51} question="How do you identify and optimize slow-running queries?" difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
Use `EXPLAIN`/`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` to find the bottleneck, add targeted indexes, avoid `SELECT *`, and restructure `JOIN`s so the database can use indexes and smaller intermediate result sets.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-02" number={52} question="What is query execution plan in SQL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
The database's chosen strategy for running a query — which scans, joins, and indexes it will use, and their estimated (or actual) co

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
st.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-03" number={53} question="Explain how to use EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE." difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
- **`EXPLAIN`** — shows the planned execution strategy without running the query
- **`EXPLAIN ANALYZE`** — actually executes the query and shows real timings and row counts alongside the plan
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-04" number={54} question="How can indexing affect performance both positively and negatively?" difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
- **Positive** — much faster `SELECT`/`JOIN`/`ORDER BY` on indexed columns
- **Negative** — slower `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`, since every index has to be updated too, plus extra storage
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-05" number={55} question="Describe

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
how to measure the performance of SQL queries." difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
Execution time, rows examined vs. rows returned, whether indexes are actually used (from `EXPLAIN` output), and I/O cost.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-06" number={56} question="How would you rewrite a query to improve its performance?" difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
Prefer `JOIN`s over correlated subqueries where possible, select only needed columns instead of `SELECT *`, filter early, and add indexes that match the query's `WHERE`/`JOIN` columns.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-opt-07" number={57} question="What are partitioned tables and how can they optimize performance?" difficulty="medium" topic="Optimization">
Tables physically split by a key (date, range, or hash

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
). Queries that filter on the partition key only scan relevant partitions ("partition pruning"), dramatically reducing I/O on large tables.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>
