title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL Security

Protecting access to data.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Security" storagePrefix="sql-security">

<QuestionCard id="sql-sec-01" number={58} question="How do you implement database encryption in SQL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
**TDE** (Transparent Data Encryption) for data at rest, column-level encryption for specific sensitive fields, and **SSL/TLS** for data in transit.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-sec-02" number={59} question="What are roles and how do they manage database access?" difficulty="easy" topic="Security">
Named collections of privileges. Users are assigned roles and inherit the underlying permissions — simplifies access control at scale versus granting permissions per-user.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-sec-03" number={60} question=

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
"Explain the concept of row-level security." difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
Restricts which rows a user can see or modify based on their identity or context (e.g. a sales rep only sees their own region's rows), enforced by the database rather than application code.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL Functions and Expressions

Scalar/table-valued functions and stored procedures.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Functions and Expressions" storagePrefix="sql-functions">

<QuestionCard id="sql-func-01" number={61} question="Describe scalar-valued and table-valued functions." difficulty="medium" topic="Functions">
- **Scalar** — returns a single value, usable inline in `SELECT`/`WHERE`
- **Table-valued** — returns a table, usable in a `FROM` clause like a regular table
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-func-02" number={62} question="How would you define a stored procedure with input and output parameters?" difficulty="medium" topic="Functions">
```sql
CREATE PROCEDURE proc(@input INT, @output INT OUTPUT) AS
BEGIN
    SET @output = @input * 2;
END;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
="sql-func-03" number={63} question="What is the difference between a function and a stored procedure?" difficulty="medium" topic="Functions">
- **Function** — returns a value, callable inline within a query, generally can't perform DML on the tables it reads
- **Procedure** — supports `OUTPUT` parameters, can perform full DML, but can't be called inline inside a `SELECT`
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-func-04" number={64} question="How do you use the CAST and CONVERT functions?" difficulty="easy" topic="Functions">
```sql
SELECT CAST(col AS INT);       -- ANSI standard
SELECT CONVERT(INT, col);      -- SQL Server style
```
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## Transaction Control and Locking

Concurrency guarantees and how the database enforces them.

<QuestionList topic="Transaction Control and Locking" storagePrefix="sql-transactions">

<QuestionCard id="sql-txn-01" number={65} question="What is a database transaction?" difficulty="easy" topic="Transactions">
An atomic unit of work that either commits fully or rolls back fully, preserving the database's ACID guarantees.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-txn-02" number={66} question="Explain the concept of locking and its types in SQL databases." difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
Locks prevent conflicting concurrent access to the same data. Granularity ranges from **row-level** (fine-grained, more concurrency) to **page-level** to **table-level** (coarse, less concurrency but ch

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
eaper to manage).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-txn-03" number={67} question="What are the properties of transactions?" difficulty="easy" topic="Transactions">
**ACID**: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-txn-04" number={68} question="How do you manage transaction isolation levels?" difficulty="hard" topic="Transactions">
```sql
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;
```
Levels range from `READ UNCOMMITTED` (weakest, allows dirty reads) through `READ COMMITTED`, `REPEATABLE READ`, to `SERIALIZABLE` (strongest, fully isolated but least concurrent).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-txn-05" number={69} question="What does it mean to commit or roll back a transaction?" difficulty="easy" topic="Transactions">
- **`COMMIT

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
`** — permanently saves all changes made in the transaction
- **`ROLLBACK`** — undoes all changes made in the transaction, as if it never happened
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL and Modern Data Ecosystems

Where SQL sits alongside big data and cloud platforms.

<QuestionList topic="SQL and Modern Data Ecosystems" storagePrefix="sql-ecosystems">

<QuestionCard id="sql-eco-01" number={70} question="How can SQL be integrated with big data technologies?" difficulty="medium" topic="Ecosystems">
SQL-on-Hadoop engines (Hive, Spark SQL), external/federated tables, and JDBC/ODBC connectors all let SQL query data sitting in big-data storage.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-eco-02" number={71} question="Discuss the interoperability of SQL with cloud-based data stores." difficulty="easy" topic="Ecosystems">
Cloud databases and warehouses (RDS, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) all expose standard-ish SQL interfaces, so most SQL knowledge transfers directly.
</Quest

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-eco-03" number={72} question="What is Data Lake and how can SQL interact with it?" difficulty="medium" topic="Ecosystems">
A centralized repository of raw data in its native format. Query engines like Presto/Trino and Athena let you run SQL directly against files in the lake without a separate load step.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-eco-04" number={73} question="Explain the interaction between SQL and NoSQL within the same application." difficulty="medium" topic="Ecosystems">
Polyglot persistence — using SQL databases for transactional/relational data and NoSQL stores for scale or flexible schema, within the same system.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-eco-05" number={74} question="How does SQL work within a microservices architecture?" diff

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
iculty="medium" topic="Ecosystems">
Each service typically owns its own database (often SQL), and shares data with other services through APIs or events rather than direct cross-service queries — avoiding a shared-schema coupling problem.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## SQL Best Practices and Standards

Writing SQL that's maintainable and portable.

<QuestionList topic="SQL Best Practices and Standards" storagePrefix="sql-best-practices">

<QuestionCard id="sql-best-01" number={75} question="What are some common SQL coding practices you follow?" difficulty="easy" topic="Best Practices">
Consistent naming conventions, indexing based on actual query patterns, avoiding `SELECT *`, and always using parameterized queries.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-best-02" number={76} question="How can you ensure the portability of SQL scripts across systems?" difficulty="medium" topic="Best Practices">
Stick to ANSI SQL where possible, avoid vendor-specific functions, and abstract vendor differences behind views where you can't avoid them.
</QuestionCard>

<Qu

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
estionCard id="sql-best-03" number={77} question="What methods do you use for version controlling SQL scripts?" difficulty="easy" topic="Best Practices">
Git for the SQL files themselves, plus migration tools (Flyway, Liquibase, or a dbt-style migrations folder) to apply schema changes as ordered, repeatable steps — "database as code."
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-best-04" number={78} question="What are the benefits of using stored procedures instead of embedded SQL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Best Practices">
Fewer network round trips, cached execution plans, centralized logic that's easier to secure and audit than SQL scattered across application code.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-best-05" number={79} question="How do you document SQL code effectively?" difficulty="e

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
asy" topic="Best Practices">
Inline comments explaining *why* (not what), ER diagrams for schema relationships, and a data dictionary describing tables/columns for anyone querying the data later.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## Analytical SQL Questions

Ranking, running totals, and other analysis-oriented patterns.

<QuestionList topic="Analytical SQL Questions" storagePrefix="sql-analytical">

<QuestionCard id="sql-analytic-01" number={80} question="How would you find the Nth highest salary from a table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Analytics">
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-analytic-02" number={81} question="How do you count occurrences of a specific value in a column?" difficulty="easy" topic="Analytics">
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table WHERE column = 'value';
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-analytic-03" number={82} question="How can you calculate running totals in SQL?" difficulty="medium" topic="A

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
nalytics">
```sql
SELECT order_date, amount,
       SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total
FROM orders;
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-analytic-04" number={83} question="Explain how to reverse the contents of a column without a reverse function." difficulty="hard" topic="Analytics">
When the database has no built-in `REVERSE()`, fall back to application-level processing, or a stored procedure/recursive query that rebuilds the string character by character in reverse order.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-analytic-05" number={84} question="What approach do you use for creating a calendar table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Analytics">
Generate a contiguous date range (via a recursive CTE or a numbers table joined to a base date) and add derived flag column

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
s — weekday, is_weekend, fiscal_quarter — for fast time-based joins and analysis.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-analytic-06" number={99} question="Explain how to use SQL for predictive analysis and machine learning." difficulty="hard" topic="Analytics">
Window functions and statistical aggregates can compute trend features directly in SQL. Warehouse-native ML (BigQuery ML, Redshift ML) and extensions like MADlib go further, letting you train and score models without leaving SQL.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## Data Manipulation and ETL

Moving and cleaning data with SQL.

<QuestionList topic="Data Manipulation and ETL" storagePrefix="sql-etl">

<QuestionCard id="sql-etl-01" number={85} question="What is the process of Extract, Transform, Load (ETL)?" difficulty="easy" topic="ETL">
Extract data from source systems, transform it (clean, join, aggregate), and load it into a target system — the foundation of most data warehousing pipelines.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-etl-02" number={86} question="How do you import/export data from/to a flat file using SQL?" difficulty="medium" topic="ETL">
- **PostgreSQL** — `COPY`
- **MySQL** — `LOAD DATA INFILE`
- **SQL Server** — `BULK INSERT`
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-etl-03" number={87} question="Explain the steps for a basic ETL pro

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
cess." difficulty="medium" topic="ETL">
Extract → clean → validate → load to a staging table → load to the warehouse → (re)build indexes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-etl-04" number={88} question="How do you cleanse and format data using SQL queries?" difficulty="medium" topic="ETL">
`TRIM` to remove stray whitespace, `UPPER`/`LOWER` to normalize case, `REPLACE` for known bad patterns, and regex functions (`REGEXP_REPLACE`, etc.) for more complex cleanup.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-etl-05" number={89} question="What tools do you use for automating data import/export?" difficulty="easy" topic="ETL">
Apache Airflow or dbt for orchestration/transformation, Talend or Informatica for GUI-driven ETL, or plain cron-scheduled scripts for simpler pipelines.
</QuestionCard>

</

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts

content:
## Domain-Specific SQL Scenarios

Modeling patterns for relationships, hierarchies, and time.

<QuestionList topic="Domain-Specific SQL Scenarios" storagePrefix="sql-domain">

<QuestionCard id="sql-domain-01" number={90} question="How would you model a many-to-many relationship in SQL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Modeling">
A junction (bridge) table holding foreign keys to both related tables — e.g. `student_id` and `course_id` in an `enrollments` table.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-domain-02" number={91} question="Describe how to manage hierarchical data in SQL." difficulty="hard" topic="Data Modeling">
- **Adjacency list** — each row stores its `parent_id`; simple, but multi-level queries need recursion
- **Nested sets** — stores left/right bounds; fast subtree reads, expe

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
nsive writes
- **Recursive CTEs** — walk an adjacency list to arbitrary depth in a single query
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-domain-03" number={92} question="How would you approach writing SQL queries for reporting?" difficulty="medium" topic="Reporting">
Query against a dimensional model (star/snowflake) rather than raw OLTP tables, aggregate with `GROUP BY`, and optimize for read patterns since reporting queries are run far more often than the underlying data changes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-domain-04" number={93} question="Explain how to handle temporal data and time zones in SQL." difficulty="hard" topic="Data Modeling">
Store timestamps in UTC using a timezone-aware type (`TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE`), and convert to a local zone only at display time with `AT TI

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
ME ZONE`. Storing local time directly makes cross-region comparisons and DST transitions error-prone.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-domain-05" number={94} question="How do you use SQL in financial applications for risk analysis?" difficulty="hard" topic="Reporting">
Time-series queries over historical prices/positions, window functions for rolling metrics (moving average, volatility), and aggregate risk measures computed directly in the warehouse close to the data.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
## Troubleshooting and Debugging

Diagnosing failures, corruption, and concurrency issues.

<QuestionList topic="Troubleshooting and Debugging" storagePrefix="sql-debugging">

<QuestionCard id="sql-debug-01" number={95} question="What steps do you take to troubleshoot a failed SQL query?" difficulty="medium" topic="Troubleshooting">
Check syntax first, verify table/column names exist, validate data types match, then isolate the problem by simplifying the query (remove joins/conditions one at a time) until it runs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-debug-02" number={96} question="How can you recover data from a corrupt SQL database?" difficulty="hard" topic="Troubleshooting">
Restore from the most recent clean backup, replay transaction logs to minimize data loss, and for engine-level

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
corruption run integrity checks like `DBCC CHECKDB` (SQL Server) to identify and, where possible, repair the damage.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-debug-03" number={97} question="What methods do you employ to ensure data integrity?" difficulty="medium" topic="Troubleshooting">
Primary/foreign key constraints, `CHECK` constraints for value rules, unique indexes to prevent duplicates, and triggers for cross-row validation that constraints alone can't express.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sql-debug-04" number={98} question="How do you decipher and resolve deadlocks in SQL?" difficulty="hard" topic="Troubleshooting">
Analyze the deadlock graph the database produces to see which transactions were holding/waiting on which locks, identify the resource contention pattern, then fix i

title: SQL Theory | Core Concepts
description: Master SQL fundamentals, advanced queries, database design, and optimization for data engineering interviews. Interactive Q&A with progress tracking.
content:
t by keeping transactions short, accessing tables in a consistent order, and reducing lock scope.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
## Data Engineer vs Data Scientist: Roles, Tools, and Collaboration

**Data engineering and data science are closely related but distinct disciplines — data engineers build the infrastructure that data scientists rely on, while data scientists extract insights and build models from that data.**

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
## Key Concepts

The two roles split along a clear line: data engineers focus on **infrastructure and pipelines**, data scientists focus on **analysis and modeling**. That difference shows up in their tools (SQL, Python, Spark, and Airflow for engineers; Python, R, Jupyter, and ML libraries for scientists), their goals (reliable data flow vs. insights and predictions), and their output (clean, structured datasets vs. models, reports, and dashboards). Neither role is self-sufficient: data engineers provide the clean, reliable datasets data scientists need, and data scientists feed data requirements back to engineers, forming a feedback loop that turns raw data into business value. Understanding where one role's responsibility ends and the other's begins — and how they hand off work — is a c

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
ommon thread in interviews for either side.

---

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Data Engineer vs Data Scientist" storagePrefix="data-engineer-vs-data-scientist">

<QuestionCard id="devds-01" number={1} question="What's the fundamental difference between data engineering and data science?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
They're closely related but distinct disciplines. Data engineers build the infrastructure that data scientists rely on — pipelines, storage, and processing systems. Data scientists extract insights and build models from that data — statistical analysis, machine learning, and experimentation. One makes data available and trustworthy; the other turns it into decisions.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-02" number={2} question="How do the tools used by data engineers and data scientists typically

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
differ?" difficulty="easy" topic="Tools">
Data engineers typically work with SQL, Python, Spark, and Airflow — tools built for moving and transforming data at scale. Data scientists typically work with Python, R, Jupyter notebooks, and ML libraries — tools built for exploration, statistical modeling, and experimentation. There's real overlap (both use SQL and Python heavily), but the surrounding toolchain diverges.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-03" number={3} question="How do the goals and outputs of each role differ?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
A data engineer's goal is reliable data flow, and their output is clean, structured datasets. A data scientist's goal is insights and predictions, and their output is models, reports, and dashboards. In short: engineers opt

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
imize for the data being correct and available; scientists optimize for the data being useful for a specific decision.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-04" number={4} question="What is a data engineer's primary responsibility?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Engineer">
A data engineer's primary responsibility is to build and maintain data pipelines — systems that move data from source systems through transformation layers to make it available for analysis, reporting, and machine learning.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-05" number={5} question="What are the four core responsibility areas for a data engineer?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Engineer">
- **Data Infrastructure** — building reliable, scalable systems for data storage and processing
- **Pipeline Architecture**

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
— designing ETL/ELT workflows that handle data movement
- **Data Quality** — ensuring accuracy, consistency, and reliability of data
- **Performance Optimization** — making queries and transformations efficient at scale
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-06" number={6} question="What does a data scientist focus on day to day?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Scientist">
A data scientist focuses on extracting insights, building statistical models, and applying machine learning to solve business problems. They rely on the clean, reliable data that data engineers provide, rather than building and maintaining that infrastructure themselves.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-07" number={7} question="What is exploratory data analysis, and why does a data scientist do it?" difficulty="

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
easy" topic="Data Scientist">
Exploratory analysis is the process of finding patterns and trends in data — understanding distributions, relationships, and anomalies before committing to a modeling approach. It's typically the first step in a data science workflow, since it shapes which hypotheses and models are worth pursuing.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-08" number={8} question="What does “modeling” mean in a data scientist's workflow?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Scientist">
Modeling means building predictive and prescriptive models — using statistical or machine learning techniques to predict outcomes or recommend actions based on data. It's the step that turns patterns found during exploratory analysis into something that generalizes to new data.
</QuestionCard>

<Questio

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
nCard id="devds-09" number={9} question="What role does experimentation play for a data scientist?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Scientist">
Experimentation means designing and running controlled experiments, such as A/B tests, to validate whether a change actually causes an outcome rather than just correlating with it. It's how data scientists move from "this pattern looks interesting" to "this change reliably improves the metric."
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-10" number={10} question="Why does communication matter as a data scientist skill?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Scientist">
Communication means translating findings into actionable business recommendations. A model or analysis only creates value once a stakeholder understands it well enough to act on it, so explaini

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
ng results clearly is as much a part of the job as producing them.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-11" number={11} question="How do data engineers and data scientists collaborate in practice?" difficulty="medium" topic="Collaboration">
Data engineers provide the clean, reliable datasets that data scientists need. Data scientists, in turn, communicate their data requirements back to engineers — what grain, freshness, or new sources they need. Together they form a feedback loop that turns raw data into business value, rather than working as two disconnected teams.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="devds-12" number={12} question="Why do data scientists depend on data engineers rather than building their own pipelines?" difficulty="medium" topic="Collaboration">
Because data engineer

title: Data Engineer vs Data Scientist
description: Interview questions on how data engineering and data science differ in focus, tools, goals, and output, and how the two roles collaborate day to day.
content:
s build the infrastructure — reliable storage, processing, and pipeline systems — that data scientists rely on. Without that foundation, data scientists would spend most of their time on data plumbing instead of analysis and modeling, which is a poor use of their specialized skills and duplicates work across a team.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"A data scientist says the data is wrong — how do you figure out whose problem it is?"** Walk through checking whether the issue traces back to the pipeline (engineer's responsibility — infrastructure, transformation logic, freshness) or to how the data is being interpreted or modeled (scientist's responsibility).
- **"How would you set up a working relationship between a data engineering team and a data science team?"** Talk through the feedback loop: engineers exposing clean, documented, conformed datasets, and scientists communicating requirements (grain, freshness, new fields) back, rather than each side working in isolation.
- **"You're a data scientist and you need a new data source ingested — what do you do?"** Describe handing off clear requiremen

ts (schema, freshness, volume, intended use) to a data engineer rather than building an ad hoc pipeline yourself.

import QuestionCard from '../../../components/mdx/QuestionCard.astro';

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
## Data Governance: Ownership, Access & Compliance

**Data governance is the set of processes, policies, and roles that make data discoverable, trusted, secure, and compliant as a platform scales beyond a single team.**

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
## Key Concepts

Governance starts with clear ownership: a **data owner** is accountable for a dataset's business meaning, a **data steward** maintains its quality and metadata day to day, and a **data custodian** operates the underlying platform. On top of ownership sits access control — **RBAC** grants permissions to roles, while **ABAC** evaluates dynamic attributes (department, region, sensitivity) for more flexible but harder-to-manage policies — plus finer-grained row- and column-level security for multi-tenant and PII-heavy datasets. A **data catalog** ties it together by making datasets discoverable, with lineage, owners, and freshness in one place, while a **business glossary** aligns what terms actually mean. Governance also has to answer compliance questions: how PII/PHI gets ma

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
sked or tokenized, how access is audited, how GDPR deletion requests get honored across raw and derived layers, and how retention policies get enforced with expiry and lifecycle rules.

---

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Data Governance" storagePrefix="data-governance">

<QuestionCard id="dg-01" number={1} question="What is data governance and why does it matter?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Data governance is the set of processes, policies, and responsibilities that ensure data is discoverable, trusted, secure, and used correctly. It matters because data platforms scale to many teams; without governance, definitions diverge, access becomes risky, and compliance issues appear. Good governance enables faster, safer self-service analytics.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-02" number={2} question="What is the difference between governance and security?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Security focuses on preventing unauthorized access and

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
ensuring confidentiality/integrity (access controls, encryption). Governance includes security but also covers ownership, definitions, quality, lifecycle, and change management. Governance answers "who owns this data and what does it mean," not just "who can access it."
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-03" number={3} question="What are common roles in data governance (owner/steward/custodian)?" difficulty="easy" topic="Roles">
Common roles are:

- **data owner** — accountable for the dataset and its business definition
- **data steward** — maintains quality, metadata, and processes
- **data custodian** — operates the technical platform (storage, access, backups)

Clear roles prevent "nobody owns it" incidents.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-04" number={4} question="What is a d

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
ata catalog and what should it contain?" difficulty="easy" topic="Cataloging">
A data catalog is an inventory of datasets with searchable metadata. It typically contains dataset descriptions, owners, lineage, classifications, freshness/SLA, schema, and links to dashboards. A good catalog reduces time to discovery and improves trust and reuse.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-05" number={5} question="What is business glossary vs. technical metadata?" difficulty="medium" topic="Metadata">
A business glossary defines business terms (for example "active user," "net revenue") and their approved definitions. Technical metadata describes datasets and fields (schemas, types, partitions, lineage). Both are needed: glossary aligns meaning, technical metadata enables implementation and discovery

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-06" number={6} question="RBAC vs. ABAC: what is the difference?" difficulty="medium" topic="Access Control">
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) grants permissions to roles (analyst, engineer) and assigns users to roles. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) uses attributes (department, region, sensitivity) to evaluate policies dynamically. ABAC is more flexible but can be harder to manage without good tooling.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-07" number={7} question="What is row-level and column-level security?" difficulty="medium" topic="Access Control">
Row-level security restricts which rows a user can see (for example only their region). Column-level security restricts which columns are visible (for example hide salary or PII). These controls

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
are important for multi-tenant analytics and compliance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-08" number={8} question="How do you handle PII/PHI data in analytics platforms?" difficulty="hard" topic="Compliance">
You classify sensitive fields, minimize exposure, and enforce least privilege. Common controls include encryption, masking/tokenization, column-level access, and auditing. You also ensure downstream marts only include necessary fields and implement secure sharing patterns.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-09" number={9} question="What is data masking and tokenization?" difficulty="medium" topic="Compliance">
Masking hides sensitive values (for example showing only the last 4 digits) while keeping the dataset usable. Tokenization replaces sensitive values with tokens that can

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
be detokenized only with access to a secure mapping. Tokenization is useful when analytics needs stable identifiers without exposing raw PII.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-10" number={10} question="How do you implement auditability for data access?" difficulty="medium" topic="Auditing">
You log access events (who queried what, when, from where) and retain logs per policy. You also track permission changes and administrative actions. Audit logs should be queryable for investigations and compliance reporting.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-11" number={11} question="How do you handle GDPR “right to be forgotten” in a lakehouse/warehouse?" difficulty="hard" topic="Compliance">
You need a process to delete or anonymize personal data across raw, derived, and serving layers. This

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
can involve targeted deletes (where supported), reprocessing from raw logs without the user, or storing personal data separately to allow deletion without rewriting large datasets. You also document what is feasible given retention and legal constraints.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dg-12" number={12} question="What are data retention policies and how do you enforce them?" difficulty="medium" topic="Retention">
Retention policies define how long data is kept and when it must be deleted or archived. Enforcement can be implemented with partition expiry jobs, lifecycle rules on object storage, snapshot expiration, and access reviews. Retention must align with legal requirements and business needs.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: Data Governance
description: Data governance interview questions covering ownership, RBAC/ABAC, PII handling, masking, auditability, GDPR deletion, and retention policy enforcement.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"A new analyst asks to see raw customer PII 'just to debug a report' — how do you respond?"** Tests least privilege, masking/tokenization, and role boundaries.
- **"Legal asks you to delete a specific user's data across the entire warehouse for a GDPR request — walk through your approach."** Tests the raw/derived layer deletion strategy and audit trail.
- **"Two teams have different definitions of 'active user' in their dashboards — how do you fix that at the platform level?"** Tests business glossary thinking and conformed definitions.
- **"Design the ownership model for a new data domain with three producing teams and ten consuming teams."** Tests owner/steward/custodian role design.

---

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
## Data Modeling: Schema Design for Analytics

**Data modeling is how you structure data so it can be queried reliably and efficiently — the difference between a warehouse that answers business questions cleanly and one that produces inconsistent metrics and slow, confusing joins.**

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
## Key Concepts

Everything in analytical data modeling starts with **grain** — the level of detail a table represents (one row per order line, one row per user per day). Get grain wrong and every downstream join or aggregation is suspect. From there, the **star schema** (a central fact table surrounded by dimension tables) is the default shape for analytics: facts hold measurable events and foreign keys, dimensions hold descriptive context. **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)** define how you handle attribute changes over time, and patterns like **conformed dimensions**, **bridge tables**, and **degenerate dimensions** solve for consistency, many-to-many relationships, and drill-through respectively.

---

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Data Modeling" storagePrefix="data-modeling">

<QuestionCard id="dm-01" number={1} question="What is data modeling in analytics and why does it matter?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Data modeling in analytics is the process of structuring data so it can be queried reliably and efficiently for business questions. A good model makes metrics consistent, reduces complexity for consumers, and improves performance by aligning storage with query patterns. A bad model leads to inconsistent KPIs, confusing joins, and expensive queries.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-02" number={2} question="What is grain and why is it the first question to answer?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Grain is the level of detail of a table — for exa

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
mple "one row per order line" or "one row per user per day." It determines which metrics are additive, how joins behave, and what can be uniquely identified. If grain is unclear, you get duplicates, incorrect aggregations, and ambiguous keys.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-03" number={3} question="What is the difference between fact and dimension tables?" difficulty="easy" topic="Star Schema">
Facts store measurable events (orders, payments, clicks) and typically contain foreign keys to dimensions. Dimensions store descriptive attributes (user, product, date) that provide context for slicing and filtering. Facts answer "what happened," dimensions answer "who/what/where/when."
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-04" number={4} question="What is a star schema and what are its benefi

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
ts?" difficulty="easy" topic="Star Schema">
A star schema has a central fact table connected to multiple dimension tables. Benefits include simpler queries, clear join paths, and good performance because joins are typically fact-to-dimension (many-to-one). It also encourages consistent definitions of metrics and attributes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-05" number={5} question="Star schema vs snowflake schema: what are the trade-offs?" difficulty="medium" topic="Star Schema">
A snowflake schema normalizes dimensions into multiple related tables. It can reduce redundancy and improve dimension maintainability, but it increases join complexity and can hurt performance. Star schemas are usually preferred for BI and ad-hoc analytics because they're easier for users and tools to work wit

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
h.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-06" number={6} question="What are the main types of fact tables?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fact Tables">
- **Transactional** — one row per business event (order, payment)
- **Periodic snapshot** — one row per entity per period (daily account balance)
- **Accumulating snapshot** — one row that gets updated as a process progresses (order lifecycle)

Choosing the right type depends on the questions you need to answer.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-07" number={7} question="What is a surrogate key and when should you use it?" difficulty="medium" topic="Keys">
A surrogate key is a synthetic identifier used instead of a natural key. It's useful when natural keys aren't stable, when you need SCD history, or when integrating multiple sources with d

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
ifferent key systems. Surrogate keys also allow consistent joins even if source keys change.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-08" number={8} question="What is a conformed dimension?" difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensions">
A conformed dimension is a dimension shared consistently across multiple fact tables or data marts — for example, the same "customer" dimension used for orders and support tickets. Conformed dimensions enable consistent reporting and cross-domain analysis.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-09" number={9} question="What is SCD (Slowly Changing Dimension)?" difficulty="medium" topic="SCD">
SCD describes how you handle changes in dimension attributes over time (for example, a user changing address).

- **Type 1** — overwrites history
- **Type 2** — tracks history w

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
ith multiple rows and effective dates
- **Type 3** — keeps limited history in additional columns

The choice depends on whether "as-of" reporting is required.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-10" number={10} question="How do you implement SCD Type 2?" difficulty="hard" topic="SCD">
SCD2 typically stores:

- a surrogate key
- the natural/business key
- `effective_start` and `effective_end` timestamps (or `end_date`)
- a `current_flag`

On change, you "close" the previous record and insert a new version. You must define how to detect changes and handle late-arriving updates.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-11" number={11} question="What is a factless fact table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fact Tables">
A factless fact table records events that don't have numeric measures — for ex

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
ample, "student attended class" or "user is eligible for campaign." The fact itself can be counted, and the table is useful for coverage, eligibility, and relationship tracking.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-12" number={12} question="What is a bridge table and when do you need it?" difficulty="hard" topic="Dimensions">
A bridge table resolves many-to-many relationships — for example, products belonging to multiple categories, or users having multiple roles. It can also store weights for allocation (for example, revenue split). Without a bridge table, joins can explode row counts and break aggregations.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-13" number={13} question="What are degenerate dimensions?" difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensions">
Degenerate dimensions are identifiers stored

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
directly in the fact table without a separate dimension table, such as `order_id` or `invoice_number`. They're useful for drill-through and filtering while avoiding unnecessary dimension tables.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-14" number={14} question="How do you prevent double counting in analytical models?" difficulty="hard" topic="Data Quality">
You prevent double counting by enforcing correct grain, using proper join keys (many-to-one), and avoiding many-to-many joins without a bridge table. You also define metric rules (sum vs. distinct count vs. last value) and test them with reconciliation queries.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dm-15" number={15} question="How do you validate a data model after changes?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Quality">
Typical validation include

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
s:

- row count and key uniqueness checks at each layer
- reconciliation of totals against source systems
- sampling and drill-through checks on known entities
- regression tests for key business metrics

This catches subtle issues like join duplication or missing late data.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a fact table for e-commerce orders."** Start by nailing down grain (order? order line?), then identify dimensions (customer, product, date, promotion) and decide how to handle returns or cancellations — usually a separate fact or a status dimension rather than mutating the original row.
- **"Our revenue metric doesn't match between two dashboards."** This is almost always a grain or join-fan-out problem. Walk through checking for many-to-many joins without a bridge table, duplicate rows from a non-conformed dimension, or inconsistent aggregation rules (sum vs. distinct count) between the two queries.
- **"A dimension's attributes change over time — how do you model it?"** Identify whether the business needs point-in-time history (SCD Type 2) or on

title: Data Modeling
description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.
content:
ly the current value (SCD Type 1), then talk through the surrogate key, effective-dating, and how late-arriving changes get handled.

---

description: Data modeling interview questions covering star vs snowflake schema, fact and dimension tables, SCD types, and analytics-ready design for data engineers.

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
## Data Quality: Accuracy, Completeness & Trust

**Data quality is the practice of ensuring data is correct, complete, consistent, and timely enough that downstream consumers can trust it — a mix of pipeline engineering (checks, contracts, monitoring) and product thinking (clear definitions and ownership).**

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
## Key Concepts

Data quality means data is fit for its intended use, which usually breaks down into six dimensions: completeness, validity, accuracy, consistency, timeliness/freshness, and uniqueness. Checks operate at two levels — **row-level checks** (`not_null`, regex, range constraints on individual records) and **aggregate checks** (row counts, distinct counts, distribution shifts across the whole dataset) — and both are needed because aggregate checks catch issues row-level rules miss, like a missing partition. Upstream, **data contracts** and schema-drift detection prevent producers from silently breaking consumers; downstream, **quarantine patterns** let bad rows get isolated with an error reason while the pipeline keeps running for valid data. A common failure mode is alert fatig

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
ue, so mature setups lean on dynamic thresholds and multi-signal alerting (freshness + volume + error rate) rather than single noisy checks.

---

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Data Quality" storagePrefix="data-quality">

<QuestionCard id="dq-01" number={1} question="What does data quality mean in data engineering?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Data quality means the data is fit for its intended use. In practice, it means data is correct, complete, consistent, timely, and well-defined so downstream consumers can trust metrics. Data quality is both a technical problem (pipelines) and a product problem (definitions and contracts).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-02" number={2} question="What are common dimensions of data quality?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Common dimensions include:

- completeness (missing rows/fields)
- validity (values in allowed ranges/domains)
- accuracy (matches real-

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
world or source truth)
- consistency (no contradictions across tables)
- timeliness/freshness (arrives within SLA)
- uniqueness (no duplicates where keys should be unique)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-03" number={3} question="What is data freshness and how do you measure it?" difficulty="easy" topic="Freshness">
Freshness is how recently data was updated relative to expected cadence. You can measure it by comparing the maximum event/ingestion timestamp in a table to current time, and alert when it exceeds an SLA threshold. Freshness should be defined per dataset because "daily" and "real-time" pipelines differ.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-04" number={4} question="What is the difference between data validation and data reconciliation?" difficulty="medium" topic="Validatio

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
n">
Validation checks whether a dataset satisfies rules (types, constraints, ranges). Reconciliation compares datasets to ensure they match expected totals or invariants (for example source row count vs. target row count, or `sum(amount)` in source vs. warehouse). Reconciliation is important when multiple systems can drift.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-05" number={5} question="What are row-level checks and aggregate checks?" difficulty="medium" topic="Checks">
Row-level checks validate each record (for example `not_null`, regex, range constraints). Aggregate checks validate the dataset as a whole (row counts, distinct counts, distribution checks, totals). Aggregate checks often catch issues that row-level rules miss, like missing partitions.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-0

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
6" number={6} question="How do you design quality checks for incremental pipelines?" difficulty="hard" topic="Pipelines">
Checks should be scoped to the incremental window and also protect the full table invariants. For example, validate uniqueness on the keys in the new batch, validate that the batch size is within expected bounds, and validate that total counts change consistently. You also need idempotency checks to detect duplicates on retries.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-07" number={7} question="How do you handle late arriving data from a quality perspective?" difficulty="hard" topic="Pipelines">
You define allowed lateness (for example 7 days), track completeness by event time, and implement backfills to "close" historical partitions. Quality checks should consider both ing

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
estion time and event time so you can detect gaps in past periods.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-08" number={8} question="How do you detect schema drift and breaking changes?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema">
You compare incoming schemas to an expected contract (column names, types, nullability) and alert on changes. Additive changes can be allowed with policy, but breaking changes (rename/type change) should fail fast. Many teams use CI checks and schema registries to enforce compatibility.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-09" number={9} question="What are data contracts?" difficulty="medium" topic="Contracts">
Data contracts are explicit agreements between producers and consumers about schema, semantics, SLAs, and ownership. Contracts reduce surprises by defining what can

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
change and what cannot, and they enable automated validation. A contract can live as code (YAML/JSON) and be enforced in CI/CD.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-10" number={10} question="How do you avoid noisy alerts (false positives) in data quality monitoring?" difficulty="medium" topic="Alerting">
Use dynamic thresholds, seasonality-aware baselines, and multi-signal alerts (freshness + volume + error rate). Also route alerts by severity and dataset criticality, and suppress alerts during known maintenance windows. Good alerting includes a runbook and clear ownership.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-11" number={11} question="What is anomaly detection for metrics and when is it useful?" difficulty="medium" topic="Monitoring">
Anomaly detection flags unusual changes in metrics

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
(spikes/drops) that may indicate pipeline issues or business events. It is useful for detecting silent failures where data still arrives but is wrong. It should be combined with context (releases, campaigns) to avoid alert fatigue.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-12" number={12} question="How do you quarantine bad data without blocking the entire pipeline?" difficulty="hard" topic="Pipelines">
A common pattern is to split data into "good" and "quarantine" outputs based on validation rules. You can store bad rows with error reasons for debugging while allowing the pipeline to continue for valid data. For critical datasets, you may still block publishing to marts while keeping raw ingestion running.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-13" number={13} question="How do you test data tr

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
ansformations?" difficulty="medium" topic="Testing">
You can test transformations by:

- unit-like tests on small fixtures (golden datasets)
- property tests (invariants like non-negative totals)
- reconciliation tests against known sources
- regression tests on key metrics

In dbt-style workflows, tests often run in the warehouse as SQL assertions.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-14" number={14} question="What is the minimum set of checks you would add to every table?" difficulty="easy" topic="Checks">
Minimum checks often include:

- freshness (max timestamp within SLA)
- row count / volume sanity check
- primary key uniqueness (if applicable)
- not_null checks on critical columns
- referential integrity for key joins (where applicable)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dq-15" num

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
ber={15} question="What are common data quality failure modes?" difficulty="medium" topic="Failure Modes">
Common failures include:

- missing partitions or partial loads
- duplicates due to retries/reprocessing
- schema drift and type coercion issues
- timezone and date boundary bugs
- silent truncation or rounding changes
- incorrect joins causing duplication
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: Data Quality
description: Data quality interview questions covering dimensions, freshness, validation, schema drift, data contracts, and how to design checks that catch silent failures.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"A dashboard's revenue number dropped 20% overnight — walk me through how you'd investigate."** Tests freshness/volume checks, anomaly detection, and reconciliation instincts.
- **"Design the minimum set of quality checks you'd add to a new fact table before it ships to production."** Tests knowledge of freshness, volume, primary key uniqueness, not-null, and referential integrity checks.
- **"A producing team wants to rename a column — how do you prevent that from breaking five downstream dashboards?"** Tests understanding of contracts, schema drift detection, and CI enforcement.
- **"Your quality checks are so noisy that the on-call engineer has started ignoring them — how do you fix that?"** Tests dynamic thresholds, multi-signal alerting, and clear ow

content:
## Data Warehousing: Architecture, Schemas & ETL

**A data warehouse is the architectural layer that turns raw, write-optimized operational data into a structure built for fast, reliable analytics — this page covers OLAP/OLTP fundamentals, star and snowflake schema design, ETL, dimensional modeling, and the platform-level trade-offs data engineers are expected to reason through in interviews.**

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
## Key Concepts

A data warehouse exists to answer one question well: how do you turn scattered, write-optimized operational data into a structure that's fast and reliable to query for analytics? The foundational split is **OLTP vs. OLAP** — OLTP systems (the applications generating your data) are normalized and optimized for many small, concurrent writes; OLAP systems are denormalized and optimized for large, read-heavy aggregations across history. Warehouses exist because running analytical queries directly against an OLTP system either degrades it or gives you an incomplete, historyless answer.

Getting data from source to warehouse is the job of **ETL/ELT** — extract from source systems, transform (clean, conform, apply business rules), and load into the warehouse, or in the ELT varian

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
t, load raw and transform inside the warehouse using its own compute. Once data lands, **dimensional modeling** — star and snowflake schemas, fact/dimension tables, slowly changing dimensions — is the standard way warehouses organize data for query performance and business usability; see the [Data Modeling](/level-2-core-concepts/data-modeling/) page for schema-design specifics, since this page stays at the platform and architecture layer around it.

The landscape has also evolved beyond a single "data warehouse" box: **data lakes** store raw, schema-on-read data cheaply at scale, and **lakehouses** try to combine lake economics with warehouse-style transactions and performance. Vendor-specific implementation details for Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Databricks live on their own pages

title: Data Warehousing

— this page stays at the concepts and trade-offs level that shows up across all of them.

### Warehouse Fundamentals & Architecture

<QuestionList topic="Warehouse Fundamentals & Architecture" storagePrefix="dwh-fund">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-01" number={1} question="What is Data Warehouse architecture?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
The data warehouse architecture is a three-tier architecture.
The following is the three-tier architecture:
- Bottom Tier
- Middle Tier
- Upper Tier

It is nothing but a repository of integrating data which is extracted from different data sources.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-02" number={2} question="What is a Real time Data Warehouse and how is it different from near to Real time Data Warehouse?" difficulty="hard" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
As the term suggests, a real-time data warehouse is a system,

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
which reflects all changes to its sources in real time. As simple as it sounds, this is still an area of active research in the field. In traditional DWH, the operational system(s) are kept separate from the DWH for a good reason.
The Operational systems are designed to accept inputs or changes to data regularly, hence have a good chance of being regularly queried. On the other hand, a DWH is supposed to do just the opposite - it is used to query data for reports only. No changes to data, through user actions is expected (or designed). The only inputs could come from the ETL feed at stipulated times.
The ETL would source its data from the Operational systems just explained above.
To create a real-time DWH we would have to merge both systems (several ways are being explored), a concept tha

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
t is against the reason of creating a DWH. Bigger challenges occur in terms of updating aggregated data in facts at real time, still maintaining the surrogate keys.
Besides, we would need lightening fast hardware to try this. Near Real time DWH is a trade-off between the conventional design and the dream of all clients today. The frequency of ETL updates in higher in this case for e.g. once in 2 hours. We can also analyze and use selective refreshes at shorter time intervals, while complete refreshes may still be kept further apart. Selective refreshes would look at only those tables that get updated regularly.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-03" number={3} question="What is Virtual Data Warehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
A virtual or point-to-poi

nt data warehousing strategy means that end-users are allowed to get at operational databases directly using whatever tools are enabled to the "data access network".
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-04" number={4} question="What are the different Types of Data Warehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
Types of data warehousing are:
1. Enterprise Data warehousing
2. ODS (Operational Data Store)
3. Data Mart
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-05" number={5} question="What are the Steps to Build the Data Warehouse?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
Gathering business requirements>>Identifying Sources>>Identifying Facts>>Defining Dimensions>>Define Attributes>>Redefine Dimensions / Attributes>>Organize Attribute Hierarchy>>Define R

elationship>>Assign Unique Identifiers.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-06" number={6} question="What are the Methodologies of Data Warehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
Every company has methodology of their own. However, to name a few SDLC Methodology, AIM methodology is standard used.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-07" number={7} question="What is Data Warehousing Hierarchy?" difficulty="hard" topic="Dimensions">
Hierarchies are logical structures that use ordered levels as a means of organizing data. A hierarchy can be used to define data aggregation. For example, in a time dimension, a hierarchy might aggregate data from the month level to the quarter level to the year level. A hierarchy can also be used to define a navigational dri

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content: ll path and to establish a family structure. Within a hierarchy, each level is logically connected to the levels above and below it. Data values at lower levels aggregate into the data values at higher levels. A dimension can be composed of more than one hierarchy. For example, in the product dimension, there might be two hierarchies--one for product categories and one for product suppliers. Dimension hierarchies also group levels from general to granular. Query tools use hierarchies to enable you to drill down into your data to view different levels of granularity. This is one of the key benefits of a data warehouse. When designing hierarchies, you must consider the relationships in business structures. Hierarchies impose a family structure on dimension values. For a particular level valu

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
e, a value at the next higher level is its parent, and values at the next lower level are its children. These familial relationships enable analysts to access data quickly.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-08" number={8} question="What is VLDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
The perception of what constitutes a VLDB continues to grow. A one-terabyte database would normally be considered VLDB. Degenerate dimension: it does not have any link with dimensions and it will not have any attribute.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-09" number={9} question="What is Loop in Data Warehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
In DWH loops may exist between the tables. If loops exist, then query generation will take more time, because more

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
than one path is available. It creates ambiguity also. Loops can be avoided by creating aliases of the table or by context.
Example: 4 Tables - Customer, Product, Time, Cost forming a close loop. Create alias for the cost to avoid loop.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-10" number={10} question="What is ODS?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
ODS is abbreviation of Operational Data Store. A database structure that is a repository for near real-time operational data rather than long term trend data. The ODS may further become the enterprise shared operational database, allowing operational systems that are being re-engineered to use the ODS as there operation databases.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-11" number={11} question="What is real time Data W

description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
arehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
Data warehousing captures business activity data. Real-time data warehousing captures business activity data as it occurs. As soon as the business activity is complete and there is data about it, the completed activity data flows into the data warehouse and becomes available instantly.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-12" number={12} question="What is a general purpose Scheduling Tool?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
The basic purpose of the scheduling tool in a DW Application is to streamline the flow of data from Source To Target at specific time or based on some condition.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-13" number={13} question="Explain in brief various fundamental stages of Dat

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
a Warehousing." difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
The following are the stages of data warehousing:
- **Offline Operational Database** — the stage of copying data off an operational system onto another server, so report-processing load against the copied data doesn't impact OS performance.
- **Offline Data Warehouse** — in this stage, data warehouses are updated from data in the OS, and the data warehouse's data is stored in a structure designed for facilitating reports.
- **Real-time Data Warehouse** — the data warehouse is updated whenever the OS performs a transaction.
- **Integrated Data Warehouse** — the data warehouse is updated by the OS at the time of performing a transaction; transactions are then generated and passed back into the operational systems.
</Question

description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.

Card>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-fund-14" number={14} question="What is a Three Tier Data Warehouse?" difficulty="medium" topic="Warehouse Fundamentals">
A data warehouse can be thought of as a three-tier system in which a middle system provides usable data in a secure way to end users. On either side of this middle system are the end users and the back-end data stores.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-01" number={15} question="What is Type 2 Version Dimension?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema Design">
Version dimension is the SCD type II in real time it using because of it will maintain the current data and full historical data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-02" number={16} question="How can you implement many relations in Star Schema Model?" difficulty="medium" topic="Star Schema">
Many-many relations can be implemented by using snowflake schema. With a max of n dimensions.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-03" number={17} question="What is Critical Column?" difficulty="medium" topic="Surrogate Key

Let us take one ex: Suppose 'XYZ' is customer in Bangalore, he was residing in the city from the last 5 years, in the period of 5 years he has made purchases worth of 3 lacs. Now, he moved to 'HYD'. When you update the 'XYZ' city to 'HYD' in your Warehouse, all the purchases by him will show in city 'HYD' only. This makes warehouse inconsistent. Here CITY is the Critical Column. Solution is use Surrogate Key.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-04" number={18} question="What is the main difference between Star and Snowflake Star Schema and which one is better and why?" difficulty="medium" topic="Star Schema">
If u have one to may relation ship in the data then only we choose snowflake schema, as per the performance-wise every-one go for the Star schema. Moreover, if the ETL i
