</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-05" number={19} question="What is the difference between star and snowflake schema, and when is each better?" difficulty="medium" topic="Star Schema">
- **Star schema** — dimension tables are mapped directly around one or more fact tables. It's a renormalized (denormalized) model, so no complicated joins are needed and queries run fast. It's the default choice when raw query performance matters most.
- **Snowflake schema** — the normalized form of a star schema: dimension tables are split into multiple related tables, which reduces redundancy and makes direct modification of dimension data easier, at the cost of mo

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
re, more complex joins and somewhat slower queries.

In short: pick snowflake when you have genuine one-to-many hierarchies within a dimension and want easier maintenance/browsing; pick star when query performance and simplicity are the priority, which is most analytics/BI workloads.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-06" number={20} question="What are the differences between Star and Snowflake Schema?" difficulty="medium" topic="Star Schema">
Star schema: A single fact table with N number of DimensionSnowflake schema: Any dimensions with extended dimensions are known as snowflake schema.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-07" number={21} question="What data type should a surrogate key use?" difficulty="medium" topic="Surrogate Keys">
There's no dedicated "surrogate k

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
ey" data type — the only hard requirement is that the value be unique per row. In practice surrogate keys are implemented as simple, system-generated sequences (`IDENTITY` in SQL Server, a `SEQUENCE` in Oracle, auto-increment elsewhere), so the standard and recommended data type is a plain **integer** (numeric), since integers index and join faster than strings.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-08" number={22} question="What is Bus Schema?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema Design">
BUS Schema is composed of a master suite of confirmed dimension and standardized definition if facts.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-09" number={23} question="What is Surrogate Key and where we use it?" difficulty="hard" topic="Surrogate Keys">
Surrogate key is a substitution for the

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
natural primary key. It is just a unique identifier or number for each row that can be used for the primary key to the table. The only requirement for a surrogate primary key is that it is unique for each row in the table.
Data warehouses typically use a surrogate, (also known as artificial or identity key), key for the dimension tables primary keys. They can use Info sequence generator, or Oracle sequence, or SQL Server Identity values for the surrogate key.
It is useful because the natural primary key (i.e. Customer Number in Customer table) can change and this makes updates more difficult.
Some tables have columns such as AIRPORT_NAME OR CITY_NAME which are stated as the primary keys (according to the business users) but ,not only can these change, indexing on a numerical value is prob

content:
ably better and you could consider creating a surrogate key called, say, AIRPORT_ID. This would be internal to the system and as far as the client is concerned, you may display only the AIRPORT_NAME.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-10" number={24} question="What is Hybrid Slowly Changing Dimension?" difficulty="hard" topic="SCD">
Hybrid SCDs are combination of both SCD 1 and SCD 2. It may happen that in a table, some columns are important and we need to track changes for them i.e. capture the historical data for them whereas in some columns even if the data changes, we don't care. For such tables we implement Hybrid SCDs, where in some columns are Type 1 and some are Type 2. You can add that it is not an intelligent key but similar to a sequence number and tied to a timestamp

Yes.

Galaxy schema is also known as fact constellation scheme. It requires no of fact tables to share dimension tables. In data, wares housing mainly the people are using the conceptual hierarchy.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-13" number={27} question="What are Slowly Changing Dimensions?" difficulty="medium" topic="SCD">
Dimensions that change over time are called Slowly Changing Dimensions. For instance, a product price

If the data in the Dimension table happen to change very rarely,then it is called as slowly changing dimension.
ex: changing the name and address of a person,which happens rerely.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-15" number={29} question="What is the need of Surrogate Key and why Primary Key not used as Surrogate Key?" difficulty="medium" topic="Surrogate Keys">
Surrogate Key is an artificial identifier for an entity. In surrogate key values are generated by the system sequentially(Like Identity property in SQL Server and Sequence in Oracle). They do not describe anything. Primary Key is a natural identifier for an entity. In Primary keys all the values are entered manually by the user which are uniquely identified. There will be no repetition of data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCar

title: Data Warehousing

d id="dwh-schema-16" number={30} question="What is Snow Flake Schema?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema Design">
Snowflake schemas normalize dimensions to eliminate redundancy. That is, the dimension data has been grouped into multiple tables instead of one large table. For example, a product dimension table in a star schema might be normalized into a products table, a product_category table, and a product_manufacturer table in a snowflake schema. While this saves space, it increases the number of dimension tables and requires more foreign key joins. The result is more complex queries and reduced query performance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-schema-17" number={31} question="What is the Importance of Surrogate Key in Data Warehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Surrogate Keys"

>
Surrogate Key is a Primary Key for a Dimension table. Most importance of using it is it is independent of underlying database. i.e Surrogate Key is not affected by the changes going on with a database.
</QuestionCard>

### Facts, Dimensions & Measures

<QuestionList topic="Facts, Dimensions & Measures" storagePrefix="dwh-dim">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-01" number={32} question="What is Junk Dimension?" difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensions">
A junk dimension is nothing but a dimension where a certain type of data is stored which is not appropriate to store in the schema. The nature of the junk dimension is usually a Boolean has flag values.
A single dimension is formed by a group of small dimensions got together. This can be considered as junk dimension.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-02" number={33} question="Differentiate between dimension and attribute?" difficulty="medium" topic="Relational Model">
In short, dimensions are nothing but which represents qualitative data. For example data l

content:
ike a plan, product, class are all considered as dimensions.
The attribute is nothing but a subset of a dimension. Within a dimension table, we will have attributes. The attributes can be textual or descriptive. For example, product name and product category are nothing but an attribute of product dimensions.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-03" number={34} question="What are Fact and Dimension and Measure?" difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensional Modeling">
Fact is key performance indicator to analyze the business. Dimension is used to analyze the fact. Without dimension there is no meaning for fact.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-04" number={35} question="What is Junk Dimension and what is the difference between Junk Dimension and Degenerated Dimension?" difficulty="

medium" topic="Dimensions">
Junk dimension: Grouping of Random flags and text attributes in a dimension and moving them to a separate sub dimension. Degenerate Dimension: Keeping the control information on Fact table ex: Consider a Dimension table with fields like order number and order line number and have 1:1 relationship with Fact table, In this case this dimension is removed and the order information will be directly store.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-05" number={36} question="Why Fact Table is in Normal Form?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fact Tables">
The fact table consists of the Index keys of the dimension/look up tables and the measures. So whenever we have the keys in a table. That it implies that the table is in the normal form.

Aggregate table contains the summary of existing warehouse data which is grouped to certain levels of dimensions. It is always easy to retrieve data from aggregated tables than visiting original table which has million records. Aggregate tables reduces the load in the database server and increases the performance of the query and can retrieve the result quickly.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-16" number={47} question="How do you load the Time Dimension?" difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensions">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-17" number={48} question="What is a Level of Granularity of a Fact Table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fact Tables">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-20" number={51} question="What are Semi additive and Factless Facts?" difficulty="hard" topic="Fact Tables">
Semi-Additive: Semi-additive facts are facts that can be summed up for some dimensions in the fact table, but not the others. For example:
Current_Balance and Profit_Margin are the facts.

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
Current_Balance is a semi-additive fact, as it makes sense to add them up for all accounts (what's the total current balance for all accounts in the bank?), but it does not make sense to add them up through time (adding up all current balances for a given account for each day of the month does not give us any useful information). A factless fact table captures the many-to-many relationships between dimensions, but contains no numeric or textual facts. They are often used to record events or coverage information.
Common examples of factless fact tables include:

- Identifying product promotion events (to determine promoted products that didn't sell)
- Tracking student attendance or registration events
- Tracking insurance-related accident events
- Identifying building, facility, and equipme

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-21" number={52} question="Explain degenerated dimension." difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensions">
A Degenerate dimension is a Dimension which has only a single attribute.
This dimension is typically represented as a single field in a fact table.
The data items thar are not facts and data items that do not fit into the existing dimensions are termed as Degenerate Dimensions.
Degenerate Dimensions are the fastest way to group similar transactions.
Degenerate Dimensions are used when fact tables represent transactional data.
They can be used as primary key for the fact table but they cannot act as foreign keys.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-dim-22" number={53} question="What are the different method

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
s of loading dimension tables?" difficulty="medium" topic="Dimensional Modeling">
There are two common methods for loading data into dimension tables:

- **Conventional load (slower)** — every constraint and key is validated against the incoming data *before* it is loaded, so data integrity is guaranteed throughout the load.
- **Direct load (faster)** — all constraints and keys are disabled before loading. Once loaded, the data is validated against the constraints; any rows found invalid are excluded from indexing and skipped in downstream processing.
</QuestionCard>

### OLTP, OLAP & Multidimensional Analysis

<QuestionCard id="dwh-oltp-01" number={59} question="Differentiate between OLTP and OLAP?" difficulty="medium" topic="OLAP">
OLTP stands for Online Transaction Process System
OLTP is known for maintaining transactional level data of the organization and generally, they are highly normalized. If it is OLTP route then it is going to be a star schema design.
OLAP stands for Online Analytical process system.
OLAP is known for a lot of analysis and fulfills reporting purposes. It is de-normalized form.
If it is an OLAP route then it is going to be a snowflake schema design.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-oltp-02" number={60} question="Where the Data Cube Te

content:
chnology is used?" difficulty="medium" topic="OLAP">
A multi-dimensional structure called the data cube. A data abstraction allows one to view aggregated data from a number of perspectives. Conceptually, the cube consists of a core or base cuboids, surrounded by a collection of sub-cubes/cuboids that represent the aggregation of the base cuboids along one or more dimensions. We refer to the dimension to be aggregated as the measure attribute, while the remaining dimensions are known as the feature attributes.
</QuestionCard>

- **Extract** — read data from a specified source database and pull out the desired subset.
- **Transform** — work with the extracted data, using rules, lookup tables, or combinations with other data, to convert it into the desired state.
- **Load** — write the resulting, transformed data into the target database/warehouse.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-etl-12" number={94} question="What is Brodcasting and Replicate?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transformations">
- **Broadcast** — takes data from multiple inputs, combines it, and sends it to all the output ports. Eg: you have 2 incoming flows (this can be data parallelism or component parallelism) on a Broadcast component, one with 10 records and the other with 20 records — every outgoing flow (any number of flows) will have 10 + 20 = 30 records.
- **Replicate** — replicates the data for a particular partition and sends it out to multiple output ports of the component, but maintains partition integrity. Eg: an incoming flow to replicate has a data parallelism level of 2, with one partition having 10 records

If the user wants to group the records on particular field values then rollup is best way to do that. Rollup is a multi-stage transform function and it contains the following mandatory functions.
1. initialise
2. rollup
3. finalise
   Also need to declare one temporary variable if you want to get counts of a particular group.

For each of the group, first it does call the initialise function once, followed by rollup function calls for each of the records in the group and finally calls the finalise function once at the end of last rollup call.

There are three different kinds of data models that are available and they are as follows:
- Conceptual
- Logical
- Physical

Conceptual data model:
- As the name itself implies that this data model depicts the high-level design of the available physical data.

Logical data model:
- Within the logical model, the entity names, entity relationships, attributes, primary keys and foreign keys will show up.

Physical data model:
- Based on this data model, the view will give out more inform

<QuestionCard id="dwh-model-02" number={110} question="What is Dimensional Modeling?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Modeling">
Dimensional Modeling is a design concept used by many data warehouse designers to build their data warehouse. In this design model all the data is stored in two types of tables — Fact tables and Dimension tables. The fact table contains the facts/measurements of the business, and the dimension table contains the context of those measurements, i.e. the dimensions on which the facts are calculated.

Dimensional modeling itself is done in three stages, matching general data modeling practice:
1. Conc

eptual modeling

<QuestionCard id="dwh-model-03" number={111} question="What is difference between Er Modeling and Dimensional Modeling?" difficulty="medium" topic="ER Modeling">
Basic difference is E-R modeling will have logical and physical model. Dimensional model will have only physical model. E-R modeling is used for normalizing the OLTP database design. Dimensional modeling is used for de-normalizing the ROLAP/MOLAP design.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-model-04" number={112} question="What is Er Diagram?" difficulty="hard" topic="ER Modeling">
The Entity-Relationship (ER) model was originally proposed in 1976 as a way to unify the network and relational database views. Simply stated the ER model is a conceptual data m

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
odel that views the real world as entities and relationships. A basic component of the model is the Entity-Relationship diagram, which is used to visually represent data objects. Since Chen wrote his paper the model has been extended and today it is commonly used for database design for the database designer, the utility of the ER model is: it maps well to the relational model. The constructs used in the ER model can easily be transformed into relational tables. It is simple and easy to understand with a minimum of training. Therefore, the database designer to communicate the design to the end user can use the model. In addition, the model can be used as a design plan by the database developer to implement a data model in specific database management software.
</QuestionCard>

### Normalization & Relational Theory

<QuestionList topic="Normalization & Relational Theory" storagePrefix="dwh-norm">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-01" number={123} question="What is Normalization?" difficulty="medium" topic="Normalization">
Database normalization is a data design and organization process applied to data structures based on rules that help build relational databases. In relational database design, the process of organizing data to minimize redundancy. Normalization usually involves dividing a database into two or more tables and defining relationships between the tables. The objective is to isolate data so that additions, deletions, and modifications of a field can be made in just one table and then propagated through the rest of the database via the defined relationships

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-02" number={124} question="What are different Normalization Forms?" difficulty="hard" topic="Normalization">
- 1NF: Eliminate Repeating Groups Make a separate table for each set of related attributes, and give each table a primary key. Each field contains at most one value from its attribute domain.
- 2NF: Eliminate Redundant Data If an attribute depends on only part of a multi-valued key, remove it to a separate table.
- 3NF: Eliminate Columns Not Dependent On Key If attributes do not contribute to a description of the key, remove them to a separate table. All attributes must be directly dependent on the primary key.
- BCNF: Boyce-Codd Normal Form If there are non-trivial dependencies between candidate key attributes, separate them out into di

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
stinct tables.
- 4NF: Isolate Independent Multiple Relationships No table may contain two or more 1:n or n:m relationships that are not directly related.
- 5NF: Isolate Semantically Related Multiple Relationships There may be practical constrains on information that justify separating logically related many-to-many relationships.
- ONF: Optimal Normal Form A model limited to only simple (elemental) facts, as expressed in Object Role Model notation.
- DKNF: Domain-Key Normal Form A model free from all modification anomalies. Remember, these normalization guidelines are cumulative. For a database to be in 3NF, it must first fulfill all the criteria of a 2NF and 1NF database.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-03" number={125} question="What is Relational Algebra?" difficulty="medium

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-04" number={126} question="What is Relational Calculus?" difficulty="hard" topic="Relational Theory">
It is an applied predicate calculus specifically tailored for relational databases proposed by E. F. Codd.
E.g. of languages based on it are DSL ALPHA, QUEL.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-05" number={127} question="How does Tuple oriented Relational calculus differ from Domain oriented Relational Calculus?" difficulty="hard" topic="Relational Theory">
The tuple-oriented calculus uses a tuple variables i.e., variable whose only permitted values are tuples of that relation

The domain-oriented calculus has domain variables i.e., variables that range over the underlying domains instead of over relation. E.g. ILL, DEDUCE.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-06" number={128} question="What is Functional Dependency?" difficulty="hard" topic="Relational Theory">
A Functional dependency is denoted by X → Y between two sets of attributes X and Y that are subsets of R specifies a constraint on the possible tuple that can form a relation state r of R. The constraint is for any two tuples t1 and t2 in r if t1[X] = t2[X] then they have t1[Y] = t2[Y]. This means the value of X component of a tuple uniquely determines the value of component Y.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-07" number={129} question="What is Multivalued Dependency?" diffic

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.

ulty="hard" topic="Relational Theory">
Multivalued dependency denoted by X → Y specified on relation schema R, where X and Y are both subsets of R, specifies the following constraint on any relation r of R: if two tuples t1 and t2 exist in r such that t1[X] = t2[X] then t3 and t4 should also exist in r with the following properties

t3[x] = t4[X] = t1[X] = t2[X]
t3[Y] = t1[Y] and t4[Y] = t2[Y]
t3[Z] = t2[Z] and t4[Z] = t1[Z]
where [Z = (R-(X U Y)) ]
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-08" number={130} question="What is Lossless Join Property?" difficulty="hard" topic="Normalization">
It guarantees that the spurious tuple generation does not occur with respect to relation schemas after decomposition.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-10" number={132} question="What is Fully Functional Dependency?" difficulty="hard" topic="Relational Theory">
It is based on concept of full functional dependency. A functional dependency X → Y is full functional dependency if removal of any attribute A from X means that the dependency does not hold any more.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-11" number={133} question="What is 2NF?" difficulty="medium" topic="Normalization">
A relation schema R is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and every non-prime attribute A in R is fully functionally dependent on primary key.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-17" number={139} question="What is Join Dependency and Inclusion Dependency?" difficulty="hard" topic="Relational Theory">
Join Dependency:
A Join dependency is generalization of Multivalued dependency. A JD `{R1, R2,..., Rn}` is said to hold over a relation R if R1, R2, R3,..., Rn is a lossless-join decomposition of R. There is no set of sound and complete inference rules for JD.
Inclusion Dependency:
An Inclusion Dependency is a statement of the form that some columns of a relation are contained in other columns. A foreign key constraint is an example of inclusion dependency.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-norm-19" number={141} question="Are resulting Relations of Product and Join Operation the same?" difficulty="medium" topic="Normalization">

PRODUCT: Concatenation of every row in one relation with every row in another.
JOIN: Concatenation of rows from one relation and related rows from another.

### Relational Model: Entities, Attributes & Keys

<QuestionList topic="Relational Model: Entities, Attributes & Keys" storagePrefix="dwh-rel">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-rel-01" number={142} question="What is an integrity constraints and what are different types of integrity constraints?" difficulty="medium" topic="Relational Model">
An integrity constraint is nothing but a specific requirement that the data in the database has to meet. It is nothing but a business rule for a particular column in a table. In the data warehouse concept, they are 5 integrity constraints.
The following are the integrity constraints:
- Null
- Unique key
- Primary key
- Foreign key
- Check
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-rel-02" number={143} question="Describe referential Integrity?" difficulty="medium" top

ic="Relational Model">
A rule defined on a column (or set of columns) in one table that allows the insert or update of a row only if the value for the column or set of columns (the dependent value) matches a value in a column of a related table (the referenced value). It also specifies the type of data manipulation allowed on referenced data and the action to be performed on dependent data as a result of any action on referenced data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-rel-03" number={144} question="What is an Integrity Constrains?" difficulty="medium" topic="Relational Model">
An integrity constraint is a declarative way to define a business rule for a column of a table.
</QuestionCard>

Relational Data Base Management Systems (RDBMS) are database management systems that maintain data records and indices in tables. Relationships may be created and maintained across and among the data and tables. In a relational database, relationships between data items are expressed by means of tables. Interdependencies among these tables are expressed by data values rather than by pointers. This allows a high degree of data independence. An RDBMS has the capability to recombine the data items from different files, providing powerful tools for data usage.
</QuestionCard>

- Redundancy is controlled.
- Unauthorised access

- Alternate Key:
  - All Candidate Keys excluding the Primary Key are known as Alternate Keys.
- Artificial Key:
  - If no obvious key, either stand alone or compound is available, then the last resort is to simply create a key, by assigning a unique number to each record or occurrence. Then this is known as developing an artificial key.
- Compound Key:
  - If no single data element uniquely identifies occurrences within a construct, then combining multiple elements to create a unique identifier for the construct is known as creating a compound key.
- Natural Key:
  - When one of the data elements stored within a construct is utilized as the primary key, then it is called the natural key.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-01" number={164} question="What is the difference between a view and a materialized view?" difficulty="medium" topic="Views">
- **View** — a logical, virtual structure with no data of its own; every query against it re-runs the underlying `SELECT` against the base tables, so changes to the base tables are always reflected immediately. A view occupies no storage space beyond its stored definition.
- **Materialized view** — stores the pre-calculated result of the query physically on disk. The query only executes once (at creation/refresh time); afterward every read hits the stored result set, which is why materialized views are muc

A Database contains one or more Rollback Segments to temporarily store "undo" information.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-03" number={166} question="What is a Table Space?" difficulty="medium" topic="Storage">
A database is divided into Logical Storage Unit called table spaces. A table space is used to grouped related logical structures together.
</QuestionCard>

A row is stored in a hash cluster based on the result of applying a hash function to the row's cluster key value. All rows with the same hash key value are stores together on disk.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-06" number={169} question="What is Schema?" difficulty="easy" topic="Database Objects">
A schema is collection of database objects of a User.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-07" number={170} question="What is Table?" difficulty="medium" topic="Database Objects">
A table is the basic unit of data storage in an ORACLE database. The tables of a database hold all of the user accessible data. Table data is stored in rows and columns.

Clusters are groups of one or more tables physically stores together to share common columns and are often used together.

When an instance of an ORACLE database is started, its control file is used to identify the database and redo log files that must be opened for database operation to proceed. It is also used in database recovery.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-21" number={184} question="What is Online redo Log?" difficulty="medium" topic="Storage">
The On-line Redo Log is a set of tow or more on-line redo files that record all committed changes made to the database. Whenever a transaction is committed, the corresponding redo entries temporarily stores in redo log buffers of the SGA are written to an on-line redo log file by the background process LGWR. The on-line redo log files are used in cyclical fashion.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-22" number={185} question="What is Log Switch?" difficulty="medium" topic="Database Objects">
The point at which ORACLE ends writing to one online redo log file and begins writing to another is called a log switch.
</QuestionCard>

On the fact table it is best to use bitmap indexes. Dimension tables can use bitmap and/or the other types of clustered/non-clustered, unique/non-unique indexes.
To my knowledge, SQLServer does not support bitmap indexes. Only Oracle supports bitmaps.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-24" number={187} question="What is View?" difficulty="medium" topic="Views">
A simple view can be thought of as a subset of a table. It can be used for retrieving data, as well as updating or deleting rows. Rows updated or deleted in the view are updated or deleted in the table the view was created with. It should also be noted that as data in the original table c

The data accessed through a view is actually constructed using standard T-SQL select command and can come from one to many different base tables or even other views.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-25" number={188} question="What is Indexing and what are the different kinds of Indexing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Indexing">
Indexing is a technique for determining how quickly specific data can be found.
Types:
- Binary search style indexing
- B-Tree indexing
- Inverted list indexing
M+ emory resident table
- Table indexing
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-obj-26" number={189} question="What is ROWID?" difficul

title: Data Warehousing

content:
ty="medium" topic="DBMS Internals">
The ROWID is a unique database-wide physical address for every row on every table. Once assigned (when the row is first inserted into the database), it never changes until the row is deleted or the table is dropped.
The ROWID consists of the following three components, the combination of which uniquely identifies the physical storage location of the row.
Oracle database file number, which contains the block with the rows
Oracle block address, which contains the row
The row within the block (because each block can hold many rows)
The ROWID is used internally in indexes as a quick means of retrieving rows with a particular key value. Application developers also use it in SQL statements as a quick way to access a row once they know the ROWID.
</QuestionCard

<QuestionCard id="dwh-admin-01" number={190} question="Explain the advantages Of RAID 1 and 1/0 And 5 and what type of RAID setup would you put your Tx Logs." difficulty="medium" topic="DB Administration">
The basic advantage of RAID is to speed up the data reading from permanent storage device (hard disk).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-admin-02" number={191} question="What are the characteristics of data files?" difficulty="medium" topic="Administration">
A data file can be associated with only one database. Once created a data file can't change size. One or more data files form a logical unit of database storage called a table space.
</QuestionCard>

A full backup is an operating system backup of all data files, on- line redo log files and control file that constitute ORACLE database and the parameter.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-admin-04" number={193} question="What is Partial Backup?" difficulty="medium" topic="Backup & Recovery">
A Partial Backup is any operating system backup short of a full backup, taken while the database is open or shut down.
</QuestionCard>

An instance can be started in (or later altered to be in) restricted mode so that when the d

Complete database recovery from disk failure is possible only in ARCHIVELOG mode. Online database backup is possible only in ARCHIVELOG mode.

title: Data Warehousing

content:
### Transactions & DBMS Internals

<QuestionList topic="Transactions & DBMS Internals" storagePrefix="dwh-txn">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-01" number={203} question="Describe Three Levels of Data Abstraction?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
There are three levels of abstraction:
- **Physical level** — the lowest level of abstraction, describes how data are stored.
- **Logical level** — the next higher level of abstraction, describes what data are stored in the database and what relationships exist among that data.
- **View level** — the highest level of abstraction, describes only part of the entire database.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-02" number={204} question="What is Data Independence?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
Data independence means that “th

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
e application is independent of the storage structure and access strategy of data”. In other words, The ability to modify the schema definition in one level should not affect the schema definition in the next higher level.
Two types of Data Independence:
Physical Data Independence: Modification in physical level should not affect the logical level.
Logical Data Independence: Modification in logical level should affect the view level.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-03" number={205} question="What is a View and how it is related to Data Independence?" difficulty="medium" topic="Views">
A view may be thought of as a virtual table, that is, a table that does not really exist in its own right but is instead derived from one or more underlying base table. In other words, there is no

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
stored file that direct represents the view instead a definition of view is stored in data dictionary. Growth and restructuring of base tables is not reflected in views. Thus the view can insulate users from the effects of restructuring and growth in the database. Hence accounts for logical data independence.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-04" number={206} question="What Is DDL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
A data base schema is specifies by a set of definitions expressed by a special language called DDL.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-05" number={207} question="What Is VDL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
It specifies user views and their mappings to the conceptual schema (View Definition Language)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
-06" number={208} question="What is SDL?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
This language is to specify the internal schema. This language may specify the mapping between two schemas. (Storage Definition Language)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-07" number={209} question="What Is Data Storage Definition Language?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
The storage structures and access methods used by database system are specified by a set of definition in a special type of DDL called data storage-definition language.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-08" number={210} question="What Is DML?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
This language that enable user to access or manipulate data as organized by appropriate data model.
Procedural DML or Low level:

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
DML requires a user to specify what data are needed and how to get those data.
Non-Procedural DML or High level: DML requires a user to specify what data are needed without specifying how to get those data.
(Data Manipulation Language)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-09" number={211} question="What is Query Evaluation Engine?" difficulty="easy" topic="Transactions">
It executes low-level instruction generated by compiler.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-10" number={212} question="What is DDL Interpreter?" difficulty="easy" topic="Transactions">
It interprets DDL statements and record them in tables containing metadata.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-11" number={213} question="What is Record at a time?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
The Low lev

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
el or Procedural DML can specify and retrieve each record from a set of records. This retrieve of a record is said to be Record-at-a-time.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-12" number={214} question="What is Set at a time or Set oriented?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
The High level or Non-procedural DML can specify and retrieve many records in a single DML statement. This retrieve of a record is said to be Set-at-a-time or Set-oriented.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-13" number={215} question="What is Durability in DBMS?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
Once the DBMS informs the user that a transaction has successfully completed, its effects should persist even if the system crashes before all its changes are reflected on disk. This property is

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
called durability.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-14" number={216} question="What do you mean by Atomicity and Aggregation?" difficulty="medium" topic="Transactions">
Atomicity:
Either all actions are carried out or none are. Users should not have to worry about the effect of incomplete transactions. DBMS ensures this by undoing the actions of incomplete transactions.
Aggregation:
A concept which is used to model a relationship between a collection of entities and relationships. It is used when we need to express a relationship among relationships.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-txn-15" number={217} question="What is Phantom Deadlock?" difficulty="hard" topic="Transactions">

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-01" number={229} question="How many clustered indexes can you create for a table in DWH and in case Of Truncate and Delete command what happens to table which has unique Id." difficulty="medium" topic="Indexing">
You can have only one clustered index per table. If you use delete command, you can rollback... it fills your redo log files.
If you do not want records, you may use truncate command, which will be faster and does not fill your redo log file.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-02" number={230} question="What is Outer Join?" difficulty="medium" topic="Joins">
An outer join is used when one wants to select all the reco

rds from a port - whether it has satisfied the join criteria or not.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-03" number={231} question="What are Cartesian Joins?" difficulty="medium" topic="Joins">
A Cartesian join will get you a Cartesian product. A Cartesian join is when you join every row of one table to every row of another table. You can also get one by joining every row of a table to every row of itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-04" number={232} question="What is the purpose of having Stored Procedures in a Database?" difficulty="medium" topic="Stored Procedures">
Main Purpose of Stored Procedure for reduse the network trafic and all SQL statement executing in cursor so speed too high.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-05" number={233} question="Why migh

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
t you create a Stored Procedure with Recompile Option?" difficulty="hard" topic="Stored Procedures">
Recompile is useful when the tables referenced by the stored proc undergoes a lot of modification/ deletion/ addition of data. Due to the heavy modification activity the execute plan becomes outdated and hence the stored proc performance goes down. If we create the stored proc with recompile option, the SQL server wont cache a plan for this stored proc and it will be recompiled every time it is run.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-06" number={234} question="What is Cursor?" difficulty="medium" topic="SQL">
The oracle engine uses work areas for internal processing in order to the execute SQL statement is called cursor. There are two types of cursors like Implecit cursor and Explic

it cursor. Implicit cursor is using for internal processing and Explicit cursor is using for user open for data required.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-07" number={235} question="Explain the difference between Truncate and Delete Commands?" difficulty="medium" topic="SQL">
Truncate :
- It is a DDL command, used to delete tables or clusters. Since it is a DDL command hence it is auto commit and Rollback can't be performed. It is faster than delete.

Delete:
- It is DML command, generally used to delete a record, clusters or tables. Rollback command can be performed , in order to retrieve the earlier deleted things. To make deleted things permanently, "commit" command should be used.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-08" number={236} question="How would you find out whe

ther SQL Query is using Indices you Expect?" difficulty="medium" topic="SQL">
Explain plan can be reviewed to check the execution plan of the query. This would guide if the expected indexes are used or not.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-09" number={237} question="What is a Query?" difficulty="medium" topic="SQL">
A query is one or more statements that request data from a database. If the data is available, then the requested data returns in the form of a table which contains rows and columns. Queries are sent to the databases in a language called SQL. However, when using the Report Panel, SQL knowledge is not required.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-10" number={238} question="What is Stored Procedure?" difficulty="medium" topic="Stored Procedures">
A stored procedu

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
re is a named group of SQL statements that have been previously created and stored in the server database. Stored procedures accept input parameters so that a single procedure can be used over the network by several clients using different input data. And when the procedure is modified, all clients automatically get the new version. Stored procedures reduce network traffic and improve performance. Stored procedures can be used to help ensure the integrity of the database.
e.g. sp_helpdb, sp_renamedb, sp_depends etc.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-11" number={239} question="What is Trigger?" difficulty="medium" topic="Triggers">
A trigger is an SQL procedure that initiates an action when an event (INSERT, DELETE or UPDATE) occurs. Triggers are stored in and managed by the DBMS.

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
Triggers are used to maintain the referential integrity of data by changing the data in a systematic fashion. A trigger cannot be called or executed; the DBMS automatically fires the trigger as a result of a data modification to the associated table. Triggers can be viewed as similar to stored procedures in that both consist of procedural logic that is stored at the database level. Stored procedures, however, are not event-drive and are not attached to a specific table as triggers are. Stored procedures are explicitly executed by invoking a CALL to the procedure while triggers are implicitly executed. In addition, triggers can also execute stored procedures.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-12" number={240} question="What is meant by Query Optimization?" difficulty="hard" topic="

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
SQL">
The phase that identifies an efficient execution plan for evaluating a query that has the least estimated cost is referred to as query optimization.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-13" number={241} question="What do you mean by Correlated Subquery?" difficulty="hard" topic="SQL">
Subqueries, or nested queries, are used to bring back a set of rows to be used by the parent query. Depending on how the subquery is written, it can be executed once for the parent query or it can be executed once for each row returned by the parent query. If the subquery is executed for each row of the parent, this is called a correlated subquery.
A correlated subquery can be easily identified if it contains any references to the parent subquery columns in its WHERE clause. Columns from the subqu

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
ery cannot be referenced anywhere else in the parent query. The following example demonstrates a non-correlated subquery.
E.g. Select * From CUST Where '10/03/1990' IN (Select ODATE From ORDER Where CUST. CNUM = ORDER. CNUM)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-sql-14" number={242} question="What are Stand alone procedures?" difficulty="medium" topic="SQL">
Procedures that are not part of a package are known as stand-alone because they independently defined. A good example of a stand-alone procedure is one written in a SQL*Forms application. These types of procedures are not available for reference from other Oracle tools. Another limitation of stand-alone procedures is that they are compiled at run time, which slows execution.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

Data Mining is used for

the estimation of the future. <QuestionCard id="dwh-ent-07" number={261} question="What is the main difference between Inmon and Kimball Philosophies of Data Warehousing?" difficulty="medium" topic="DW Philosophy">

s a conformed dimension of the data marts. Hence, a unified view of the enterprise can be obtained from the dimension modeling on a local departmental level. Inmon beliefs in creating a data warehouse on a subject-by-subject area basis. Hence, the development of the data warehouse can start with data from the online store. Other subject areas can be added to the data warehouse as their needs arise. Point-of-sale (POS) data can be added later if management decides it is necessary.

content:
ly just picking the right method for the source and volume involved:

- Validation in a data warehouse is typically done manually rather than through a dedicated tool.
- If the source and target are both relational, write SQL scripts that compare row counts, key sets, and aggregate values between source and target.
- If the source is a flat file or non-relational and the data volume is small, spreadsheet checks (e.g. Excel) are often enough.
- For larger or more complex loads, build dummy/staging tables to validate the ETL logic before trusting the mart.
</QuestionCard>

ata modeler captures information about the data itself: its source (where and how it originated), its nature (data type, nullability, valid values, etc.), and its behavior (how it's modified or derived, and its life cycle). This is captured in a data dictionary.

Examples of metadata include data element descriptions, data type descriptions, attribute/property descriptions, range/domain descriptions, and process/method descriptions. The repository environment encompasses all corporate metadata resources — database catalogs, data dictionaries, and navigation services.

Metadata is also presented at the data mart level: subsets, facts, dimensions, ODS, etc. For a data warehouse user, metadata provides vital information for analysis and decision support, and it insulates the warehouse from ch

anges in the schema of operational systems.

<QuestionCard id="dwh-ent-18" number={272} question="What is Data Cardinality?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Quality">
Cardinality is the term used in database relations to denote the occurrences of data on either side of the relation.
There are 3 basic types of cardinality:
High data cardinality:
Values of a data column are very uncommon.
e.g.: email ids and the user names

Normal data cardinality:
Values of a data column are som

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
ewhat uncommon but never unique.
e.g.: A data column containing LAST_NAME (there may be several entries of the same last name)

Low data cardinality:
Values of a data column are very usual.
e.g.: flag statuses: 0/1
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-ent-19" number={273} question="What is Chained Data Replication?" difficulty="hard" topic="Data Quality">
In Chain Data Replication, the non-official data set distributed among many disks provides for load balancing among the servers within the data warehouse.
Blocks of data are spread across clusters and each cluster can contain a complete set of replicated data. All data block in every cluster is a unique permutation of the data in other clusters.
When a disk fails then all the calls made to the data in that disk are redirected to the oth

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
er disks when the data has been replicated.
At times replicas and disks are added online without having to move around the data in the existing copy or affect the arm movement of the disk.
In load balancing, Chain Data Replication has multiple servers within the data warehouse share data request processing since data already have replicas in each server disk.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="dwh-ent-20" number={274} question="What is the difference between Enterprise Data Warehouse and Data Warehouse?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Marts">
Big Organizations have a lot of diversified sources of data. There might be a data warehouse exclusively for transport and others for data related to the project the company runs. In such case the complete enterprise's (company's) data warehouse is a

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
big combination of all and is known as the Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a data warehouse for a retail company's sales reporting."** Start by nailing down the source systems and grain, then walk through staging, a star schema for the sales fact table (product, store, date, promotion dimensions), and how OLTP data gets denormalized for reporting performance.
- **"Our OLAP queries are slow — where do you start?"** Probe fact table grain, aggregation/rollup strategy, indexing choice (bitmap vs. B-tree), and whether the underlying MOLAP/ROLAP/HOLAP trade-offs actually fit the data volume and query patterns.
- **"How would you choose between a data warehouse, a data lake, and a lakehouse for a new analytics platform?"** Talk through schema-on-write vs. schema-on-read, cost profile, data variety, and the query patterns each

title: Data Warehousing
description: Data warehousing interview questions covering OLAP vs OLTP, star/snowflake schemas, ETL, dimensional modeling, and warehouse architecture for data engineers.
content:
is actually good at.
- **"A dimension needs to track history — how do you model that, and how does it flow through the ETL?"** Walk through SCD Type 2 (or a hybrid approach) plus the load logic — surrogate keys, effective-dating, detecting changes — that keeps it correct.
- **"How would you migrate an on-prem warehouse to the cloud without breaking downstream BI reports?"** Discuss re-architecting ETL/ELT, keeping schema parity during the transition, and a reconciliation/validation strategy before cutting reports over.

title: Level 2 - Core Concepts
description: Core concepts in data engineering including modeling, warehousing, quality, and governance
content:
## Level 2 - Core Concepts: Data Engineering Discipline

This section covers the core concepts and disciplines that define modern data engineering. These topics are essential for building reliable, scalable data systems.

title: Level 2 - Core Concepts
description: Core concepts in data engineering including modeling, warehousing, quality, and governance
content:
## Topics Covered

- **Data Modeling**: Designing effective data models with star schemas, dimension tables, and slowly changing dimensions
- **Data Warehousing**: Architecture and design patterns for building data warehouses and marts
- **Data Quality**: Ensuring data reliability through validation, testing, and monitoring
- **Data Governance**: Managing data responsibly with policies, catalogs, and access controls

A strong understanding of these concepts is necessary for designing and managing enterprise data systems.

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
## What is Data Engineering: Role, Responsibilities, and Lifecycle

**Data engineering is the discipline of designing, building, and maintaining the infrastructure that stores, processes, and serves data at scale — the foundation every downstream analytics, reporting, and machine learning workload depends on.**

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
## Key Concepts

At its core, data engineering is about **building and maintaining data pipelines** — systems that move data from source systems through transformation layers so it's reliably available for analysis, reporting, and machine learning. That work spans four recurring responsibilities: **data infrastructure** (reliable, scalable storage and processing systems), **pipeline architecture** (designing the ETL/ELT workflows that move data), **data quality** (ensuring accuracy and consistency), and **performance optimization** (keeping queries and transformations efficient as volume grows). The role leans on a specific skill set — SQL, Python, cloud platforms, data modeling, and system design — and the work itself follows a repeatable lifecycle: data is generated, ingested, processed,

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
stored, served, and finally consumed by analysts, data scientists, and applications.

---

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="What is Data Engineering" storagePrefix="what-is-data-engineering">

<QuestionCard id="wde-01" number={1} question="What is data engineering, and why does it matter to an organization?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Data engineering is the practice of designing, building, and maintaining the infrastructure that stores, processes, and serves data at scale. It matters because data engineers create the foundation that lets an organization collect, transform, and analyze data reliably — without that foundation, analytics, reporting, and machine learning all sit on unreliable ground.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-02" number={2} question="What is a data engineer's primary responsibility?" difficulty="easy" topic="Role">
A data engin

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
eer's primary responsibility is to build and maintain data pipelines — systems that move data from source systems, through transformation layers, to make it available for analysis, reporting, and machine learning. The emphasis is on the plumbing and infrastructure that gets data where it needs to go, reliably and repeatably.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-03" number={3} question="How does a data engineer's focus differ from a data scientist's?" difficulty="easy" topic="Role">
Data scientists focus on extracting insights from data — analysis, modeling, prediction. Data engineers focus on the infrastructure and pipelines that make that data available and trustworthy in the first place: data infrastructure, pipeline architecture, data quality, and performance optimization. One builds

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
the foundation; the other builds on top of it.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-04" number={4} question="What does “data infrastructure” mean as one of a data engineer's core responsibilities?" difficulty="easy" topic="Responsibilities">
Data infrastructure refers to building reliable, scalable systems for data storage and processing — the databases, warehouses, lakes, and compute platforms that everything else is built on top of. If this layer isn't reliable or doesn't scale, every downstream pipeline and analysis inherits that fragility.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-05" number={5} question="What does “pipeline architecture” involve, and why does the ETL/ELT design choice matter?" difficulty="medium" topic="Responsibilities">
Pipeline architecture is designing the ETL/ELT

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
workflows that handle data movement — deciding where data gets extracted from, where and when it gets transformed, and how it lands in its final destination. Getting this design right determines how maintainable, debuggable, and cost-efficient the whole data flow is, since it dictates where complexity and compute cost actually live.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-06" number={6} question="Why is data quality treated as one of the four core responsibilities of a data engineer?" difficulty="easy" topic="Responsibilities">
Data quality means ensuring accuracy, consistency, and reliability of data as it flows through the platform. It's called out as a core responsibility — not a nice-to-have — because every downstream consumer (dashboards, models, reports) inherits whatever quality prob

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
lems exist upstream; fixing quality at the source is far cheaper than firefighting it in a hundred dashboards later.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-07" number={7} question="What does “performance optimization” mean in a data engineering context?" difficulty="medium" topic="Responsibilities">
Performance optimization means making queries and transformations efficient at scale — the same pipeline that works fine on a small dataset needs to keep working as data volume, query concurrency, and complexity grow. It's a continuous responsibility rather than a one-time task, since data volumes and usage patterns keep shifting.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-08" number={8} question="Why are SQL and Python considered the core languages for data engineering?" difficulty="easy" topic="S

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
kills">
SQL is the lingua franca for data transformation and querying — nearly every warehouse, lake engine, and BI tool speaks it. Python is the most common language for pipeline scripting and automation — orchestration logic, custom transformations, and integrations are typically written in it. Together they cover both the "what data should look like" and "how do I move and shape it" sides of the job.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-09" number={9} question="Why do data engineers also need cloud platform knowledge, data modeling skills, and system design skills?" difficulty="medium" topic="Skills">
- **Cloud platforms** — modern data infrastructure runs on AWS, GCP, or Azure, so operating there is a baseline expectation, not a bonus skill.
- **Data modeling** — structuring data for

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
efficient querying and analysis determines whether downstream consumers get clean, fast answers or confusing, slow ones.
- **System design** — building scalable, resilient data architectures is what lets a platform keep working as data volume and the number of consumers grow.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-10" number={10} question="Walk through the stages of the data engineering lifecycle, from generation to consumption." difficulty="medium" topic="Lifecycle">
The lifecycle runs: **Data Generation → Ingestion → Processing → Storage → Serving → Consumption**.

1. **Generation** — data is created in source systems (databases, APIs, logs, IoT devices)
2. **Ingestion** — data is collected and brought into the platform, either batch or streaming
3. **Processing** — data is cleaned, tra

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
nsformed, and enriched
4. **Storage** — data is stored in warehouses, lakes, or databases
5. **Serving** — data is made available to consumers via APIs or query engines
6. **Consumption** — analysts, data scientists, and applications use the data

Each stage hands off to the next, so a failure or quality issue early in the chain propagates through everything downstream of it.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="wde-11" number={11} question="What topics does a typical data engineering learning roadmap cover?" difficulty="easy" topic="Roadmap">
A typical roadmap covers: programming fundamentals (Python, SQL, Java/Scala), database systems (RDBMS, NoSQL, data warehouses), cloud platforms and infrastructure, pipeline orchestration (Airflow, dbt), big data tools (Spark, Kafka), and monitoring and

title: What is Data Engineering
description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
testing practices. It's designed to build from language and database fundamentals up through orchestration and operational maturity.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Explain data engineering to a non-technical stakeholder."** Frame it around the plumbing analogy: you're the one making sure clean water (data) reliably reaches every tap (dashboard, model, report) in the building, even as usage grows.
- **"Walk me through what happens to a single event, from the moment it's generated to the moment an analyst sees it in a dashboard."** Trace it through the six lifecycle stages — generation, ingestion, processing, storage, serving, consumption — naming a concrete tool or pattern at each step.
- **"Why do we need a dedicated data engineer instead of having analysts or data scientists build their own pipelines?"** Anchor the answer in the four core responsibilities — infrastructure, pipeline architecture, data quality, perf

description: Interview questions on what data engineering is, a data engineer's core responsibilities and must-have skills, and the end-to-end data engineering lifecycle.
content:
ormance — and explain why treating those as first-class, owned work (rather than side projects) is what keeps a platform reliable at scale.
- **"What skills would you look for when hiring a data engineer?"** Walk through SQL, Python, cloud platforms, data modeling, and system design, and explain what gap each one closes.
