title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
### Memory Management & Persistence

<QuestionList topic="Memory Management" storagePrefix="spark-mem">

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-01" number={99} question="How will you do memory tuning in Spark?" difficulty="hard" topic="Memory">
Memory tuning has to account for the amount of memory objects consume, the cost of accessing them, and garbage-collection overhead. Since Spark caches objects in memory, start by measuring usage: cache a representative RDD and check its size on the Storage page of the Web UI, then estimate memory needs from there. To reduce usage where needed:
- Prefer arrays of primitives/objects over linked lists or `HashMap`s — the `fastutil` library provides convenient primitive-friendly collection classes for Java.
- Reduce nested data structures with many small objects a

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
nd pointers (a linked list, for example, carries a pointer per node).
- Use numeric IDs instead of Strings for keys where possible.
- Pass the JVM flag `-XX:+UseCompressedOops` to shrink pointers from eight bytes to four.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-02" number={100} question="What is the difference between cache() and persist()?" difficulty="easy" topic="Persistence">
Both persist an RDD in memory across operations. The difference is that `persist()` lets you choose the storage level explicitly, while `cache()` always uses the default storage level, `MEMORY_ONLY`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-03" number={101} question="How will you remove data from cache in Apache Spark?" difficulty="easy" topic="Persistence">
Spark automatically evicts unused cached object

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
s using a Least Recently Used (LRU) policy, and it monitors cache usage on each node to do this. To forcibly remove an object from cache yourself, call `RDD.unpersist()`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-04" number={102} question="What are the different Persistence/Storage levels in Apache Spark?" difficulty="medium" topic="Persistence">
- **`MEMORY_ONLY`** — stores the RDD as deserialized Java objects in the JVM; if it doesn't fit in memory, it's recomputed on demand.
- **`MEMORY_AND_DISK`** — deserialized in the JVM; spills to disk if it doesn't fit in memory.
- **`MEMORY_ONLY_SER`** — stores the RDD as serialized Java objects, more space-efficient than the deserialized form.
- **`MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`** — serialized in the JVM; spills to disk if it doesn't fit in memory.
- **

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
`DISK_ONLY`** — stored only on disk.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-05" number={103} question="How will you select the storage level in Apache Spark?" difficulty="medium" topic="Persistence">
The choice is a trade-off between CPU efficiency and memory usage. If your RDD fits comfortably in memory, use `MEMORY_ONLY` for the best performance. If it doesn't fit, try `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` with a compact serialization library — still fast, with real space savings. If there's a big gap between available memory and total object size, fall back to `MEMORY_AND_DISK`. Where fast fault recovery matters, replicate objects across multiple partitions.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-06" number={104} question="What do you mean by Persistence in Spark?" difficulty="medium" topic="Pe

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
rsistence">
RDD persistence is an optimization technique that saves the result of evaluating an RDD so it can be reused, avoiding recomputation and reducing overall computation overhead. It's implemented via `cache()` and `persist()`, and is a key tool for iterative algorithms: once persisted, each node keeps in memory whatever partitions of the RDD it computed, ready for reuse — which can speed up subsequent computations by roughly 10x.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-07" number={105} question="What is Executor Memory in a Spark application, and how do you calculate it?" difficulty="hard" topic="Memory">
Every Spark application has a fixed heap size and fixed core count per executor — the heap size is the **executor memory**, controlled by the `spark.executor.memory` property

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
(or the `--executor-memory` flag), and each worker node runs one executor per application. It's effectively a measure of how much of a worker node's memory the application is allowed to use.

**Worked example** — given 10 nodes, 15 cores per node, and 61 GB RAM per node, with a rule-of-thumb target of 5 concurrent tasks per executor:

```text
Number of executors per node = cores per node / concurrent tasks = 15 / 5 = 3
Total executors for the job   = nodes × executors per node = 10 × 3 = 30
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-mem-08" number={106} question="What is spark.executor.memory in a Spark application?" difficulty="easy" topic="Memory">
`spark.executor.memory` sets the amount of memory used per executor process; its default value is 1 GB.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
### Spark Streaming

<QuestionList topic="Spark Streaming" storagePrefix="spark-stream">

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-01" number={107} question="What is Spark Streaming?" difficulty="easy" topic="Streaming">
Spark Streaming is an extension of the core Spark API for processing live data streams with high throughput and fault tolerance. It ingests data from sources like Kafka, Kinesis, and Flume, lets you apply familiar Spark functions (`map`, `reduce`, `join`, etc.) to the incoming data, and writes the processed results to a filesystem, a database, or a live dashboard.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-02" number={108} question="How does Spark Streaming work internally?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
Spark Streaming listens to live data streams from various sources

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
and, as data arrives, divides it into small batches the Spark engine can handle. Internally, this uses an abstraction called a **DStream** (discretized stream) — a continuous stream of data that is, under the hood, simply a sequence of RDDs. Spark applies the usual transformations and actions to that sequence of RDDs to produce a resulting output stream.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-03" number={109} question="What is a DStream (Discretized Stream)?" difficulty="easy" topic="Streaming">
A DStream is the high-level abstraction Spark Streaming uses to represent a continuous stream of data — internally, just a sequence of RDDs. You can create a DStream either from a source like Kafka, Flume, or Kinesis, or by applying transformations to another DStream.
</QuestionCard>

<Qu

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
estionCard id="spark-stream-04" number={110} question="What are the different types of transformations on DStreams?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
- **Stateless transformations** — processing of the current batch doesn't depend on the output of the previous batch. Examples: `map()`, `filter()`, `reduceByKey()`.
- **Stateful transformations** — processing depends on the intermediary results of the previous batch, such as transformations built on sliding windows.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-05" number={111} question="What are receivers in Apache Spark Streaming?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
Receivers consume data from external sources and hand it off to Spark for processing. They're created as long-running tasks scheduled by the streaming context in a

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
round-robin fashion across executors, with each receiver using a single core. There are two kinds:
- **Reliable receivers** — send an acknowledgment back to the data source once the data has been received and replicated in Spark's storage.
- **Unreliable receivers** — send no acknowledgment.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-06" number={112} question="What is Structured Streaming in Apache Spark?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
Introduced in Spark 2.1, Structured Streaming is a scalable, fault-tolerant stream-processing engine built directly on the Spark SQL engine. You express streaming aggregations, event-time windows, and similar logic using the Dataset/DataFrame API, and the computation runs on the same optimized Spark SQL execution engine used for batch queries.

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-07" number={113} question="What is Checkpointing in Spark Streaming?" difficulty="hard" topic="Streaming">
Checkpointing adds resiliency to a streaming application so it can recover after a failure. There are two types:
- **Metadata checkpointing** — saves the configuration and other information that defines the streaming application (configuration, DStream operations, incomplete batches), used to recover the driver after a failure.
- **Data checkpointing** — saves RDDs themselves to reliable storage; useful for stateful transformations where a generated RDD depends on the RDD from the previous batch, since that dependency chain can otherwise grow very long. Checkpointing intermediate RDDs bounds the recovery time.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionC

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
ard id="spark-stream-08" number={114} question="What is Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) / journaling in Spark Streaming?" difficulty="hard" topic="Streaming">
There are two kinds of failure in a Spark job: driver failure and worker failure. When a **worker** fails, its executor processes are killed and their scheduled tasks are automatically moved to other running worker nodes. When the **driver** fails, every associated executor is killed along with whatever data was sitting in its memory — in a streaming application, that data hasn't necessarily been persisted anywhere yet, so it can be lost for good, unlike batch jobs reading from a reliable filesystem like HDFS (where zero data loss is guaranteed since the data can always be re-read).

**Write-Ahead Logging**, introduced in Spark 1.2, solves

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
this: the intent of every operation is first written to a log file, so if the driver fails and restarts, it can replay the logged operations against the data. For streaming sources like Kafka or Flume, receivers also write the data they receive into these log files, not just into executor memory. Enable it with:
1. `streamingContext.checkpoint(path)` — sets the checkpoint directory.
2. `spark.stream.receiver.WriteAheadLog.enable = true` — enables WAL logging.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-09" number={115} question="How is caching relevant in Spark Streaming?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
Spark Streaming divides a data stream into DStream batches, and those batches can be cached in memory — very useful when the same DStream feeds multiple downstream computations

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
. Caching uses `cache()` or `persist(StorageLevel)`:

```scala
val cacheDf   = dframe.cache()
val persistDf = dframe.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY)
```

For network-based input streams (Kafka, Flume, etc.), the default persistence level replicates data to 2 nodes for fault tolerance. Caching's main advantages: cost efficiency (avoids recomputation), time efficiency, and more jobs completed per unit of time since worker nodes spend less time recomputing.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-10" number={116} question="What is the significance of the Sliding Window operation?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
Spark Streaming's windowed computations apply transformations over a sliding window of RDDs rather than a single batch. As the window slides, the RDDs that fall insid

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
e it are combined and processed together, producing new RDDs for the windowed DStream — useful for aggregations that need to look back over a recent time span rather than just the latest micro-batch.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-11" number={117} question="Explain the level of parallelism in Spark Streaming." difficulty="medium" topic="Streaming">
To reduce processing time, you generally need to increase parallelism, in one of three ways:
- **Increase the number of receivers** — if a single receiver can't read and distribute records fast enough, it becomes a bottleneck; add more receivers as the scenario demands.
- **Re-partition the received data** — if you can't add more receivers, redistribute the data you already have by re-partitioning it.
- **Increase parallelism in

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
aggregation** — raise the parallelism of the aggregation stage itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-stream-12" number={118} question="Which companies use Spark Streaming?" difficulty="easy" topic="Streaming">
Well-known adopters include Uber, Netflix, and Pinterest.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
### MLlib & GraphX

<QuestionList topic="MLlib & GraphX" storagePrefix="spark-ml">

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-01" number={119} question="What is the main use of MLlib in Apache Spark?" difficulty="easy" topic="MLlib">
MLlib is Spark's machine-learning library, delivering both speed (up to 100x faster than MapReduce for some workloads) and a broad set of algorithms usable from Java, Scala, or Python as part of a larger Spark workflow:
- **ML Algorithms** — classification, regression, clustering, collaborative filtering.
- **Featurization** — feature extraction, transformation, dimensionality reduction, and selection.
- **Pipelines** — tools for constructing, evaluating, and tuning ML pipelines.
- **Persistence** — saving and loading algorithms, models, and pipelines.
- **Utilities** — line

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
ar algebra, statistics, and data-handling helpers.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-02" number={120} question="List some commonly used Machine Learning algorithms available via MLlib." difficulty="easy" topic="MLlib">
Machine learning algorithms broadly fall into three categories: **supervised**, **unsupervised**, and **reinforcement learning**. Commonly used algorithms in MLlib and the wider Spark ML ecosystem include Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, K-Means, KNN, SVM, Random Forest, Naive Bayes, dimensionality-reduction algorithms, and gradient boosting/AdaBoost.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-03" number={121} question="How is machine learning implemented in Apache Spark?" difficulty="easy" topic="MLlib">
Through **MLlib**, Spark's scalable

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
machine-learning library, which aims to make common ML algorithms and workflows (classification, clustering, regression, dimensionality reduction, and more) both easy to use and scalable across a cluster.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-04" number={122} question="Is Apache Spark a good fit for reinforcement learning?" difficulty="medium" topic="MLlib">
Not really — Spark works best for comparatively simple machine learning algorithms like clustering, regression, and classification; reinforcement learning's sequential, stateful nature doesn't map cleanly onto Spark's execution model.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-05" number={123} question="What is a Pipeline in Apache Spark (ML)?" difficulty="medium" topic="MLlib">
A Pipeline is a machine-learning concept represent

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
ing a sequence of algorithms — one or more stages — executed in order to process and learn from data, similar to a workflow. A DataFrame is passed in as input and is progressively transformed as it moves through each stage; Spark typically performs runtime checks on the DataFrame as it flows through the pipeline. Pipelines can also be saved to disk and reloaded later.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-06" number={124} question="What is the difference between a Transformer and an Estimator in Apache Spark?" difficulty="medium" topic="MLlib">
A **Transformer** is an abstraction for a feature transformer or a learned model — it implements `transform()`, converting one DataFrame into another (typically by appending one or more new columns). An **Estimator** is an abstraction for a le

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
arning algorithm that fits/trains on data — it implements `fit()`, which takes a DataFrame as input and produces a Model (itself a Transformer).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-07" number={125} question="What are the two main types of Vector in Spark, and how do dense and sparse vectors differ?" difficulty="medium" topic="MLlib">
- **Dense Vector** — backed by a plain array of `double` values holding every entry, e.g. a vector like `(1.0, 0.0, 3.0)`.
- **Sparse Vector** — backed by two parallel arrays, one for indices and one for values, storing only the non-zero entries to save space.

```scala
val sparseVec: Vector = Vectors.sparse(5, Array(0, 4), Array(1.0, 2.0))
val denseVec: Vector  = Vectors.dense(4405d, 260100d, 400d, 5.0, 4.0, 198.0, 9070d, 1.0, 1.0, 2.0, 0.0)
```

`spa

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
rseVec` above is a size-5 vector with non-zero values only at indices 0 and 4. Sparse vectors are worthwhile when most entries are zero; used on data with only a few zero values, they actually hurt performance, since the extra index bookkeeping adds overhead without saving meaningful space. Either representation gives identical calculation results — the difference is purely about memory and speed.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-08" number={126} question="What is GraphX, and is there an API for implementing graphs in Spark?" difficulty="easy" topic="GraphX">
GraphX is Spark's API for graphs and graph-parallel computation. It extends the Spark RDD abstraction with a **Resilient Distributed Property Graph** — a directed multigraph that can have multiple parallel edges, with user-

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
defined properties attached to every vertex and edge. To support graph computation, GraphX exposes operators such as `subgraph`, `joinVertices`, and `mapReduceTriplets`, along with an optimized variant of the Pregel API, plus a growing collection of graph algorithms and builders for common analytics tasks.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-09" number={127} question="What is a Property Graph?" difficulty="medium" topic="GraphX">
A Property Graph is a directed multigraph, where you can attach an object (properties) to every vertex and edge. Because it's a multigraph, it allows multiple parallel edges sharing the same source and destination — useful for modeling more than one relationship between the same pair of vertices at once (e.g., two people who are both "boss" and "mentor" to

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
each other).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-10" number={128} question="What are the options in Spark to create a Graph?" difficulty="easy" topic="GraphX">
- **`Graph.apply`** — the simplest option; builds a graph from RDDs of vertices and edges.
- **`Graph.fromEdges`** — builds a graph from just an RDD of edges; vertices are created automatically with a default value.
- **`Graph.fromEdgeTuples`** — builds a graph from an RDD of tuples alone.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-11" number={129} question="What are the basic Graph operators in Spark GraphX?" difficulty="easy" topic="GraphX">
`numEdges`, `numVertices`, `inDegrees`, `outDegrees`, `degrees`, `vertices`, `edges`, `persist`, `cache`, `unpersistVertices`, and `partitionBy`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="s

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
park-ml-12" number={130} question="What partitioning approach does GraphX use?" difficulty="hard" topic="GraphX">
GraphX uses a **vertex-cut** approach to distributed graph partitioning: instead of splitting a graph along its edges, it partitions along vertices, which can then span multiple machines. Edges are assigned to partitions according to whichever partition strategy is selected. This reduces communication and storage overhead compared to edge-cut partitioning.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-13" number={131} question="What is Neighborhood Aggregation in Spark GraphX?" difficulty="hard" topic="GraphX">
Neighborhood Aggregation is the task of aggregating information about each vertex's local neighborhood — for example, counting how many books a given book references, or h

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
ow many times a tweet has been retweeted. It's a building block for iterative graph algorithms like PageRank and shortest-path, implemented in GraphX via the `aggregateMessages[]` and `mergeMsg[]` operations.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-14" number={132} question="What is PageRank in GraphX?" difficulty="medium" topic="GraphX">
PageRank measures the importance of each vertex in a graph, treating an edge from `u` to `v` as `u` endorsing `v`'s importance — a Twitter user followed by many others ends up ranked highly, for instance. GraphX ships both a **static** implementation (runs for a fixed number of iterations) and a **dynamic** one (runs until ranks converge within a tolerance) as methods on the PageRank object, and `GraphOps` lets you call either directly as a method on

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
a `Graph`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-15" number={133} question="What makes Apache Spark well-suited to low-latency workloads like graph processing and machine learning?" difficulty="medium" topic="MLlib">
Spark keeps data in memory, which speeds up both model building/training and graph traversal — machine learning algorithms typically need multiple iterations to converge on an optimal model, and graph algorithms need to traverse all nodes and edges, often repeatedly. Workloads that require many iterations benefit the most from Spark's in-memory model: less disk access and more controlled network traffic make a large difference once there's a meaningful amount of data to process.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="spark-ml-16" number={134} question="Is it possible to use

content:
Spark to access and analyze data stored in Cassandra databases?" difficulty="easy" topic="Ecosystem">
Yes — Spark can access and analyze data in Cassandra using the Cassandra Connector.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Walk me through what happens when I call `.count()` on a chain of `.filter()` and `.map()` calls."** Trace the lazy-evaluation story: nothing runs until `count()` (an action) is called, at which point Spark builds a DAG, the DAG Scheduler splits it into stages at shuffle boundaries, and the Task Scheduler dispatches tasks to executors — one task per partition.
- **"A join in my Spark job is taking forever and spilling to disk — how do you debug it?"** Talk through checking for data skew across partitions, whether a shuffle is unavoidable given the join keys, whether a broadcast join would work if one side is small enough, and whether `repartition`/`coalesce` are being used appropriately before the join.
- **"Design a Spark job to process a stream of clic

title: Apache Spark
description: Apache Spark interview questions covering RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL, partitioning, shuffling, memory management, streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and deployment.
content:
kstream events and maintain a running count per user."** This is a stateful-streaming question — expect to discuss DStreams vs. Structured Streaming, windowing, checkpointing for fault tolerance, and how state gets persisted across micro-batches.
- **"Why is this Spark application running out of memory?"** Walk through executor memory sizing (`spark.executor.memory`, cores per executor), storage level choice (`MEMORY_ONLY` vs. `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`), and whether the job is caching more than it needs to or shuffling more data than necessary.
- **"When would you reach for RDDs instead of DataFrames?"** A good answer touches on low-level control over partitioning, working with unstructured data that doesn't fit a schema, and cases needing fine-grained transformations the Catalyst optimizer do

title: Apache Spark

content:
esn't help with — while acknowledging that DataFrames/Datasets are the default choice for structured data in modern Spark.

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
## Looker: Business Intelligence & Exploration

**Looker is a modeling-first BI platform: instead of pulling data into its own engine, it defines reusable metrics and relationships in LookML and pushes queries down to the warehouse — which shapes almost every answer about performance, governance, and real-time capability.**

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
## Key Concepts

Looker's defining trait is that it doesn't store or move data — it's a semantic layer written in **LookML** that defines dimensions, measures, and relationships between tables, then compiles user interactions in an **Explore** into SQL run directly against the underlying warehouse. That "push-down" architecture explains most of Looker's trade-offs: it inherits the warehouse's scalability and real-time limits rather than imposing its own, relies on the warehouse (or **PDTs** — Persistent Derived Tables — materialized back into it) for performance, and depends on the warehouse's own security and partitioning features rather than reinventing them. Governance in Looker is mostly about **access control** (roles, Explore permissions, row-level filters) and **change management**

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
(LookML in Git, Development Mode for safe iteration), since the modeling logic — not the data itself — is what Looker actually owns. Most interview questions boil down to: what does Looker do itself, and what does it delegate to the connected database?

---

### Fundamentals & Core Concepts

<QuestionList topic="Looker Fundamentals" storagePrefix="looker-fund">

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-01" number={1} question="What is Looker?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Looker is a business intelligence (BI) and data visualization platform that lets users explore, analyze, and share data insights, built around a modeling layer (LookML) rather than its own data engine.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-02" number={2} question="How does Looker handle data integration?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Looker connects to various data sources using database dialects and APIs, letting it integrate with different data platforms — SQL databases, cloud storage, and data warehouses — without moving the data into Looker itself.
</Q

uestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-03" number={3} question="Can you explain the concept of a LookML model in Looker?" difficulty="medium" topic="LookML">
LookML is Looker's modeling language. It defines the relationships between data tables and creates reusable dimensions and measures. A LookML model is a collection of files describing the structure and logic of your data — the single source of truth that every Explore and dashboard is built on top of.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-04" number={4} question="What is a dimension in Looker?" difficulty="easy" topic="LookML">
A dimension represents a field or attribute in your data. It adds descriptive, qualitative context to your analysis — the counterpart to a measure, which holds the numeric values you aggregate.
</

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-05" number={5} question="How can you create a calculated field in Looker?" difficulty="medium" topic="LookML">
Calculated fields can be created either in LookML or directly in the Explore interface. LookML-based calculations support more advanced logic and are reusable across every Explore built on that model, while Explore-level calculations are quicker but scoped to a single query.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-06" number={6} question="What is a Looker Explore?" difficulty="easy" topic="Explores">
A Looker Explore is the interface that lets users interactively explore and analyze data through a graphical, point-and-click UI, built on top of the relationships and fields defined in LookML.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="look

er-fund-07" number={7} question="What is the purpose of a Looker dashboard?" difficulty="easy" topic="Dashboards">
A Looker dashboard is a customizable collection of visualizations and reports that gives a consolidated view of key metrics and data insights, typically built from one or more Explores.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-08" number={8} question="What is the purpose of a Looker filter?" difficulty="easy" topic="Explores">
A Looker filter lets users dynamically limit the data shown in a report or visualization based on specific criteria, without needing to rebuild the underlying query by hand.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-09" number={9} question="What is the difference between Looker's Explore and join types?" difficulty="medium" topic="Explores">
A

n **Explore** defines a specific, queryable view of your data — the starting point and set of fields available to a user. **Join types** determine how the multiple underlying tables referenced by that Explore are combined (e.g., inner, left outer) to produce the consolidated result set.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-10" number={10} question="Can you explain Looker's data modeling best practices?" difficulty="medium" topic="LookML">
- Use views and Explores deliberately — keep views focused on a single table/concept, and Explores focused on a specific analytical use case.
- Create reusable dimensions and measures rather than duplicating logic across models.
- Organize files and folders in LookML clearly, so models stay navigable as they grow.
- Apply consistent naming conve

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-11" number={11} question="How does Looker handle data modeling for complex data structures?" difficulty="medium" topic="LookML">
LookML's flexibility handles complex data structures by letting you explicitly define relationships, joins, and transformations across files — so even deeply nested or multi-table structures can be modeled as a coherent set of Explores.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-12" number={12} question="What are some advantages of using Looker compared to traditional BI tools?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Looker offers a more agile, self-service approach to data analysis — users can explore data and build reports without heavy reliance on IT — while

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.

LookML provides centralized, powerful modeling capabilities that keep metric definitions consistent across the organization.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-13" number={13} question="What are some limitations of Looker?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Looker is fundamentally built around batch processing, so real-time capability is limited. It also depends entirely on the underlying data infrastructure (a data warehouse or database) rather than storing data itself, which means Looker's performance and scalability are only as good as what it's connected to.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-14" number={14} question="Can Looker be integrated with other BI tools or data visualization platforms?" difficulty="medium" topic="Ecosystem">
Yes. Looker provide

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-15" number={15} question="What are some advanced analytical capabilities offered by Looker?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Advanced calculations, flexible data modeling, advanced filtering, derived tables (including PDTs), and support for custom visualizations for cases the built-in chart library doesn't cover.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-fund-16" number={16} question="Can Looker perform predictive analytics or machine learning?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Not natively — Looker itself doesn't provide built-in predictive analytics or machine lear

### LookML, PDTs & Development Workflow

<QuestionList topic="Looker LookML & PDTs" storagePrefix="looker-lookml">

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-01" number={17} question="Can you explain the concept of Looker PDTs (Persistent Derived Tables)?" difficulty="medium" topic="PDTs">
PDTs are precomputed tables that Looker builds back into the connected database to improve query performance. They're especially useful for complex or resource-intensive calculations that would otherwise be recomputed on every query.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-02" number={18} question="What is the purpose of Looker's PDT Events feature?" difficulty="medium" topic="PDTs">
PDT Events lets you schedule PDT builds at specific intervals, or trigger a rebuild based on specific events or upstream da

ta changes, rather than relying purely on a fixed refresh schedule.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-03" number={19} question="What is the purpose of Looker's PDT Performance Optimization features?" difficulty="hard" topic="PDTs">
These features let you manage PDT builds and refresh schedules more efficiently, so PDTs improve query performance without themselves becoming a source of unnecessary database load.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-04" number={20} question="What is the purpose of Looker's Liquid syntax in LookML?" difficulty="hard" topic="LookML">
Liquid syntax is used inside LookML to dynamically generate SQL, or to control the behavior of dimensions, measures, or filters based on conditions or user inputs — effectively templating logic that would

otherwise require duplicating LookML.

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-05" number={21} question="What is the purpose of Looker's Development Mode?" difficulty="medium" topic="Administration">
Development Mode lets developers change LookML models without affecting the production environment, giving them a safe space to test changes before deploying them live.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-06" number={22} question="How can you create a custom visualization in Looker?" difficulty="medium" topic="Visualization">
Looker supports custom visualizations built with custom JavaScript or HTML — you can build your own from scratch or adapt third-party visualization libraries for cases the built-in chart types don't cover.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lo

okml-07" number={23} question="What is the purpose of Looker's SQL Runner?" difficulty="medium" topic="SQL Runner">
SQL Runner provides a SQL interface directly inside Looker, letting users write and execute custom SQL queries against connected data sources without going through LookML or an Explore.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-08" number={24} question={"What is the purpose of Looker's \"Always Use SQL Runner\" option?"} difficulty="medium" topic="SQL Runner">
This option lets users bypass LookML entirely and directly write/execute custom SQL through the SQL Runner interface — useful for ad-hoc investigation, at the cost of losing LookML's reusable modeling layer for that query.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-09" number={25} question="Can you explain

Looker's dynamic time frame filters?" difficulty="medium" topic="Explores">
Dynamic time frame filters let users select relative, time-based periods — such as "last month" or "last year" — that are evaluated dynamically based on the current date, rather than a fixed date range.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-10" number={26} question="How does Looker handle data versioning and change management for LookML models?" difficulty="medium" topic="Version Control">
Looker integrates with Git (or other version control systems) so LookML models can be version-controlled like any other codebase. This lets teams track changes, compare versions, revert to a previous version of a model, and manage changes through a standard review process rather than editing production models directly.

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-11" number={27} question="What is Looker's Explore API used for?" difficulty="medium" topic="API">
The Explore API lets developers programmatically query and retrieve data from Looker Explores, enabling integration with external applications that need Looker-modeled data without going through the UI.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-lookml-12" number={28} question="What is Looker's Explore Embed feature used for?" difficulty="medium" topic="Embedding">
Explore Embed lets developers embed Looker's Explore functionality directly into external applications, giving end users a full data-exploration experience inside a product they're already using rather than a separate Looker login.

### Performance, Scale & Data Integration

<QuestionList topic="Looker Performance & Integration" storagePrefix="looker-perf">

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-01" number={30} question="How does Looker handle data caching?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Looker intelligently caches query results to improve performance, and the caching strategy can be customized based on how frequently and how much the underlying data actually changes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-02" number={31} question="What are some ways to optimize Looker performance?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
- Optimize the underlying SQL queries that LookML generates
- Use caching effectively rather than hitting the warehouse on every request
- Use PDTs strategically for expensive or frequent

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
ly reused calculations
- Reduce the number of queries a dashboard actually issues
- Organize LookML models efficiently, and tune the underlying database's own performance
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-03" number={32} question="Can Looker handle large-scale data processing?" difficulty="medium" topic="Scalability">
Yes — Looker is designed to handle large-scale data processing by leveraging the computing power and scalability of the modern data warehouse or database it's connected to, rather than processing data itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-04" number={33} question="How does Looker handle data storage and scalability?" difficulty="medium" topic="Scalability">
Looker doesn't handle data storage directly. It relies entirely on the underlying data inf

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
rastructure — a data warehouse or database — for both storage and scalability.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-05" number={34} question="How does Looker handle data partitioning and sharding?" difficulty="medium" topic="Scalability">
Looker relies on the data warehouse or database's own partitioning and sharding capabilities. LookML models can leverage whatever partitioning/sharding is configured at the database level, but Looker doesn't implement it itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-06" number={35} question="Can Looker handle incremental data loads?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Integration">
Looker relies on the underlying data infrastructure to handle incremental data loads — proper configuration and modeling on the warehouse side is required to su

title: Looker
description: Looker interview questions covering LookML modeling, Explores, PDTs, caching and performance, governance, and data lineage for BI and analytics roles.
content:
pport incremental updates cleanly.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-07" number={36} question="Can Looker handle real-time data?" difficulty="medium" topic="Real-Time">
Looker primarily works with batch data processing and isn't designed for true real-time data analysis. You can, however, set up frequent scheduled data refreshes to get near real-time insights.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-08" number={37} question="Can Looker handle near real-time data analysis?" difficulty="medium" topic="Real-Time">
Looker can approach near real-time analysis by setting up frequent data refresh schedules, or by combining it with streaming data sources and appropriate data modeling techniques.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-09" number={38} question="Can Looker

handle data visualization in real-time dashboards?" difficulty="medium" topic="Real-Time">
Looker is primarily designed for batch data processing, so it may not be well suited to strictly real-time dashboards. With appropriate data modeling and frequent refresh schedules, though, it can deliver near real-time insights.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="looker-perf-10" number={39} question="Can Looker handle streaming data sources?" difficulty="medium" topic="Real-Time">
Looker is primarily designed for batch data processing, but it can leverage streaming data sources through appropriate integrations and data modeling techniques rather than consuming a stream natively.

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
## Superset: Open-Source BI & Visualization

**Apache Superset is an open-source, Python-built BI platform that pairs a SQL-first exploration workflow (SQL Lab) with a drag-and-drop dashboard builder, connecting to almost any SQLAlchemy-compatible database without licensing cost.**

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
## Key Concepts

Superset is built on Python and Flask, and stores its own **metadata** (dashboards, charts, connections, users) in a metadata database that's separate from the databases it queries. Every visualization starts as a **slice** — a single chart tied to a dataset — and slices are composed into **dashboards**. Two workflows feed slices: the drag-and-drop **Explore** view for point-and-click chart building, and **SQL Lab**, a full SQL IDE for ad-hoc querying, complete with query history and a saved-query workflow. Because Superset connects via SQLAlchemy, it can talk to most relational databases and several NoSQL/OLAP engines with SQL-like interfaces — including **Druid**, historically the go-to backend for fast, real-time-ish analytics on top of Superset. Security is role-based

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
(RBAC), extendable to **row-level security** for restricting which rows a given user's queries can return. As with most BI tools, performance is largely inherited from the underlying database, with Superset's own caching layer sitting on top to avoid re-running identical queries.

---

### Fundamentals, Architecture & Connectivity

<QuestionList topic="Superset Fundamentals" storagePrefix="superset-fund">

<QuestionCard id="superset-fund-01" number={1} question="What is Apache Superset?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Apache Superset is an open-source business intelligence (BI) and data visualization tool that lets users explore and analyze large datasets through interactive visualizations, dashboards, and SQL-based queries.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-fund-02" number={2} question="How does Superset differ from other BI tools?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Superset stands out for its intuitive interface, extensive customization options, and ability to handle large datasets. It supports many databases, offers a wide range of visualiza

Superset is primarily built using Python (on Flask), with a React/TypeScript frontend.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-fund-04" number={4} question="What are the key components of Superset?" difficulty="medium" topic="Architecture">
The web server, the database backend for the data being visualized, the metadata database that stores Superset's own dashboards/charts/connections, and the visualization engine that renders charts.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-dash-01" number={30} question="What is a slice in Superset?" difficulty="easy" topic="Visualization">
A slice is a visual representation of a dataset in Superset — a chart or table built from a single query against a dataset.

<QuestionCard id="superset-dash-02" number={31} question="How can you create a new slice in Superset?" difficulty="easy" topic="Visualization">

### Performance, Caching & Scale

<QuestionList topic="Superset Performance" storagePrefix="superset-perf">

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-01" number={49} question="What are some ways to optimize query performance in Superset?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Use appropriate indexes on the underlying database tables, aggregate data at the database level rather than in Superset, cache query results, and tune Superset's own configuration settings (like query timeouts and row limits).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-02" number={50} question="How does Superset handle large datasets?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Superset leverages the power of the underlying database systems to handle large datasets — it uses efficient SQL queries and implements paginati

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
on and caching to keep performance reasonable rather than pulling everything into the app itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-03" number={51} question="What is Superset's data caching mechanism?" difficulty="medium" topic="Caching">
Superset caches query results to improve performance and reduce load on the underlying databases — subsequent identical requests are served from the cache instead of re-executing the query.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-04" number={52} question="What is Superset's approach to data caching and cache invalidation?" difficulty="medium" topic="Caching">
Query results are cached based on the underlying database and query parameters. Cache invalidation is handled either through cache timeout settings, or by manually clearing th

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
e cache when the underlying data is known to have changed.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-05" number={53} question="How does Superset handle data caching for queries with dynamic parameters?" difficulty="medium" topic="Caching">
For queries with dynamic parameters, Superset includes the parameter values as part of the cache key — this ensures different query instances with different parameter values don't collide or get mixed up in the cache.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-06" number={54} question="How can you monitor the performance of Superset?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Using monitoring agents, log analyzers, and performance tracking tools — analyzing server logs, watching resource usage, and tracking query performance to identify and r

title: Superset
description: Apache Superset interview questions covering SQL Lab, dashboards, database connectivity, caching and performance, and security for BI and analytics roles.
content:
esolve bottlenecks.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-07" number={55} question="Can you deploy Superset in a distributed environment?" difficulty="medium" topic="Deployment">
Yes — Superset can be deployed in a distributed environment using tools like Kubernetes or Docker Swarm to manage containerized instances across multiple nodes or machines.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-08" number={56} question="Does Superset support multi-tenancy?" difficulty="medium" topic="Deployment">
Yes — Superset supports multi-tenancy by letting you configure and manage multiple instances or workspaces within a single deployment, each with its own users, databases, and resources.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-perf-09" number={57} question="What are Superset's ale

rting capabilities?" difficulty="medium" topic="Alerting">
Superset has limited built-in alerting. You can leverage external tools, or integrate Superset with alerting systems, to set up notifications based on predefined metrics or thresholds.
</QuestionCard>

### Security, Governance & Lineage

<QuestionList topic="Superset Security & Governance" storagePrefix="superset-sec">

<QuestionCard id="superset-sec-01" number={58} question="How can you secure Superset?" difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
Enable authentication and authorization mechanisms such as LDAP, OAuth, or database-backed authentication, and configure role-based access control (RBAC) to control what each user can see and do.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-sec-02" number={59} question="How does Superset handle data security and access control?" difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
Superset provides access control through role-based permissions — you define roles and assign them specific permissions governing who can access and modify datasets, slices, dashboards, a

title: Superset

content:
nd other resources.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-sec-03" number={60} question="What is Superset's support for row-level security (RLS)?" difficulty="hard" topic="Security">
Superset supports RLS by letting you define filters or query conditions based on a user's role or attributes, restricting the rows a given query can return based on who's running it.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="superset-sec-04" number={61} question="What is Superset's support for data permissions and data masking?" difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
Superset enforces data permissions through RBAC, letting you grant or restrict access to specific datasets or columns. Data masking itself, though, needs to be implemented at the database layer rather than within Superset.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionC

</QuestionCard>

Superset can capture and display data lineage, letting users trace the source and transformations behind the data used in dashboards and visualizations. It also supports governance through access controls and data security measures.

## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"A dashboard mixes data from Postgres and a data lake table — how would you build that in Superset?"** Walk through registering both connections, building separate datasets, and either joining/unioning them via a federated query engine or combining them at the dashboard level with multiple slices.
- **"SQL Lab queries are timing out on a large table — what do you check?"** Cover query timeout configuration, whether the underlying table is properly indexed/partitioned, row limits, and whether caching or a materialized aggregate table would help.
- **"How would you make sure a sales rep only sees their own region's data on a shared dashboard?"** Explain row-level security (RLS) rules tied to user attributes, and how that differs from just hiding a column vi

content:
a RBAC.
- **"The team wants near-real-time dashboards on top of clickstream data — is Superset a fit?"** Discuss pairing Superset with Druid or a similarly fast OLAP store, versus the limits of connecting it directly to a raw Kafka stream.

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
## Tableau: Enterprise BI & Visualization

**Tableau is a drag-and-drop business intelligence platform built around fast, associative in-memory querying — the interview bar is less about syntax and more about knowing when to use a calculated field vs. an LOD expression, a join vs. a blend, and a live connection vs. an extract.**

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
## Key Concepts

Tableau organizes every data source into **measures** (numeric values to aggregate) and **dimensions** (qualitative attributes to slice by), and every field as **continuous** (plotted on an axis) or **discrete** (plotted as a header). Getting data in happens two ways: a **live connection** queries the source directly for up-to-date results, while an **extract** snapshots the data into Tableau's own fast columnar engine (`.hyper`/`.tde`) for speed and offline use. Combining multiple tables is either a **join** (same data source) or a **blend** (across data sources, via a common field). On top of that foundation sit three tiers of calculation — basic calculated fields, **Level of Detail (LOD)** expressions for granularity that doesn't match the view, and **table calculations

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
** that operate only on what's already rendered — plus **sets**, **groups**, and **parameters** for reshaping how data is filtered and categorized. Interviewers use these building blocks to probe whether you understand *why* a number in a viz looks wrong, not just how to build the viz.

---

content: ## Interview Questions

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
### Fundamentals & Product Landscape

<QuestionList topic="Tableau Fundamentals" storagePrefix="tableau-fund">

<QuestionCard id="tableau-fund-01" number={1} question="What is data visualization in Tableau?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Data visualization is a way to represent data that's visually appealing and interactive. As business intelligence tools have matured, they've made it easier for users to understand data, data sets, and data points through charts and graphs — letting people focus on the impact of the data rather than the mechanics of the tool itself.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="tableau-fund-02" number={2} question="What is the difference between traditional BI tools and Tableau?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
- Traditional BI tools have hardware

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
-driven architecture limitations; Tableau has no such dependency.
- Traditional BI tools rely on complex underlying technology; Tableau uses a simple associative search model that makes exploration dynamic.
- Traditional BI tools generally don't support multi-thread, in-memory, or multi-core computing; Tableau supports all three.
- Traditional BI tools present a pre-defined data view; Tableau supports more predictive, exploratory analysis.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="tableau-fund-03" number={3} question="What are the different Tableau products?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- **Tableau Desktop** — the authoring tool. Connects to data from various sources into its data engine and builds interactive dashboards from optimized, code-free queries.
- **Tableau Server** — shares

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
dashboards published from Desktop across an organization. An enterprise feature installed on Windows or Linux.
- **Tableau Reader** — a free desktop app for opening and viewing visualizations. You can filter or drill down but can't edit formulas or take actions on the data.
- **Tableau Online** — a paid, hosted service (no installation required) for sharing published dashboards anywhere.
- **Tableau Public** — a free product for publishing visualizations as worksheets/workbooks on a public Tableau Server.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="tableau-fund-04" number={4} question="What are shelves in Tableau?" difficulty="easy" topic="Workspace">
Tableau worksheets contain named areas — Columns, Rows, Marks, Filters, Pages, and more — called shelves. You place fields on shelves to build visua

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
lizations, add levels of detail, or bring extra context into the view.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="tableau-fund-05" number={5} question="Why would someone choose not to use Tableau?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
- **Cost** — Tableau isn't the cheapest data visualization tool once you factor in software upgrades, deployment, maintenance, and training.
- **Security gaps** — Tableau lacks centralized, data-level security; it leans on row-level security and per-user accounts instead, which increases exposure.
- **Limited BI depth** — Tableau isn't built for large-scale reporting, static layouts, or building data tables. Result-sharing is limited, email notifications are admin-only, and there's no support for trigger-based notifications.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ta

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
bleau-fund-06" number={6} question="What is the DRIVE program methodology?" difficulty="medium" topic="Methodology">
DRIVE is a structured, iterative methodology for rolling out data analytics across an enterprise deployment. It borrows from agile practices to make adoption faster and more effective than a big-bang rollout.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="tableau-fund-07" number={7} question="What is the difference between Tableau and similar tools like QlikView or IBM Cognos?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Tableau is an intuitive visualization tool that simplifies storytelling through drag-and-drop. QlikView and Cognos instead convert data into metadata so users can explore data relationships more deeply. If your priority is presenting data in aesthetically strong visualiza

title: Tableau
description: Tableau interview questions covering data visualization, calculated fields, LOD expressions, joins vs. blending, dashboards, and server administration.
content:
tions, Tableau tends to win; if you need a full BI platform with deeper relationship exploration, Cognos or QlikView may fit better. Tableau is also generally easier to hand to non-technical users — a salesperson can read a Tableau view and draw insight, whereas Cognos tends to reward users with deeper tool expertise.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>
