content:
### Tables, Keys & Capacity Model

<QuestionList topic="DynamoDB Data Model" storagePrefix="dynamodb-data-model">

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-01" number={1} question="What is DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
DynamoDB is a fully managed, serverless NoSQL database service provided by AWS that handles large-scale, high-performance distributed workloads.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-02" number={2} question="What are the key characteristics of DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Serverless, fully managed, highly scalable, low latency, built-in replication across availability zones, automatic backup, and pay-per-request pricing.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-03" number={3} question="What is the difference between DynamoDB and relation

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
al databases?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
DynamoDB is NoSQL — flexible schema, horizontal scaling by design — while relational databases use a fixed schema and traditionally scale vertically. DynamoDB also requires you to design around known access patterns upfront, rather than querying flexibly with joins after the fact.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-04" number={4} question="What is a DynamoDB table?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
A DynamoDB table is a collection of items (records) with attributes (columns), identified by a unique primary key.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-05" number={5} question="What is a partition key in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Keys">
The partition key (hash key) determines which physical partition stores a

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
n item, based on a hash of the key's value. It's the primary lever for how evenly data and traffic spread across DynamoDB's underlying storage.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-06" number={6} question="What is a sort key in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Keys">
The sort key (range key) orders items within a partition, allowing range queries (e.g., "all orders for this customer between two dates") on items that share the same partition key.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-07" number={7} question="What is the difference between partition key and sort key?" difficulty="easy" topic="Keys">
The partition key distributes data across partitions; the sort key orders data within a single partition. Together they form a composite key that supports both distribution and ri

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
ch per-partition querying.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-08" number={8} question="What is a composite key in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Keys">
A composite key combines a partition key and a sort key, enabling efficient queries on both dimensions — items are distributed by partition key, then queryable and orderable by sort key within that partition.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-09" number={9} question="What is throughput in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Capacity">
Throughput measures a table's read/write capacity — how many read and write operations per second the table can handle, expressed in read and write capacity units.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-10" number={10} question="What is a read capacity unit in DynamoDB?" difficul

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
ty="medium" topic="Capacity">
One RCU (read capacity unit) equals one strongly consistent read per second, or two eventually consistent reads per second, for items up to 4KB. Larger items consume proportionally more RCUs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-11" number={11} question="What is a write capacity unit in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Capacity">
One WCU (write capacity unit) equals one write per second for items up to 1KB. Larger items consume proportionally more WCUs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-12" number={12} question="What is on-demand billing in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Capacity">
A pay-per-request pricing model: you pay for actual reads/writes performed, without provisioning capacity upfront — well suited to unpredictable or spiky

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
traffic.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-13" number={13} question="What is provisioned billing in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Capacity">
You reserve read/write capacity upfront and pay a fixed rate for it. Better for predictable, steady workloads where you can forecast throughput and want lower, stable costs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-14" number={14} question="How do you handle hot partitions in DynamoDB?" difficulty="hard" topic="Performance">
Use better partition key design (higher-cardinality, evenly-distributed keys, possibly with a random or calculated suffix to spread "hot" items), implement DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) as an in-memory cache to absorb read pressure, or switch to on-demand billing so the table can adapt to spikes automatically.
</Qu

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
estionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-core-15" number={15} question="What is auto scaling in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Capacity">
Auto scaling automatically adjusts provisioned capacity up or down based on actual traffic patterns, within limits you configure — it keeps provisioned mode responsive to changing load without manual intervention.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
### Indexes, Queries & Advanced Features

<QuestionList topic="DynamoDB Queries" storagePrefix="dynamodb-queries">

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-01" number={16} question="What are secondary indexes in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Indexes">
Secondary indexes let you query on non-key attributes, providing flexible access patterns beyond what the table's primary key alone supports — DynamoDB offers two kinds: Local Secondary Indexes and Global Secondary Indexes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-02" number={17} question="What is a Global Secondary Index?" difficulty="medium" topic="Indexes">
A GSI lets you query on a different partition key (and optional sort key) than the base table, across the entire table, with its own provisioned throughput independent of the base table

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
's capacity.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-03" number={18} question="What is a Local Secondary Index?" difficulty="medium" topic="Indexes">
An LSI lets you query using an alternate sort key while keeping the same partition key as the base table, sharing that partition's throughput. It's limited to 10GB of data per partition key value.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-04" number={19} question="What is eventual consistency in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Consistency">
Eventually consistent reads return the most recent write in milliseconds in the common case, but might briefly return stale data if a read hits a replica that hasn't yet received the latest write. It's the default and cheaper read type.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-05" number=

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
{20} question="What is strong consistency in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Consistency">
Strongly consistent reads always return the most recent successful write, but consume twice the read capacity of an eventually consistent read and aren't supported on Global Secondary Indexes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-06" number={21} question="How do you query DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Querying">
Use the `Query` API to retrieve items matching a specific partition key value, with optional sort key conditions to narrow the range — this is the efficient, key-driven way to read from DynamoDB.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-07" number={22} question="What is a scan operation in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Querying">
`Scan` reads every item in a

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
table (or index), which is useful for full-table operations but inefficient and expensive at scale since it consumes capacity for every item read, not just matches.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-08" number={23} question="What is the difference between query and scan?" difficulty="medium" topic="Querying">
`Query` uses key conditions to target a specific partition (efficient); `Scan` reads every item in the table or index (inefficient but flexible when you don't know the key upfront).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-09" number={24} question="What is projection in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Querying">
Projection specifies which attributes to return in query or scan results, reducing data transfer and, for indexes, controlling which attributes are copied i

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
nto the index in the first place.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-10" number={25} question="What is filtering in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Querying">
A filter expression applies conditions to items *after* DynamoDB retrieves them (post key-condition evaluation), reducing the result set size — but it doesn't reduce the read capacity consumed, since filtering happens after the read.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-11" number={26} question="How do you handle transactions in DynamoDB?" difficulty="hard" topic="Transactions">
Use `TransactWriteItems` for multiple atomic writes across one or more tables, or `TransactGetItems` for atomic reads — both succeed or fail as a unit, giving you ACID guarantees for the specific items involved.
</QuestionCard>

<Quest

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
ionCard id="ddb-query-12" number={27} question="What is batch processing in DynamoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Batch Operations">
`BatchGetItem` reads multiple items (potentially across multiple tables) in one request, and `BatchWriteItem` writes or deletes multiple items in one request — both are more efficient than issuing many individual requests, though neither is atomic across items the way transactions are.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-13" number={28} question="What is TTL in DynamoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="TTL">
TTL (Time To Live) automatically deletes items after a specified expiration timestamp attribute is reached, useful for managing data expiration (session data, temporary caches) without writing cleanup jobs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
14" number={29} question="What is DynamoDB Streams?" difficulty="medium" topic="Streams">
Streams capture item-level modifications (inserts, updates, deletes) as an ordered, time-limited log, enabling real-time processing with AWS Lambda or other consumers — commonly used for change data capture, replication, or triggering downstream workflows.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="ddb-query-15" number={30} question="How do you design DynamoDB tables?" difficulty="hard" topic="Data Modeling">
Plan access patterns upfront (this is the step relational modeling doesn't force you to do), choose partition keys that distribute traffic evenly, use secondary indexes for alternate access patterns you didn't design the base table's key around, and optimize the overall data model — including denormalizi

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
ng and using single-table design where appropriate — for the specific queries the application needs.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a DynamoDB table for an e-commerce order history feature."** Walk through identifying access patterns first (get all orders for a customer, get a specific order by ID), then choosing `customer_id` as the partition key and `order_date#order_id` as a composite sort key to support both patterns, and where a GSI would be needed for a third pattern like "look up an order by ID alone."
- **"One partition is getting throttled while the rest of the table is idle — what's happening and how do you fix it?"** This is a hot partition. Diagnose via CloudWatch partition-level metrics, then discuss redesigning the partition key for higher cardinality, adding a random/calculated suffix to spread a naturally hot key, or introducing DAX as a caching layer.
- **"Whe

title: DynamoDB
description: Interview questions covering DynamoDB tables, partition and sort keys, capacity modes, secondary indexes, consistency, and access-pattern-first query design.
content:
n would you reach for a GSI versus an LSI?"** Cover that LSIs must be created at table creation time and share the base table's partition and throughput, while GSIs can be added later, use their own partition key, and have independent capacity — making GSIs the more common choice for genuinely new access patterns.
- **"How would you keep a search index or analytics store in sync with a DynamoDB table in near-real time?"** Talk through DynamoDB Streams feeding a Lambda function that writes to OpenSearch, Kinesis, or a downstream data pipeline.

---

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What are the differences between RDBMS and HBase data model?
The main differences between RDBMS and HBase data model are as follows:
Schema: In RDBMS, there is a schema of tables, columns etc. In HBASE, there is no schema.
Normalization: RDBMS data model is normalized as per the rule of Relation DB normalization.
HBase data model does not require any normalization.
Partition: In RDBMS we can choose to do partitioning. In HBase, partitioning happens automatically.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain what is Hbase?
Hbase is a column-oriented database management system which runs on top of HDFS (Hadoop Distribute File System). Hbase is not a relational data store, and it does not support structured query language like SQL.
In Hbase, a master node regulates the cluster and region servers to store portions of the tables and operates the work on the data.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain why to use Hbase?
High capacity storage system
Distributed design to cater large tables
Column-Oriented Stores
Horizontally Scalable
High performance & Availability
Base goal of Hbase is millions of columns, thousands of versions and billions of rows
Unlike HDFS (Hadoop Distribute File System), it supports random real time CRUD operations

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

Zookeeper: It does the co-ordination work between client and Hbase Maser
Hbase Master: Hbase Master monitors the Region Server
RegionServer: RegionServer monitors the Region
Region: It contains in memory data store(MemStore) and Hfile.
Catalog Tables: Catalog tables consist of ROOT and META

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain what is WAL and Hlog in Hbase?
WAL (Write Ahead Log) is similar to MySQL BIN log; it records all the changes occur in data. It is a standard sequence file by Hadoop and it stores HLogkey’s.  These keys consist of a sequential number as well as actual data and are used to replay not yet persisted data after a server crash. So, in cash of server failure WAL work as a life-line and retrieves the lost data’s.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## When you should use Hbase?
Data size is huge: When you have tons and millions of records to operate
Complete Redesign: When you are moving RDBMS to Hbase, you consider it as a complete re-design then mere just changing the ports
SQL-Less commands: You have several features like transactions; inner joins, typed columns, etc.
Infrastructure Investment: You need to have enough cluster for Hbase to be really useful

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## In Hbase what is column families?
Column families comprise the basic unit of physical storage in Hbase to which features like compressions are applied.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain what is the row key?
Row key is defined by the application. As the combined key is pre-fixed by the rowkey, it enables the application to define the desired sort order. It also allows logical grouping of cells and make sure that all cells with the same rowkey are co-located on the same server.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain deletion in Hbase? Mention what are the three types of tombstone markers in Hbase?
When you delete the cell in Hbase, the data is not actually deleted but a tombstone marker is set, making the deleted cells invisible.  Hbase deleted are actually removed during compactions.
Three types of tombstone markers are there:
Version delete marker: For deletion, it marks a single version of a column
Column delete marker: For deletion, it marks all the versions of a column
Family delete marker: For deletion, it marks of all column for a column family

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain how does Hbase actually delete a row?
In Hbase, whatever you write will be stored from RAM to disk, these disk writes are immutable barring compaction. During deletion process in Hbase, major compaction process delete marker while minor compactions don’t. In normal deletes, it results in a delete tombstone marker- these delete data they represent are removed during compaction.
Also, if you delete data and add more data, but with an earlier timestamp than the tombstone timestamp, further Gets may be masked by the delete/tombstone marker and hence you will not receive the inserted value until after the major compaction.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain what happens if you alter the block size of a column family on an already occupied database?
When you alter the block size of the column family, the new data occupies the new block size while the old data remains within the old block size. During data compaction, old data will take the new block size.  New files as they are flushed, have a new block size whereas existing data will continue to be read correctly. All data should be transformed to the new block size, after the next major compaction.

## Define the difference between Hive and HBase?
Apache Hive is a data warehousing infrastructure built on top of Hadoop. It helps in querying data stored in HDFS for analysis using Hive Query Language (HQL), which is a SQL-like language, that gets translated into MapReduce jobs. Hive performs batch processing on Hadoop.
Apache HBase is NoSQL key/value store which runs on top of HDFS. Unlike Hive, HBase operations run in real-time on its database rather than MapReduce jobs. HBase partitions the tables, and the tables are further splitted into column families.
Hive and HBase are two different Hadoop based technologies – Hive is an SQL-like engine that runs MapReduce jobs, and HBase is a NoSQL key/value database of Hadoop. We can use them together. Hive can be used for analytical queries whi

le HBase for real-time querying. Data can even be read and written from HBase to Hive and vice-versa.

Any access to HBase tables uses this Primary Key.
Each column qualifier present in HBase denotes attributes corresponding to the object which resides in the cell.

## What are the data manipulation commands of HBase?
Data Manipulation commands of HBase are:
put – Puts a cell value at a specified column in a specified row in a particular table.
get – Fetches the contents of a row or a cell.
delete – Deletes a cell value in a table.
deleteall – Deletes all the cells in a given row.
scan – Scans and returns the table data.
count – Counts and returns the number of rows in a table.
truncate – Disables, drops, and recreates a specified table.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What happens when you issue a delete command in HBase?
Once you issue a delete command in HBase for cell, column or column family, it is not deleted instantly. A tombstone marker in inserted. Tombstone is a specified data, which is stored along with standard data. This tombstone makes hides all the deleted data.
The actual data is deleted at the time of major compaction. In Major compaction, HBase merges and recommits the smaller HFiles of a region to a new HFile. In this process, the same column families are placed together in the new HFile. It drops deleted and expired cell in this process. All the results from scan and get filters the deleted cells.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What are different tombstone markers in HBase?
There are three types of tombstone markers in HBase:
Version Marker: Marks only one version of a column for deletion.
Column Marker: Marks the whole column (i.e. all version) for deletion.
Family Marker: Marks the whole column family (i.e. all the columns in the column family) for deletion

The ZooKeeper is used to maintain the configuration information and communication between region servers and clients. It also provides distributed synchronization. It helps in maintaining server state inside the cluster by communicating through sessions.
Every Region Server along with HMaster Server sends continuous heartbeat at regular interval to Zookeeper and it checks which server is alive and available. It also provides server failure notifications so that, recovery measures can be executed.

## Explain what is Wal and Hlog in Hbase?
WAL (Write Ahead Log) is similar to MySQL BIN log; it records all the changes occur in data. It is a standard sequence file by Hadoop and it stores HLogkey’s. These keys consist of a sequential number as well as actual data and are used to replay not yet persisted data after a server crash. So, in cash of server failure WAL work as a life-line and retrieves the lost data’s.

Column families comprise the basic unit of physical storage in Hbase to which features like compressions are applied.

content:
## Name three disadvantages Hbase has as compared to Rdbms?
Hbase does not have in-built authentication/permission mechanism
The indexes can be created only on a key column, but in RDBMS it can be done in any column.
With one HMaster node there is a single point of failure.

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain what is the Row Key?
Row key is defined by the application. As the combined key is pre-fixed by the rowkey, it enables the application to define the desired sort order. It also allows logical grouping of cells and make sure that all cells with the same rowkey are co-located on the same server.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Is Hbase a scale out or scale up process?
Hbase runs on top of Hadoop which is a distributed system. Haddop can only scale uo as and when required by adding more machines on the fly. So Hbase is a scale out process.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain deletion in Hbase?
When you delete the cell in Hbase, the data is not actually deleted but a tombstone marker is set, making the deleted cells invisible. Hbase deleted are actually removed during compactions.
Three types of tombstone markers are there:
+ Version delete marker: For deletion, it marks a single version of a column
+ Column delete marker: For deletion, it marks all the versions of a column
+ Family delete marker: For deletion, it marks of all column for a column family

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What are the step in writing something into Hbase by a Client?
In Hbase the client does not write directly into the HFile. The client first writes to WAL(Write Access Log), which then is accessed by Memdtore. The Memstore Flushes the data into permanent memory from time to time.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain how does Hbase actually delete a Row?
In Hbase, whatever you write will be stored from RAM to disk, these disk writes are immutable barring compaction. During deletion process in Hbase, major compaction process delete marker while minor compactions don’t. In normal deletes, it results in a delete tombstone marker- these delete data they represent are removed during compaction.
Also, if you delete data and add more data, but with an earlier timestamp than the tombstone timestamp, further Gets may be masked by the delete/tombstone marker and hence you will not receive the inserted value until after the major compaction.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is compaction in Hbase?
As more and more data is written to Hbase, many HFiles get created. Compaction is the process of merging these HFiles to one file and after the merged file is created successfully, discard the old file.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Explain what happens if you alter the Block Size of a column Family on an already occupied Database?
When you alter the block size of the column family, the new data occupies the new block size while the old data remains within the old block size. During data compaction, old data will take the new block size. New files as they are flushed, have a new block size whereas existing data will continue to be read correctly. All data should be transformed to the new block size, after the next major compaction.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What are the different compaction types in Hbase?
There are two types of compaction. Major and Minor compaction. In minor compaction, the adjacent small HFiles are merged to create a single HFile without removing the deleted HFiles. Files to be merged are chosen randomly.
In Major compaction, all the HFiles of a column are emerged and a single HFiles is created. The delted HFiles are discarded and it is generally triggered manually.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is the Scope of a Rowkey in Hbase?
Rowkeys are scoped to ColumnFamilies. The same rowkey could exist in each ColumnFamily that exists in a table without collision.

description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:

This class is used to store information about a column family such as the number of versions, compression settings, etc. It is used as input when creating a table or adding a column.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is a Namespace in Hbase?
A Namespace is a logical grouping of tables . It is similar to a database object in a Relational database system.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is the Lower Bound of Versions in Hbase?
The lower bound of versions indicates the minimum number of versions to be stored in Hbase for a column. For example If the value is set to 3 then three latest version wil be maintained and the older ones will be removed.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is Hotspotting in Hbase?
Hotspotting is a situation when a large amount of client traffic is directed at one node, or only a few nodes, of a cluster. This traffic may represent reads, writes, or other operations. This traffic overwhelms the single machine responsible for hosting that region, causing performance degradation and potentially leading to region unavailability.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is Ttl in Hbase?
TTL is a data retention technique using which the version of a cell can be preserved till a specific time period.Once that timestamp is reached the specific version will be removed.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Why do we Pre create empty Regions?
Tables in HBase are initially created with one region by default. Then for bulk imports, all clients will write to the same region until it is large enough to split and become distributed across the cluster. So empty regions are created to make this process faster.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Does Hbase support Table Joins?
Hbase does not support table joins. But using a mapreduce job we can specify join queries to retrieve data from multiple Hbase tables.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Which File in Hbase is designed after Sstable File of Bigtable?
The HFile in Habse which stores the Actual data(not metadata) is designed after the SSTable file of BigTable.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is a Hbase Store?
Hbase Store hosts a MemStore and 0 or more StoreFiles (HFiles). A Store corresponds to a column family for a table for a given region.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What are the Two Types of Table Design Approach in Hbase?
They are:
Short and Wide
Tall and Thin

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## When do we do Manual Region Splitting?
The manual region splitting is done we have an unexpected hotspot in your table because of many clients querying the same table.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## In which scenario should we consider creating a short and wide Hbase Table?
The short and wide table design is considered when there is
There is a small number of columns
There is a large number of rows

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## In Hbase what is Log Splitting?
When a region is edited, the edits in the WAL file which belong to that region need to be replayed. Therefore, edits in the WAL file must be grouped by region so that particular sets can be replayed to regenerate the data in a particular region. The process of grouping the WAL edits by region is called log splitting.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## How does Hbase support Bulk Data Loading?
There are two main steps to do a data bulk load in Hbase:
Generate Hbase data file(StoreFile) using a custom mapreduce job) from the data source. The StoreFile is created in Hbase internal format which can be efficiently loaded.
The prepared file is imported using another tool like comletebulkload to import data into a running cluster. Each file gets loaded to one specific region.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## Why Multiwal is Needed?
With a single WAL per RegionServer, the RegionServer must write to the WAL serially, because HDFS files must be sequential. This causes the WAL to be a performance bottleneck.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## How does Hbase provide High Availability?
Hbase uses a feature called region replication. In this feature for each region of a table, there will be multiple replicas that are opened in different RegionServers. The Load Balancer ensures that the region replicas are not co-hosted in the same region servers.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## How does Wal help when a Regionserver Crashes?
The Write Ahead Log (WAL) records all changes to data in HBase, to file-based storage. if a RegionServer crashes or becomes unavailable before the MemStore is flushed, the WAL ensures that the changes to the data can be replayed.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What is Hregionserver in Hbase?
HRegionServer is the RegionServer implementation. It is responsible for serving and managing regions. In a distributed cluster, a RegionServer runs on a DataNode.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

title: Hbase
description: Interview questions covering hbase
content:
## What are the different Block Caches in Hbase?
HBase provides two different BlockCache implementations: the default on-heap LruBlockCache and the BucketCache, which is (usually) off-heap.

[Table of Contents](#Hbase)

content:
## Databases: Operational & NoSQL Stores

This section covers operational databases and NoSQL stores used in data engineering.

title: Databases
description: NoSQL and operational databases (MongoDB, Cassandra, HBase, DynamoDB, BigTable)
content:
## Database Technologies

- **MongoDB**: Document-based NoSQL database
- **Cassandra**: Distributed wide-column database
- **HBase**: Column-oriented storage built on Hadoop
- **DynamoDB**: AWS managed NoSQL service
- **BigTable**: Google's distributed column store

These databases are optimized for transactional workloads, real-time applications, and operational systems.

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
## MongoDB: Document Database at Scale

**MongoDB is a document database that trades the rigid, normalized schema of RDBMS for flexible, JSON-like documents — a design that shapes everything from how you model data to how the database scales, replicates, and stays consistent.**

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
## Key Concepts

MongoDB stores records as **documents** (compressed BSON, retrievable as JSON) grouped into **collections** — the rough analogs of RDBMS rows and tables, but with a dynamic schema: fields can vary document-to-document, and related data is often **embedded** in a single document rather than joined across tables. Scaling happens two ways: **replica sets** (a primary plus secondaries) provide high availability and read scaling through replication and automatic failover, while **sharding** distributes collections horizontally across multiple replica sets using a shard key, coordinated by `mongos` query routers and config servers. Consistency is tunable rather than fixed — **write concern** and **read preference** let you trade strict consistency for availability/latency, which

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
is why MongoDB is often described as CP-by-default but AP-capable. Around this core sit an **aggregation pipeline** for multi-stage data processing, **indexes** (chosen with the ESR rule: Equality, Sort, Range) for query performance, and multi-document **transactions** for the cases where single-document atomicity isn't enough.
---

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content: ## Interview Questions

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
### Document Model & Core Concepts

<QuestionList topic="MongoDB Fundamentals" storagePrefix="mongodb-fundamentals">

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-01" number={1} question="What is MongoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
MongoDB is a document database built on a horizontal scale-out architecture that uses a flexible schema for storing data. Founded in 2007, it has a large developer community and is one of the most widely used NoSQL databases.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-02" number={2} question="Why use MongoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
As a document database, MongoDB makes it easy to store structured or unstructured data using a JSON-like format. That format directly maps to native objects in most modern programming languages, so developers don't

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
have to think as much about normalizing data. MongoDB can also handle high volume, scaling both vertically and horizontally to accommodate large data loads.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-03" number={3} question="What are the advantages of MongoDB?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- A powerful, document-oriented database
- Strong developer experience (documents map naturally to application objects)
- Scalability and transactionality (horizontal scaling plus multi-document transactions)
- Platform and ecosystem maturity (drivers, tooling, Atlas)
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-04" number={4} question="When should you use MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
- Integrating large amounts of diverse data
- Describing complex data structures th

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
at evolve over time
- Delivering data in high-performance applications
- Supporting hybrid and multi-cloud applications
- Supporting agile development and collaboration, where the schema isn't fully known upfront
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-05" number={5} question="How does MongoDB store data?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Model">
Records are stored as documents in compressed BSON files. The documents can be retrieved directly in JSON format, which has several benefits:

- It's a natural, human-readable form to store data
- Structured and unstructured information can live in the same document
- You can nest JSON to store complex data objects
- JSON has a flexible, dynamic schema, so adding or omitting a field isn't a problem
- Documents map to objects in most popular pro

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
gramming languages

Most developers find JSON easy to work with because it's a simple and powerful way to describe and store data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-06" number={6} question="MongoDB vs. RDBMS: what are the differences?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
The main difference is that RDBMS is relational while MongoDB is non-relational. Most RDBMS systems use SQL to manage stored data, while MongoDB uses BSON. RDBMS uses tables and rows; MongoDB uses collections and documents — a table (equivalent to a MongoDB collection) stores data as columns and rows, a row (equivalent to a document) stores structured data items, and a column (equivalent to a field) denotes a set of data values.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-07" number={7} question="What is

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
an aggregation pipeline in MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Aggregation">
An aggregation pipeline consists of one or more stages that process documents:

- Each stage performs an operation on the input documents — for example, filtering, grouping, or calculating values
- The documents output from one stage are passed to the next
- A pipeline can return results for groups of documents — for example, the total, average, maximum, and minimum values per group
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-08" number={8} question="How does MongoDB manage schema design and changes?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema Design">
MongoDB uses a flexible, document-based model for schema design:

- **Schemaless** — collections don't enforce document structure by default
- **Dynamic schema** — docume

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
nts in the same collection can have different fields
- **Schema validation** — optional rules can be set to enforce document structure
- **Indexing** — supports various index types to optimize query performance

For schema changes: add fields by simply writing documents with new fields, remove fields by deleting them or using `$unset` in updates, change field types by rewriting documents with new data types, and handle large-scale changes programmatically or with tools like Mongoose for Node.js.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-09" number={9} question="What are the limitations or disadvantages of MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Trade-offs">
- Limited JOIN functionality compared to relational databases
- Document size limit of 16MB
- Lack of built-in data integrity constra

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
ints (like foreign key constraints)
- Higher storage space requirements due to data duplication in denormalized models
- Steeper learning curve for those accustomed to relational databases
- Less mature ecosystem compared to some traditional RDBMSs
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-10" number={10} question="How do you perform migrations from RDBMS to MongoDB?" difficulty="hard" topic="Migration">
Migrating from RDBMS to MongoDB typically involves:

1. Analyzing the relational schema and designing a document model
2. Mapping relational tables to MongoDB collections
3. Converting relational data to JSON format
4. Using tools like `mongoimport` or custom scripts to import data
5. Verifying data integrity after migration
6. Updating application code to work with MongoDB instead of

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
SQL
7. Testing thoroughly to ensure functionality and performance

Helpful tools include MongoDB Compass for visualizing and manipulating data, official MongoDB connectors for various languages, and third-party ETL tools.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-11" number={11} question="What are the best practices for designing a MongoDB schema?" difficulty="hard" topic="Schema Design">
- Embed related data in a single document when possible for faster reads
- Use references when data is used in multiple places or for very large datasets
- Design schema based on application query patterns, not on normalization habits
- Avoid deeply nested documents (keep nesting to 2-3 levels for better performance)
- Use appropriate data types for fields
- Create indexes to support common queries
-

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
Consider document growth when designing schemas
- Use schema versioning for easier updates and migrations
- Normalize data only when necessary (e.g., for consistency across multiple collections)
- Use array fields for one-to-many relationships within reasonable limits
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-core-12" number={12} question="How does MongoDB handle geographic data and geospatial queries?" difficulty="hard" topic="Geospatial">
MongoDB provides robust support for geographic data and geospatial queries:

- **Geospatial indexes** — `2d` and `2dsphere` indexes for efficient geospatial queries
- **GeoJSON objects** — store location data using the standard GeoJSON format
- **Geospatial queries** — proximity (find documents near a point), intersection (geometries that intersect a giv

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
en geometry), and within (geometries contained inside a given geometry)
- **Aggregation pipeline** — geospatial stages like `$geoNear` for complex geo-based analytics
- **Coordinate systems** — support for both legacy coordinate pairs and GeoJSON objects
- **Geospatial operators** — `$near`, `$geoWithin`, `$geoIntersects`, etc., for flexible querying
- **Spherical geometry** — the `2dsphere` index supports queries on an Earth-like sphere
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
### Scaling, Performance & Operations

<QuestionList topic="MongoDB Operations" storagePrefix="mongodb-operations">

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-01" number={13} question="How does MongoDB scale?" difficulty="easy" topic="Scaling">
MongoDB scales the same way any distributed application would: throw more resources at a single machine (**vertical scaling**) or distribute the data across multiple physical servers or nodes (**horizontal scaling**).

- **Vertical scaling** — increasing the capacity of a single server (more CPU, RAM, or storage)
- **Horizontal scaling** — dividing the dataset and load across multiple servers, adding more as needed

While a single machine's overall speed or capacity may not be high, each machine handles a subset of the overall workload, which can be more efficien

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
t than one very large, very fast server.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-02" number={14} question="How does MongoDB scale horizontally?" difficulty="medium" topic="Sharding">
MongoDB supports horizontal scaling through sharding — a method for distributing data across multiple machines, used for deployments with very large datasets and high throughput. A sharded cluster consists of:

- **Shard** — each shard holds a subset of the sharded data, and each shard is itself deployed as a replica set
- **`mongos`** — a query router that provides the interface between client applications and the sharded cluster, and can support hedged reads to minimize latency
- **Config servers** — store metadata and configuration settings for the cluster, and (since MongoDB 3.4) must themselves be de

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
ployed as a replica set (CSRS)

MongoDB shards data at the collection level, distributing a collection's data across the shards in the cluster.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-03" number={15} question="What are the advantages of sharding?" difficulty="medium" topic="Sharding">
- **Reads/writes** — MongoDB distributes read and write workload across shards, letting each shard process a subset of operations; both can be scaled horizontally by adding more shards. Queries that include the shard key (or a prefix of a compound shard key) can be targeted at a specific shard instead of broadcast to all of them, which is far more efficient. `mongos` can also use hedged reads to minimize latency.
- **Storage capacity** — sharding distributes data across shards so each holds a subset of t

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
he total dataset; adding shards increases total cluster capacity as data grows.
- **High availability** — because config servers and shards are deployed as replica sets, the cluster stays available even if one or more shard replica sets become unavailable: reads/writes to the unavailable shard fail, but the rest of the cluster keeps serving.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-04" number={16} question="What methods can be used for sharding in MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Sharding">
MongoDB supports two sharding strategies:

- **Hashed sharding** — computes a hash of the shard key field's value; each chunk is assigned a range based on the hashed values, which spreads writes evenly but sacrifices efficient range queries.
- **Ranged sharding** — divides data into ranges based

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
on the shard key values directly; each chunk is assigned a range based on those values, which supports efficient range queries but risks uneven ("hot") distribution if the key isn't chosen carefully.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-05" number={17} question="How do atomicity and transactions work in MongoDB?" difficulty="hard" topic="Transactions">
A write operation is atomic at the level of a single document, even if it modifies multiple embedded documents within that one document. When a single write operation (e.g., `updateMany()`) modifies multiple documents, the modification of each document is atomic, but the operation as a whole is not. For situations that require atomicity of reads and writes across multiple documents (in a single collection or across collections), Mon

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
goDB supports distributed transactions, including on replica sets and sharded clusters.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-06" number={18} question="Where should you use an index in MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Indexing">
If your application repeatedly runs queries on the same fields, create an index on those fields to improve performance. Indexes improve query performance but have a negative impact on write operations — for collections with a high write-to-read ratio, indexes are expensive because every insert must also update each index.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-07" number={19} question="How would you choose an indexing strategy in MongoDB, and what should you consider?" difficulty="hard" topic="Indexing">
The best indexes for an application depend o

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
n the kinds of queries you expect, the ratio of reads to writes, and available memory. The best overall strategy is to profile a variety of index configurations against data sets similar to production to see which performs best, and to periodically inspect existing indexes to confirm they still support current and planned queries — dropping any that are no longer used. Key considerations:

- **Use the ESR (Equality, Sort, Range) rule** — a guide to ordering compound index fields so they support queries efficiently
- **Create indexes to support your queries** — an index supports a query when it contains all the fields the query scans
- **Use indexes to sort query results** — order and direction of index fields matters for supporting efficient sorts
- **Ensure indexes fit in RAM** — when an

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
index fits in memory, MongoDB avoids reading it from disk, which is much faster
- **Create indexes for query selectivity** — selectivity is an index's ability to narrow results; higher selectivity lets MongoDB use the index for more of the work
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-08" number={20} question="How does MongoDB ensure data consistency and reliability?" difficulty="medium" topic="Reliability">
- **Write concern** — lets you specify the level of acknowledgment required from MongoDB for write operations
- **Replica sets** — provide data redundancy and high availability
- **Journaling** — ensures data durability by writing operations to a journal before applying them to the data files
- **Data validation** — lets you enforce document structure
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
id="mongo-ops-09" number={21} question="What are MongoDB's backup and restore options?" difficulty="medium" topic="Backup">
- **`mongodump` / `mongorestore`** — command-line tools for creating binary exports of database contents and restoring them
- **Filesystem snapshots** — point-in-time snapshots of the underlying data
- **MongoDB Atlas Backup** — continuous backups and point-in-time recovery for cloud-hosted databases
- **MongoDB Ops Manager** — for on-premises deployments, offering backup automation and point-in-time recovery
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-10" number={22} question="How does MongoDB handle concurrency?" difficulty="medium" topic="Concurrency">
- **Document-level locking** — allows multiple clients to read and write different documents simultaneously
- **

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
WiredTiger storage engine** — provides document-level concurrency control and compression
- **Multi-version concurrency control (MVCC)** — lets readers see a consistent view of data without blocking writers
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-11" number={23} question="What security features does MongoDB offer?" difficulty="medium" topic="Security">
- **Authentication** — supports various mechanisms (SCRAM, x.509 certificates, LDAP, Kerberos)
- **Authorization** — role-based access control (RBAC) for fine-grained access control
- **Encryption** — supports encryption at rest and in transit (TLS/SSL)
- **Auditing** — tracks system and user activity
- **Network isolation** — supports IP allow-listing and VPC peering
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-12" number={24} question

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
="What is MongoDB Atlas and what benefits does it provide?" difficulty="easy" topic="Atlas">
MongoDB Atlas is the cloud-hosted database-as-a-service (DBaaS) platform for MongoDB. Benefits include:

- Automated deployment and scaling of MongoDB clusters
- Multi-cloud and multi-region deployment options
- Built-in security features (network isolation, encryption, access controls)
- Automated backups and point-in-time recovery
- Performance monitoring and optimization tools
- Easy integration with other cloud services
- Reduced operational overhead for database management
- Automatic updates and patches for the database software
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-13" number={25} question="How can you monitor and optimize MongoDB performance?" difficulty="medium" topic="Monitoring">

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
**Monitoring:**

- Use MongoDB's built-in tools like `mongotop` and `mongostat`
- Leverage MongoDB Compass for visual performance insights
- Use MongoDB Atlas monitoring for cloud deployments
- Use third-party monitoring tools like Prometheus with a MongoDB exporter

**Optimization:**

- Analyze and optimize slow queries using the `explain()` method
- Create appropriate indexes based on query patterns
- Use proper schema design to minimize data duplication
- Configure appropriate write concern and read preferences
- Optimize server and storage configuration
- Use appropriate shard keys for distributed systems
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-14" number={26} question="What is a replica set in MongoDB and how does it work?" difficulty="medium" topic="Replication">
A replica set i

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
s a group of `mongod` processes that maintain the same data set. It provides:

- **High availability** — if the primary node fails, an election chooses a new primary from the secondary nodes
- **Data redundancy** — data is replicated across multiple nodes
- **Read scaling** — secondary nodes can handle read operations

How it works: one primary node accepts all write operations, multiple secondary nodes replicate data from the primary, optional arbiter nodes participate in elections but don't hold data, and automatic failover occurs if the primary becomes unavailable.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-15" number={27} question="How do you handle data integrity in MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Integrity">
MongoDB doesn't have built-in referential integrity like relatio

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
nal databases, but you can maintain data integrity through:

- **Schema validation** — define a JSON Schema to enforce document structure
- **Application-level checks** — implement integrity checks in application code
- **Atomic operations** — use multi-document transactions for operations that must succeed or fail as a unit
- **Unique indexes** — ensure uniqueness of certain fields
- **Data validation** — use the `$jsonSchema` operator to define validation rules
- **Consistent data modeling** — design schemas to minimize the need for complex integrity constraints
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-16" number={28} question="How does MongoDB fit into the CAP theorem?" difficulty="hard" topic="Consistency">
The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can only fully provide two

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
of three guarantees: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. MongoDB is designed to handle network partitions (Partition Tolerance), and lets you tune the balance between Consistency and Availability:

- With default settings, MongoDB leans toward **CP** (Consistency and Partition Tolerance)
- By adjusting write concern and read preferences, you can shift toward **AP** (Availability and Partition Tolerance)

In practice: use majority write concern and read preference for strong consistency, or lower write concerns / reads from secondaries for higher availability at the cost of potential staleness.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="mongo-ops-17" number={29} question="How do you handle large data volumes in MongoDB?" difficulty="medium" topic="Scaling">
- **Sharding** — distrib

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
ute data across multiple machines to handle large datasets and high throughput
- **Indexing** — create appropriate indexes to improve query performance on large collections
- **Aggregation pipeline** — efficient data processing and analysis on large datasets
- **GridFS** — store and retrieve large files efficiently
- **Data compression** — the WiredTiger storage engine compresses data to reduce storage requirements
- **Capped collections** — for high-volume data that doesn't require long-term storage
- **Time series collections** — optimized storage and querying for time series data
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a schema for an e-commerce order system in MongoDB."** Talk through when to embed (order line items inside the order document) versus reference (a customer document referenced by ID), grounded in query patterns and document growth rather than normalization habits.
- **"A collection's queries are slow despite having what looks like the right indexes."** Walk through `explain()`, the ESR rule for compound index field order, whether the working index set fits in RAM, and whether write concern/read preference settings are adding unexpected latency.
- **"How would you shard a multi-tenant collection that's grown to billions of documents?"** Discuss shard key selection (avoiding hot shards), hashed vs. ranged sharding trade-offs, and how `mongos` target

title: MongoDB
description: Interview questions covering MongoDB's document model, sharding, replica sets, indexing strategy, transactions, and schema design for data engineers.
content:
s queries that do or don't include the shard key.
- **"Explain how MongoDB behaves during a network partition, and how a client would configure around it."** Use this to walk through the CAP theorem trade-off, replica set elections/failover, and how write concern and read preference tune consistency vs. availability.

---

import QuestionCard from '../../../../components/mdx/QuestionCard.astro';
import QuestionList from '../../../../components/mdx/QuestionList.astro';

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
## Apache Avro: Row-Based Data Serialization

**Apache Avro is a data serialization and RPC framework from the Hadoop ecosystem that pairs a JSON-defined schema with a compact binary encoding — making it a common choice for streaming pipelines (like Kafka) that need compact messages and strong schema evolution.**

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
## Key Concepts

Avro stores every record alongside a **JSON schema**, but the data itself is written in a **compact binary format**, which keeps files small and serialization fast. Because the schema always travels with (or is resolvable alongside) the data, Avro supports strong **schema resolution** — a reader can use a different, compatible schema version than the writer used, which is the foundation of its **schema evolution** story. Avro defines a fixed set of **primitive types** (null, boolean, int, long, float, double, bytes, string) and **complex types** (record, enum, array, map, union, fixed). Unlike Thrift or Protocol Buffers, Avro supports both dynamic and static typing without requiring an Interface Definition Language (IDL) or manually-assigned field IDs, and it's used for tw

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
o main purposes: **data serialization** for storage (compact, splittable, and compressible) and **RPC** (remote procedure calls) between systems.

---

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Avro" storagePrefix="avro">

<QuestionCard id="avro-01" number={1} question="What is Apache Avro?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Apache Avro is an open-source project that offers data serialization as well as data exchange services for Apache Hadoop. These services can be used together or independently. Programs can efficiently serialize data into files or messages using the serialization service. Data storage is very compact and efficient in Avro because the data definition is written in JSON while the data itself is stored in binary format, making it compact and efficient.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-02" number={2} question="What are some key points about Apache Avro?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- Avro is a d

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
ata serialization system.
- It uses JSON-based schemas.
- To send data, it uses RPC calls.
- During data exchange, the schema is sent along with the data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-03" number={3} question="What does Avro offer?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
- Rich data structures.
- A compact, fast, binary data format.
- A container file for storing persistent data.
- Remote procedure calls (RPC).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-04" number={4} question="Who is the intended audience for learning Avro?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
People who want to learn the basics of big data analytics using the Hadoop framework, and those who aspire to become a Hadoop developer, are good candidates to learn Avro. Aspirants who want to use Avro specifically for

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
data serialization and deserialization can also learn it.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-05" number={5} question="What are the prerequisites to learn Avro?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Before learning Avro, you should know Hadoop's architecture and APIs, and have experience with Java, since writing basic applications typically requires it before going for Avro.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-06" number={6} question="Explain Avro schemas." difficulty="medium" topic="Schemas">
Avro heavily depends on its schema — it permits data to be written without prior knowledge of the schema by the reader. Avro serialization is fast, and the data resulting from serialization is small in size because the schema travels with (or is resolvable against) the data.
</QuestionCard

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
>

<QuestionCard id="avro-07" number={7} question="How does Avro compare to Thrift and Protocol Buffers?" difficulty="medium" topic="Comparison">
The most comparable libraries to Avro are Thrift and Protocol Buffers. The key differences:

- As needed, Avro supports both dynamic and static types, while Protocol Buffers and Thrift use Interface Definition Languages (IDLs) to specify schemas and their types.
- Avro is built into the Hadoop ecosystem, whereas Thrift and Protocol Buffers are not.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-08" number={8} question="Why Avro?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
Some features where Avro differs from other systems:

- Dynamic typing.
- Untagged data.
- No manually-assigned field IDs.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-09" number={9} questio

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
n="How do you use Avro?" difficulty="medium" topic="Workflow">
The typical workflow: first create schemas, then read those schemas into your program — which is possible in two ways, either by generating a class corresponding to the schema or by using the parser library directly. Then perform serialization using the Avro serialization API, and deserialization using the Avro deserialization API.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-10" number={10} question="What primitive data types does Avro support?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Types">
Avro supports a range of primitive data types:

- `null` — no value
- `boolean` — a binary value
- `int` — 32-bit signed integer
- `long` — 64-bit signed integer
- `float` — single precision (32-bit) IEEE 754 floating-point number
- `double` — double pr

description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
ecision (64-bit) IEEE 754 floating-point number
- `bytes` — a sequence of 8-bit unsigned bytes
- `string` — a Unicode character sequence
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-11" number={11} question="What complex data types does Avro support?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Types">
Avro supports six kinds of complex types:

- Records
- Enums
- Arrays
- Maps
- Unions
- Fixed
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-12" number={12} question="What are the best features of Apache Avro?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
- Schema evolution
- Untagged data
- Language support
- Transparent compression
- Dynamic typing
- Native support in MapReduce
- Rich data structures
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-13" number={13} question="What are the advantages of Avro?" difficulty="medium"

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
topic="Trade-offs">
- Smallest serialized size among comparable formats.
- Compresses one block at a time; the resulting file is splittable.
- Maintains object structure.
- Supports reading old data with a new schema.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-14" number={14} question="What are the disadvantages of Avro?" difficulty="medium" topic="Trade-offs">
- The C# implementation requires .NET 4.5 to make the best use of it.
- Serialization can be potentially slower compared to some alternatives.
- A schema is required in order to read or write data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-15" number={15} question="What do you mean by schema declaration?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schemas">
In JSON, a schema is represented by one of:

- A JSON string
- A JSON object, e.g. `{"type": "ty

pename", ...attributes...}`
- A JSON array
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-16" number={16} question="Explain the term serialization." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Serialization is the process of translating data structures or object state into binary or textual form, in order to transport the data over a network or store it on persistent storage. Serialization is also known as marshaling, and deserialization is known as unmarshalling.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-17" number={17} question="What do you mean by schema resolution?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schemas">
A reader of Avro data — whether from an RPC call or a file — can always parse that data since its schema is available alongside it. But the reader's expected schema may not be exactly the schema the

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
data was written with, so Avro uses schema resolution to reconcile the two.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-18" number={18} question="Explain the Avro SASL profile." difficulty="hard" topic="Security">
SASL (Simple Authentication and Security Layer) offers a framework for authentication and security of network protocols. Avro also uses a SASL profile for authentication and security purposes, particularly around its RPC mechanism.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-19" number={19} question="What are the ways of creating Avro schemas?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schemas">
Avro schemas are created in the "lightweight text-based data interchange" format, JSON (JavaScript Object Notation). They can be created in several ways:

- As a JSON string
- As a JSON object
- As a JSON array

</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-20" number={20} question="Name some Avro reference APIs." difficulty="medium" topic="APIs">
Classes and methods commonly used in the serialization and deserialization of Avro schemas:

- `SpecificDatumWriter`
- `SpecificDatumReader`
- `DataFileWriter`
- `DataFileReader`
- `Schema.Parser`
- `GenericRecord` (interface)
- `GenericData.Record`
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-21" number={21} question="When should you use Avro?" difficulty="medium" topic="Use Cases">
Avro is mainly used for two purposes:

- Data serialization
- RPC (remote procedure call) protocol

Some key points: Avro lets applications written in languages other than Java or the JVM read data from disk. It also lets you transfer data across a remote system without the overhead o

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
f Java serialization. Use Avro when you need to store a large set of data on disk, since it conserves space, and use it for RPC when you want more efficient remote data transfer.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-22" number={22} question="Explain sort order in Avro." difficulty="hard" topic="Fundamentals">
Avro defines a standard sort order for data, which allows data written by one system to be efficiently sorted by another system. Since sort order comparisons are often the most frequent per-object operation, this can be an important performance optimization.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-23" number={23} question="What is the advantage of Hadoop's Writable serialization over Java serialization?" difficulty="medium" topic="Comparison">
With the help of `Writable` objects, H

title: Avro
description: Apache Avro interview questions covering JSON-based schemas, serialization, schema resolution, data types, and RPC use cases in the Hadoop ecosystem.
content:
adoop's Writable-based serialization reduces object-creation overhead, which isn't possible with Java's native serialization framework — which is why using Hadoop's approach is advantageous for performance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="avro-24" number={24} question="What are the disadvantages of Hadoop's Writable serialization?" difficulty="medium" topic="Comparison">
The main disadvantage is that `Writable` and Sequence Files only have a Java API. Avro was created in part to solve this problem, since it supports multiple languages.
</QuestionCard>

## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Why would you pick Avro over Parquet for a Kafka pipeline?"** Talk through Avro being row-oriented (good for record-at-a-time streaming and write-heavy workloads), its compact binary encoding, and how the schema travels with the data (or lives in a schema registry) to support producer/consumer evolution.
- **"A downstream consumer needs to read data written with an older schema version — how does that work?"** Explain schema resolution: the reader schema and writer schema don't need to be identical, only compatible, so Avro can reconcile added/removed/renamed fields at read time.
- **"How would you evolve a schema without breaking existing consumers?"** Discuss safe changes (adding a field with a default, for instance) versus breaking ones, and how that
