title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
## Delta Lake: Reliable Data Storage

**Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that brings ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel to Parquet-based data lakes — turning a folder of files into something that behaves like a table.**

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
## Key Concepts

Delta Lake sits on top of **Parquet** and adds a **transaction log** (`_delta_log/`) that records every change to a table, which is what makes **ACID transactions**, **time travel**, and reliable **schema enforcement/evolution** possible. Writers use **optimistic concurrency control** against that log, so multiple concurrent writers can commit safely, with conflicts detected and retried rather than silently corrupting data. Table maintenance revolves around `OPTIMIZE` (compacting small files and applying **Z-order clustering**) and `VACUUM` (removing old, unreferenced files) — both important because frequent writes or streaming ingestion tend to produce many small files. Delta also supports row-level `MERGE`/`DELETE`/`UPDATE`, **Change Data Capture (CDC)** for downstream c

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
onsumers, and performance features like **data skipping** built on file-level statistics.

---

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Delta Lake" storagePrefix="delta">

<QuestionCard id="delta-01" number={1} question="What is Delta Lake?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that brings ACID transactions, data versioning, and unified batch/streaming processing to data lakes built on Apache Spark.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-02" number={2} question="What are the key features of Delta Lake?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Key features include ACID transactions, schema enforcement and evolution, time travel, unified batch/streaming, data lineage, and high performance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-03" number={3} question="What is the Delta Lake transaction log?" difficulty="medium" topic="Architecture

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
">
The transaction log (`_delta_log/`) records all transactions on a Delta table, enabling ACID guarantees, time travel, and data recovery.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-04" number={4} question="How does Delta Lake provide ACID transactions?" difficulty="medium" topic="ACID">
Delta Lake uses the transaction log together with optimistic concurrency control to ensure Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-05" number={5} question="What is schema enforcement in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema">
Schema enforcement prevents accidental data corruption by rejecting writes that don't match the table's schema.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-06" number={6} question="What is schema evolution in Delta Lake?" diffic

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
ulty="medium" topic="Schema">
Schema evolution allows adding new columns to a Delta table without rewriting the entire dataset.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-07" number={7} question="What is time travel in Delta Lake?" difficulty="easy" topic="Time Travel">
Time travel allows querying previous versions of a table by specifying a version number or a timestamp.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-08" number={8} question="How do you query historical data in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Time Travel">
Use:

```sql
SELECT * FROM table VERSION AS OF version_number
SELECT * FROM table TIMESTAMP AS OF timestamp_string
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-09" number={9} question="What is the VACUUM command in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Maintenance">
`V

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
ACUUM` removes old versions of files from Delta tables, reducing storage costs while still preserving recent history.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-10" number={10} question="What is the OPTIMIZE command in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Maintenance">
`OPTIMIZE` consolidates small files into larger ones and can apply Z-order clustering, improving query performance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-11" number={11} question="What is Z-order clustering in Delta Lake?" difficulty="hard" topic="Performance">
Z-order clustering reorganizes data on disk to maximize data-skipping efficiency, which can dramatically improve query performance for queries that filter on the clustered columns.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-12" number={12} question="How does Delta L

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
ake handle data lineage?" difficulty="medium" topic="Governance">
Delta Lake tracks lineage through the transaction log, providing a complete audit trail of data changes over time.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-13" number={13} question="What is Change Data Capture in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="CDC">
Change Data Capture (CDC) captures INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on a table, allowing downstream systems to react to data changes incrementally.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-14" number={14} question="How do you enable CDC in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="CDC">
Enable CDC when creating (or altering) a table with:

```sql
TBLPROPERTIES ("delta.enableChangeDataFeed" = true)
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-15" number={15} question="

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
What is Delta Lake's compatibility with Parquet?" difficulty="easy" topic="Architecture">
Delta Lake uses Parquet as its underlying data format, while adding a transaction log layer on top for reliability, ACID guarantees, and performance features.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-16" number={16} question="Can you use Delta Lake with Spark?" difficulty="easy" topic="Ecosystem">
Yes, Spark is the primary engine for Delta Lake. Use `spark.read.format("delta")` to read and `df.write.format("delta")` to write.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-17" number={17} question="Can you use Delta Lake with other tools?" difficulty="easy" topic="Ecosystem">
Yes, Delta Lake works with Databricks, Apache Spark, Presto, Trino, and many other query engines.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="d

elta-18" number={18} question="How do you create a Delta Lake table?" difficulty="easy" topic="Basics">
Use `df.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").save(path)`, or the SQL form `CREATE TABLE ... USING DELTA`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-19" number={19} question="What is the difference between managed and external Delta tables?" difficulty="medium" topic="Table Types">
Managed tables store their data inside the warehouse's managed directory; external tables reference data at an external storage location that the warehouse doesn't own.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-20" number={20} question="How do you handle concurrent writes in Delta Lake?" difficulty="hard" topic="Concurrency">
Delta Lake uses optimistic concurrency control, allowing multiple writers to operate

description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
against the same table with automatic conflict detection and resolution at commit time.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-21" number={21} question="What is the MERGE command in Delta Lake?" difficulty="hard" topic="DML">
`MERGE` performs INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations in a single statement based on join conditions, similar to the SQL `MERGE` statement — commonly used for upserts.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-22" number={22} question="How do you delete records in Delta Lake?" difficulty="easy" topic="DML">
Use:

```sql
DELETE FROM table WHERE condition
```
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-23" number={23} question="What is data skipping in Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Data skipping uses file-level statistics to skip data files that d

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
on't match query predicates, improving performance without scanning the entire table.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-24" number={24} question="How does Delta Lake improve query performance?" difficulty="medium" topic="Performance">
Through data skipping, Z-order clustering, predicate pushdown, and an optimized file layout maintained via `OPTIMIZE`.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-25" number={25} question="What is the Delta Lake protocol?" difficulty="hard" topic="Architecture">
The Delta protocol specifies the transaction log format, ensuring different engines and implementations remain compatible when reading and writing the same tables.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-26" number={26} question="How do you upgrade an existing table to Delta Lake?" difficulty="me

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
dium" topic="Migration">
Convert existing tables using `CONVERT TO DELTA table_name`, or recreate the table in Delta format by rewriting the data.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-27" number={27} question="Can you downgrade from Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Migration">
Downgrading is complex and not a first-class operation. A better approach is usually to keep a separate non-Delta table or maintain a dedicated export process.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-28" number={28} question="What are the storage requirements for Delta Lake?" difficulty="easy" topic="Infrastructure">
Delta Lake requires cloud object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS, etc.) plus the transaction log directory (`_delta_log`) alongside the data files.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-29" number=

title: Delta Lake
description: Delta Lake interview questions covering ACID transactions, the transaction log, time travel, schema evolution, MERGE, and performance optimization.
content:
{29} question="How do you handle Delta Lake compaction?" difficulty="medium" topic="Maintenance">
Run the `OPTIMIZE` command periodically to compact small files and improve query performance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="delta-30" number={30} question="What is the impact of frequent writes on Delta Lake?" difficulty="medium" topic="Maintenance">
Frequent writes create many small files and many transaction log entries, which degrades query planning and performance over time — requiring periodic `OPTIMIZE` and `VACUUM` to keep the table healthy.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Delta Lake

content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"How does Delta Lake guarantee ACID transactions on top of object storage, which has no native transactions?"** Explain the role of the `_delta_log` transaction log and optimistic concurrency control — writers propose new log entries and retry on conflict, rather than relying on storage-level locking.
- **"A Delta table has thousands of small files after months of streaming writes — how do you fix it?"** Talk through `OPTIMIZE` for compaction (and Z-order clustering on frequently filtered columns), followed by `VACUUM` to reclaim space, and how you'd schedule that maintenance.
- **"How would you implement an upsert pipeline into a Delta table?"** Walk through `MERGE INTO` with match/no-match conditions, and how CDC (`delta.enableChangeDataFeed`) could let

downstream consumers react to just the changed rows instead of rescanning the table.

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What is Apache Hudi?
Apache Hudi is an open-source data lakehouse framework that brings database-like capabilities (upserts, deletes, incremental pulls) to data lakes on object storage. It manages table metadata and timelines so batch and streaming engines can write and read consistently.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What problems does Hudi solve in a data lake?
Hudi enables efficient updates and deletes without rewriting entire datasets, supports incremental processing (read only changes), and helps manage small files and write amplification. It is commonly used for near-real-time ingestion and CDC-style workloads where tables must be kept up-to-date.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What is the difference between Copy-on-Write (COW) and Merge-on-Read (MOR)?
COW stores data in columnar files and rewrites them on updates, optimizing read performance at the cost of write amplification. MOR stores base files plus delta log files and merges them at read time (or during compaction), optimizing write performance at the cost of more complex reads.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## How do you choose between COW and MOR?
Choose COW when read latency and query simplicity are most important and updates are moderate. Choose MOR when write throughput and near-real-time updates are critical, and you can manage compaction and read-time merges. The choice also depends on engine support and operational constraints.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What is a record key, partition path, and precombine field?
Record key identifies a row uniquely (like a primary key). Partition path controls how data is grouped for storage and pruning. Precombine field is used to resolve multiple records for the same key in a batch (for example choose the latest by `updated_at`) to ensure deterministic upserts.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## How does Hudi support upserts?
Hudi indexes records by key and can locate existing records to update them. On upsert, it writes new versions and updates the timeline/metadata so readers see a consistent view. Correctness depends on stable keys and proper handling of late and duplicate events.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What is compaction in Hudi?
Compaction merges delta log files into base columnar files (mostly relevant for MOR tables). It improves read performance and reduces the overhead of reading many log files. Compaction must be scheduled and monitored to avoid backlog.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What is clustering in Hudi and when do you need it?
Clustering reorganizes data files to improve layout (file sizes, sorting, locality) and query performance. You use clustering to combat small files, improve pruning, or optimize for common access patterns (for example sorting by event time).

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## Why does the small files problem happen and how do you mitigate it in Hudi?
It happens when writers commit frequently with small batches or when partitions are highly granular. Mitigations include tuning write parallelism and file sizing, using clustering/compaction, and adjusting partitioning strategy to reduce tiny partitions.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## How do you handle CDC with Hudi?
Hudi can ingest CDC events and apply them as upserts/deletes to maintain a current-state table. You typically standardize event ordering and deduplication (record key + precombine field) and monitor lag/compaction. Some setups also keep an append-only log for auditing alongside the current-state table.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## What are common operational metrics for Hudi tables?
Common metrics include commit/compaction backlog, file counts per partition, average file size, write latency, failed commits, and query scan metrics. Operational alerts often focus on increasing small files and compaction lag.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Hudi
description: Interview questions covering hudi
content:
## When would you choose Hudi vs Iceberg vs Delta?
Hudi is often chosen for CDC-heavy and upsert/delete workloads with near-real-time ingestion requirements. Iceberg is often chosen for engine-agnostic open tables with strong metadata and evolution features. Delta is often chosen when you are aligned with the Delta ecosystem and its tooling. The best choice depends on engines, governance, and operational capabilities.

[Table of Contents](#Apache-Hudi)

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
## Apache Iceberg: Open Table Format

**Apache Iceberg is an open table format for large analytics datasets that adds transactional guarantees, consistent snapshots, and safe schema/partition evolution on top of files like Parquet — so multiple engines can read and write the same tables safely.**

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)

- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
## Key Concepts

Iceberg tracks table state through **snapshots** — each commit produces a new snapshot referencing a set of data and delete files via manifest lists/manifests, which is what enables consistent reads and **time travel**. Writers use **optimistic concurrency control**, retrying commits if the base snapshot has moved. Two design choices set Iceberg apart from a plain folder of Parquet files: **hidden partitioning** (partitioning is metadata-driven, e.g. `day(ts)`, not baked into folder paths) and field-ID-based **schema evolution**, both of which allow **partition evolution** and schema changes without rewriting historical data. Deletes are handled via **equality** and **positional delete files** rather than rewriting whole data files, and ongoing **table maintenance** — comp

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
action, manifest rewriting, snapshot expiration, orphan file removal — keeps metadata and the small-files problem under control. A **catalog** (Hive metastore, REST catalog, AWS Glue, etc.) tracks where each table's metadata lives.

---

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Iceberg" storagePrefix="iceberg">

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-01" number={1} question="What is Apache Iceberg?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Apache Iceberg is an open table format for large analytics datasets. It defines how table metadata, snapshots, and data files are managed so multiple engines can read and write the same tables safely. Iceberg tables are usually stored on object storage (S3/GCS/ADLS) with files like Parquet, but the table semantics — transactions, schema/partition evolution — come from Iceberg's own metadata layer.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-02" number={2} question="What problems does Iceberg solve compared to plain Parquet datasets?" difficulty="medium" topic="Fundamentals">
A plain Parquet "datase

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
t in folders" lacks transactional guarantees and consistent metadata. Iceberg adds ACID-like commits, consistent snapshots, reliable schema/partition evolution, and query planning features like partition pruning based on metadata. It also supports deletes and updates in a table-like way, rather than requiring you to rewrite everything.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-03" number={3} question="What is a snapshot in Iceberg?" difficulty="medium" topic="Snapshots">
A snapshot is a point-in-time view of the table. Each commit creates a new snapshot that references a set of data and delete files through metadata (manifest lists/manifests). Readers can query a specific snapshot to get consistent results even while writers continue to append or modify the table.
</QuestionCard>

<Questi

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
onCard id="iceberg-04" number={4} question="What is time travel and why is it useful?" difficulty="medium" topic="Time Travel">
Time travel means querying the table "as of" a previous snapshot (or timestamp). It's useful for debugging regressions, auditing changes, reproducing past reports, and safe backfills — computing against a stable snapshot and then committing a new one.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-05" number={5} question="How does Iceberg handle concurrent writes?" difficulty="hard" topic="Concurrency">
Iceberg typically uses optimistic concurrency control. Writers create new metadata based on a known current snapshot and then attempt to commit; if the base snapshot changed in the meantime, the commit may conflict and must be retried. This prevents silent lost updates

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
and keeps commits atomic.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-06" number={6} question="What is hidden partitioning in Iceberg?" difficulty="medium" topic="Partitioning">
Hidden partitioning means partitioning is defined at the table metadata level (for example `day(ts)`), not as user-managed folder paths. Engines can still prune partitions, but you're not tied to a physical directory layout. This makes partition evolution safer and avoids leaking storage details into query logic.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-07" number={7} question="What is partition evolution and why is it important?" difficulty="medium" topic="Partitioning">
Partition evolution allows changing the partition spec over time — for example switching from `day(ts)` to `month(ts)`, or adding a new partiti

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
on field — without rewriting all historical data. It matters because real datasets change: query patterns evolve, and the "right" partitioning today may not be right in a year.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-08" number={8} question="How does schema evolution work in Iceberg?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema">
Iceberg tracks columns by unique field IDs, enabling safe add/drop/rename operations without breaking readers. Schema evolution can be applied while keeping historical snapshots queryable. Some changes, like certain type changes, depend on engine support and can still be risky if consumers assume a fixed schema.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-09" number={9} question="What are equality deletes and positional deletes?" difficulty="hard" topic="Deletes">
Deletes

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
in Iceberg can be represented as separate delete files:

- **Equality deletes** — delete rows matching a key predicate (for example `id = 123`).
- **Positional deletes** — delete specific row positions in data files.

They allow deletes without rewriting full data files, but too many delete files can hurt read performance until they're compacted.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-10" number={10} question="How do upserts/merges work with Iceberg?" difficulty="hard" topic="DML">
Upserts are usually implemented by engines (Spark, Flink, Trino) using `MERGE INTO` semantics, which may create new data files and delete files. The exact behavior depends on the engine and write mode. Operationally, merges are often followed by maintenance jobs to compact data files and remove old snapshot

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
s.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-11" number={11} question="Why does the small files problem happen, and how do you mitigate it?" difficulty="medium" topic="Maintenance">
Small files appear when many writers write tiny batches, or when streaming jobs checkpoint frequently. Too many files increase planning overhead and reduce scan efficiency. Mitigations include setting target file sizes, batching writes, and running compaction/rewrite jobs to merge files.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-12" number={12} question="What maintenance operations are common for Iceberg tables?" difficulty="medium" topic="Maintenance">
Common maintenance includes:

- Rewriting data files (compaction) to hit target file sizes.
- Rewriting manifests to reduce metadata overhead.
- Expiring old

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
snapshots to control metadata growth.
- Removing orphan files.

These jobs keep query planning fast and storage cost under control.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-13" number={13} question="What is an Iceberg catalog, and what options exist?" difficulty="medium" topic="Catalogs">
A catalog stores table metadata pointers and namespace information. Common options include the Hive metastore, a REST catalog, and cloud-native catalogs (for example AWS Glue). Catalog choice affects authorization, interoperability across engines, and operational complexity.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-14" number={14} question="How would you migrate an existing dataset to Iceberg?" difficulty="hard" topic="Migration">
You typically create an Iceberg table schema and then either:

- Regist

title: Iceberg
description: Apache Iceberg interview questions covering snapshots, time travel, hidden partitioning, schema/partition evolution, deletes, and table maintenance.
content:
er existing files in place (where supported), if they already conform to the expected layout.
- Rewrite the data into Iceberg using an engine (Spark/Flink) for safety.

After migration, validate row counts, key constraints, and query performance, then switch readers over to the new table.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="iceberg-15" number={15} question="Iceberg vs. Delta vs. Hudi: when would you choose Iceberg?" difficulty="hard" topic="Comparison">
Iceberg is a strong choice when you need an open, engine-agnostic table format with robust metadata, time travel, and evolution features. It fits well in environments with multiple query engines (Spark + Trino + Flink) and a preference for open standards. The best choice still depends on your stack, operational tooling, and update/CDC requir

ements.

## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"You have multiple engines — Spark, Trino, Flink — that all need to read and write the same tables. How do you avoid stepping on each other?"** Discuss Iceberg's catalog-based metadata, snapshot isolation, and optimistic concurrency control, which let independent engines commit safely against the same table.
- **"A partitioning scheme chosen a year ago no longer matches query patterns — what are your options?"** Walk through partition evolution: changing the partition spec going forward without rewriting historical data, thanks to hidden partitioning decoupling logical partitions from physical layout.
- **"Query planning on an Iceberg table has gotten slow over time — what would you check?"** Talk through snapshot/manifest bloat from frequent small commit

title: Parquet
description: Interview questions covering Parquet columnar storage, compression codecs, row groups, predicate pushdown, partitioning, and data lake optimization.
content:
## Parquet: Columnar Storage Format

**Parquet is an open-source columnar storage format built for efficient analytics — it stores data by column rather than by row, which lets query engines read only what they need and compress it far better than row-oriented formats like CSV.**

title: Parquet
description: Interview questions covering Parquet columnar storage, compression codecs, row groups, predicate pushdown, partitioning, and data lake optimization.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Parquet
description: Interview questions covering Parquet columnar storage, compression codecs, row groups, predicate pushdown, partitioning, and data lake optimization.
content:
## Key Concepts

Parquet's core idea is **columnar storage**: values from the same column are stored contiguously, which enables strong **compression** (Snappy, Gzip, LZO, ZSTD, and others), **dictionary encoding** for low-cardinality columns, and reading only the columns a query actually touches. Physically, a file is organized into **row groups**, each holding a **column chunk** per column, with a **footer** carrying the schema and per-chunk statistics (including min/max values). Those statistics power **predicate pushdown** and row-group skipping, so engines like Spark can avoid scanning data that can't match a filter. Parquet also supports **schema evolution** and **nested/complex types** (arrays, maps, structs) via record shredding, which is why it's the default format for data lakes

title: Parquet
description: Interview questions covering Parquet columnar storage, compression codecs, row groups, predicate pushdown, partitioning, and data lake optimization.
content:
and Spark, and is commonly paired with **partitioning** (directory-based pruning) for further performance gains.

---

Parquet is an open-source columnar storage format designed for efficient data analytics and storage. It's widely used in big data platforms for storing and processing large datasets.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-02" number={2} question="What are the key benefits of Parquet?" difficulty="easy" topic="Benefits">
Key benefits include efficient compression, faster query performance, reduced storage costs, excellent schema evolution support, and compatibility with multiple data processing frameworks.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-03" number={3} question="What is columnar storage?" d

ifficulty="easy" topic="Storage">
Columnar storage stores data by columns instead of rows, allowing efficient compression and faster query performance when accessing specific columns.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-04" number={4} question="How does Parquet compare to CSV?" difficulty="easy" topic="Comparison">
Parquet is compressed (saves around 90% storage vs. raw text), faster to read (only reads needed columns), supports nested data, and has better data type preservation. CSV is text-based, uncompressed, row-oriented, and less efficient for analytical queries.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-05" number={5} question="What is the structure of a Parquet file?" difficulty="medium" topic="File Structure">
A Parquet file contains: magic bytes (header), one or more row g

roups, metadata, and a footer with schema and statistics information.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-06" number={6} question="What compression algorithms does Parquet support?" difficulty="medium" topic="Compression">
Parquet supports Snappy, Gzip, LZO, Brotli, LZ4, ZSTD, and uncompressed. Snappy is the default.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-07" number={7} question="What is Snappy compression?" difficulty="easy" topic="Compression">
Snappy is a fast compression algorithm that prioritizes speed over compression ratio, offering a good balance between compression and query performance.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="parquet-08" number={8} question="What is Gzip compression?" difficulty="easy" topic="Compression">
Gzip provides higher compression ratios than Snappy

<QuestionCard id="parquet-09" number={9} question="What is LZO compression?" difficulty="medium" topic="Compression">
LZO provides fast compression/decompression with moderate compression ratios. It's useful for bandwidth-limited scenarios.

<QuestionCard id="parquet-10" number={10} question="How does Parquet handle schema evolution?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema">
Parquet supports adding, removing, and reordering columns, allowing schema changes without rewriting entire files.

<QuestionCard id="parquet-11" number={11} question="What is a Parquet footer?" difficulty="medium" topic="File Structure">
The footer contains metadata about the file: schema, row gro

A row group is a collection of rows that are stored together. Each row group contains column chunks for each column.

<QuestionCard id="parquet-13" number={13} question="What is a column chunk?" difficulty="medium" topic="File Structure">

## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Why would you choose Parquet over CSV for a data lake?"** Walk through compression ratio, columnar reads for wide tables, predicate pushdown via row-group statistics, and schema evolution — then contrast with CSV's lack of types, compression, and column pruning.
- **"A query scanning a large Parquet dataset is slow — how do you speed it up?"** Talk through partitioning by a commonly filtered column, right-sizing row groups, choosing an appropriate compression codec, and confirming predicate pushdown is actually happening (filter on a partition/statistics-friendly column).
- **"How does Parquet support schema evolution when new columns get added upstream?"** Explain that new/removed/reordered columns don't require rewriting existing files, and that reader

s reconcile the file's schema against the expected schema at read time.

title: Level 3 - Technologies
description: Modern data engineering technologies across batch, streaming, cloud, and BI platforms
content:
## Level 3 - Technologies: Tools & Platforms

This section covers the specific technologies and tools used in modern data engineering. It's organized by function area to help you understand the landscape of data engineering tools.

title: Level 3 - Technologies
description: Modern data engineering technologies across batch, streaming, cloud, and BI platforms
content:
## Technology Categories

- **Batch Processing**: Hadoop, Spark, Hive for large-scale batch data processing
- **Streaming**: Kafka, Flink for real-time data ingestion and processing
- **Cloud**: AWS, GCP, Azure cloud data platforms and services
- **Databases**: BigQuery, Redshift, MongoDB, Cassandra, HBase, DynamoDB, and others
- **Data Formats**: Parquet, Avro, Delta, Hudi, Iceberg for efficient data storage
- **Orchestration**: Airflow, Kubernetes for workflow and infrastructure orchestration
- **Ingestion**: NiFi, Flume, CDC for data ingestion patterns
- **Transformation**: dbt for data transformation and modeling
- **BI Tools**: Tableau, Looker, Superset for visualization and analytics

These technologies form the modern data stack and understanding them is crucial for data engineers.

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
## CDC: Capturing and Streaming Database Changes

**Change Data Capture (CDC) streams row-level inserts, updates, and deletes out of a source system in near real time, replacing expensive full-table batch scans with a continuous, ordered log of what changed.**

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
## Key Concepts

CDC captures row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) from a source system and streams them downstream, most commonly by reading the database's transaction log (WAL/binlog) rather than repeatedly scanning full tables. A production CDC pipeline almost always needs both a **snapshot** (a consistent baseline of existing rows) and **incremental** capture (an ongoing stream of changes from a log position/offset onward). Correctness hinges on a handful of hard problems: **delivery semantics** (at-least-once is the realistic default, made safe with idempotent writes), **ordering** (per-key or per-partition, since events can arrive out of order or be retried), **offset/watermark management** (durably tracking how far you've processed), **schema evolution**, and **delete handl

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
ing** (hard delete, soft delete, or full history). Patterns like the **outbox pattern** sidestep dual-write problems by writing business events to a table CDC can capture in the same transaction as the application write. Because of all this, mature teams treat CDC pipelines as production software — with lag monitoring, safe backfill strategies, and strong observability — rather than a "just wire it up" integration.

---

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="CDC" storagePrefix="cdc">

<QuestionCard id="cdc-01" number={1} question="What is CDC (Change Data Capture)?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
CDC is a technique to capture row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) from a source system and stream them downstream. In databases, CDC is commonly log-based (reading the WAL/binlog) so it can capture changes without repeatedly scanning full tables. CDC pipelines often feed event logs (append-only) and/or materialized current-state tables.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-02" number={2} question="When would you choose CDC over batch ingestion?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Choose CDC when you need low-latency updates, continuous incremental processing, or a reliable change h

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
istory. CDC is also useful when full-table scans are too expensive because the source is large or changes frequently. Batch ingestion is often simpler when latency requirements are relaxed and operational complexity should be minimized.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-03" number={3} question="What are common CDC sources (WAL/binlog/triggers) and their trade-offs?" difficulty="medium" topic="Sources">
Common sources are:
- **Database logs (WAL/binlog)** — low overhead and high fidelity, but requires elevated privileges and careful offset handling
- **Triggers** — can work without log access, but adds write overhead and is easy to break
- **Timestamp-based queries** — simple but can miss updates or create duplicates if clocks/transactions behave unexpectedly

Log-based CDC is usually

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
preferred for production at scale.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-04" number={4} question="What is the difference between snapshot and incremental CDC?" difficulty="medium" topic="Snapshotting">
A snapshot reads an initial consistent baseline of existing rows. Incremental CDC then streams changes after that baseline using a log position/offset. Many systems require both: snapshot for bootstrap, incremental for ongoing updates.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-05" number={5} question="What delivery semantics exist (at-most-once/at-least-once/exactly-once)?" difficulty="medium" topic="Semantics">
At-most-once can lose messages but avoids duplicates. At-least-once avoids loss but can produce duplicates on retries. Exactly-once is hard to achieve end-to-end; many systems instead

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
implement "effectively once" by combining at-least-once delivery with idempotent writes and deterministic merge logic.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-06" number={6} question="How do you make CDC ingestion idempotent?" difficulty="hard" topic="Idempotency">
You make downstream writes idempotent by using stable keys (primary key + source LSN/transaction id) and applying deterministic upsert/merge logic. A common pattern is to load changes into an append-only raw log, then build current-state tables using "latest per key" logic. Idempotency must hold across retries and replays.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-07" number={7} question="How do you handle deletes in CDC pipelines?" difficulty="medium" topic="Deletes">
Deletes may appear as explicit delete events, tombstones, or a "

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
before image" without an "after image." Downstream you can:
- apply hard deletes (remove the row)
- apply soft deletes (an `is_deleted` flag with `deleted_at`)
- keep full history (append-only) and compute current state with delete logic

The choice depends on compliance requirements, analytics needs, and downstream consumers.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-08" number={8} question="How do you handle updates when the source does not provide full row images?" difficulty="hard" topic="Updates">
If updates are partial (only changed columns), you often need to reconstruct the full row by joining against previous state. Some CDC tools can be configured to emit before/after images; if not, current-state reconstruction becomes part of your pipeline. You must also handle out-of-order update

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
s carefully.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-09" number={9} question="What is ordering and why is it hard in CDC?" difficulty="hard" topic="Ordering">
Ordering means applying changes in the same order they happened in the source. It is hard because events can be partitioned (across multiple tables/partitions), delivered out of order, or retried. Correctness often requires per-key ordering (or per-partition ordering) using source transaction metadata.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-10" number={10} question="What is a watermark/offset and where should it be stored?" difficulty="medium" topic="Offsets">
An offset (watermark) is the position in the source log (or stream) up to which changes have been processed. It should be stored durably in the ingestion system's connector stat

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
e store and, for reliability, often also in your platform (for example, a control table) so you can audit and recover. Offsets must be updated atomically with downstream writes to avoid gaps or duplicates.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-11" number={11} question="How do you handle schema evolution with CDC?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema Evolution">
You need a strategy for compatible changes (additive columns) and breaking changes (renames, type changes). Common tools include schema registries and compatibility policies, plus strict validation in CI. Downstream tables should tolerate additive fields, and pipelines should alert on breaking changes before production data is corrupted.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-12" number={12} question="What is the outbox pattern and w

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
hy is it useful?" difficulty="medium" topic="Patterns">
The outbox pattern writes business events into an "outbox" table in the same transaction as the application write. CDC then captures the outbox changes and publishes them reliably. This avoids dual-write problems where the database update succeeds but the event publish fails (or vice versa).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-13" number={13} question="How do you monitor CDC lag and where can lag come from?" difficulty="medium" topic="Monitoring">
Lag can come from several layers:
- **source** — heavy write load, log retention limits
- **connector** — slow polling, backpressure, failures
- **broker/stream** — throughput limits, partition hotspots
- **downstream** — slow merges/compaction, expensive transformations

You monitor lag

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
using source LSN vs. processed LSN, consumer offsets, and end-to-end freshness in target tables.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-14" number={14} question="How do you design a safe backfill/reprocessing strategy for CDC?" difficulty="hard" topic="Backfill">
A safe strategy usually separates raw change storage from derived tables. You keep the raw CDC log (immutable) and rebuild derived current-state/marts from it when needed. For backfills, you often run in a separate environment or commit to new snapshots/versions, then switch consumers over after validation.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="cdc-15" number={15} question="What are the most common failure modes in CDC pipelines?" difficulty="medium" topic="Reliability">
Common issues include:
- duplicates due to retries/rebalances
-

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
missed events due to offset mismanagement or log retention
- schema drift/breaking changes
- incorrect delete handling
- out-of-order application of updates

Good pipelines treat CDC as "production software" with strong observability and tests.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a CDC pipeline from Postgres into a warehouse that must stay under 1 minute of freshness lag."** Talk through log-based capture (WAL), a snapshot-then-incremental bootstrap, idempotent merge on primary key + LSN, and how you'd monitor end-to-end lag.
- **"Two teams built two CDC consumers off the same stream and their row counts disagree."** Walk through duplicate causes (retries/rebalances without idempotent writes), out-of-order updates being applied non-deterministically, and inconsistent delete handling between the two consumers.
- **"The source database just underwent a breaking schema change mid-stream — what happens to your pipeline?"** Discuss schema registries, compatibility policies, why downstream tables should tolerate additive changes

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
, and how you'd alert on breaking changes before they corrupt production data.
- **"How would you avoid the dual-write problem when an application needs to update its database and publish an event?"** This is the outbox pattern — walk through why writing the event to a table in the same transaction, then letting CDC capture and publish it, is safer than writing to the database and a message queue separately.

---

title: CDC
description: Change Data Capture interview questions covering log-based CDC, delivery semantics, idempotency, schema evolution, and the outbox pattern for engineers.
content:
## Further Reading

- [Flume](/level-3-technologies/ingestion/flume/) — a log-collection alternative to CDC for streaming ingestion
- [NiFi](/level-3-technologies/ingestion/nifi/) — a flow-based tool often used to route and transform CDC streams
- [Kafka](/level-3-technologies/streaming/kafka/) — the most common transport layer for CDC event streams

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
## Flume: Distributed Log Collection for Hadoop

**Apache Flume is a distributed service for collecting, aggregating, and moving large volumes of log data into Hadoop, built around a simple agent pipeline of sources, channels, and sinks.**

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
## Key Concepts

A Flume **Agent** is a JVM process hosting the three core components of every flow: a **Source** (receives events from an external system), a **Channel** (a temporary store that holds events until a sink consumes them), and a **Sink** (removes events from the channel and forwards them to a destination like HDFS or another agent). The **Channel** you pick is a reliability/throughput trade-off — MEMORY is fastest but can lose data, FILE is the most durable because it persists to disk before acknowledging, and JDBC stores events in an embedded Derby database. **Channel Selectors** (replicating or multiplexing) decide whether an event goes to one channel or is fanned out to several, and **Interceptors** sit between source/channel or channel/sink to filter or enrich events in f

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
light. Reliability comes from channel-based **transactions**: an event only moves to the next hop once both the sending and receiving agent commit their transaction, giving Flume end-to-end guaranteed delivery. Multi-hop topologies are wired together with the **Avro RPC** mechanism, and agent behavior is entirely driven by local configuration files defining each agent's sources, channels, and sinks.

---

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content: ## Interview Questions

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
### Core Architecture & Components

<QuestionList topic="Flume Core Architecture" storagePrefix="flume-core">

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-01" number={1} question="What is Apache Flume?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Apache Flume is a distributed, reliable, and available service for efficiently collecting, aggregating, and moving large amounts of log data from many different sources to a centralized data store — most commonly Hadoop. It has a simple, flexible architecture based on streaming data flows, is robust and fault-tolerant with tunable reliability mechanisms and failover/recovery mechanisms, and uses a simple extensible data model that supports online analytic applications.

Flume is fundamentally a framework for populating Hadoop with data: agents are deployed throughou

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
t an organization's IT infrastructure — inside web servers, application servers, and mobile devices, for example — to collect data and integrate it into Hadoop.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-02" number={2} question="What is an Agent?" difficulty="easy" topic="Architecture">
An Agent is a process that hosts Flume components such as sources, channels, and sinks, and thus has the ability to receive, store, and forward events to their destination.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-03" number={3} question="What is a Channel?" difficulty="easy" topic="Architecture">
A Channel stores events. Events are delivered to the channel via sources operating within the agent, and an event stays in the channel until a sink removes it for further transport.
</QuestionCard>

<Quest

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
ionCard id="flume-core-04" number={4} question="What are Flume's core components?" difficulty="easy" topic="Architecture">
Source, Channel, and Sink are the core components in Apache Flume. When a Flume source receives an event from an external source, it stores the event in one or multiple channels. The channel temporarily stores and holds the event until it's consumed by the sink — acting as Flume's repository. The sink then removes the event from the channel and puts it into an external repository like HDFS, or moves it on to the next Flume hop.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-05" number={5} question="What is a Flume Event?" difficulty="medium" topic="Architecture">
A Flume Event is a unit of data with a set of string attributes. An external source, like a web server, send

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
s events to the source. Internally, Flume has built-in functionality to understand the source's format — each log file is typically considered an event. Each event has a header and a value section: the header holds header information, and the value is the appropriate content assigned to that header.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-06" number={6} question="Why Flume?" difficulty="medium" topic="Use Cases">
Flume isn't limited to collecting logs from distributed systems — it's capable of other use cases too, such as:
- collecting readings from an array of sensors
- collecting impressions from custom apps for an ad network
- collecting readings from network devices in order to monitor their performance

Flume is designed to preserve reliability, scalability, manageability, and e

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
xtensibility while serving a maximum number of clients with higher quality of service (QoS).
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-07" number={7} question="Why are we using Flume?" difficulty="easy" topic="Use Cases">
Hadoop developers most often use this tool to get data from social media sites. It was developed by Cloudera for aggregating and moving very large amounts of data. Its primary use is to gather log files from different sources and asynchronously persist them into a Hadoop cluster.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-core-08" number={8} question="Name two features of Flume." difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Flume collects data efficiently, and aggregates and moves large amounts of log data from many different sources into a centralized data store. It's also

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
not restricted to log data aggregation — it can transport massive quantities of event data including, but not limited to, network traffic data, social-media-generated data, email messages, and pretty much any other data source.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
### Channels, Reliability & Failure Handling

<QuestionList topic="Flume Reliability" storagePrefix="flume-reliability">

<QuestionCard id="flume-reliability-01" number={9} question="Which is the reliable channel in Flume to ensure there is no data loss?" difficulty="medium" topic="Channels">
The FILE channel is the most reliable channel among the three (JDBC, FILE, and MEMORY), because it writes events to disk before acknowledging them.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-reliability-02" number={10} question="Explain the different channel types in Flume, and which channel type is faster?" difficulty="medium" topic="Channels">
The three built-in channel types available in Flume are:
- **MEMORY Channel** — events are read from the source into memory and passed to the sink
- **JDBC Chan

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
nel** — stores events in an embedded Derby database
- **FILE Channel** — writes the contents to a file on the file system after reading the event from a source; the file is deleted only after the contents are successfully delivered to the sink

MEMORY Channel is the fastest of the three, but carries the risk of data loss. The channel you choose depends entirely on the nature of the big data application and the value of each event.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-reliability-03" number={11} question="Does Flume provide 100% reliability to the data flow?" difficulty="medium" topic="Reliability">
Yes — Apache Flume provides end-to-end reliability because of its transactional approach to data flow.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-reliability-04" number={12} question="Explain

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
reliability and failure handling in Apache Flume." difficulty="hard" topic="Reliability">
Flume NG uses channel-based transactions to guarantee reliable message delivery. When a message moves from one agent to another, two transactions are started: one on the agent that delivers the event, and one on the agent that receives it. For the sending agent to commit its transaction, it must receive a success indication from the receiving agent. The receiving agent only returns a success indication once its own transaction commits properly first. This guarantees delivery semantics between every hop the flow makes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-reliability-05" number={13} question="Explain the replication and multiplexing selectors in Flume." difficulty="hard" topic="Channels">
Channel S

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
electors are used to handle multiple channels. Based on the Flume header value, an event can be written to just a single channel or to multiple channels. If a channel selector isn't specified for a source, the default is the Replicating selector, which writes the same event to all the channels in the source's channel list. The Multiplexing channel selector is used when the application needs to send different events to different channels.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
### Sinks, Integrations & Data Flow

<QuestionList topic="Flume Sinks and Integrations" storagePrefix="flume-sinks">

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-01" number={14} question="How can Flume be used with HBase?" difficulty="hard" topic="Sinks">
Apache Flume can be used with HBase using one of two HBase sinks:
- **HBaseSink** (`org.apache.flume.sink.hbase.HBaseSink`) supports secure HBase clusters and also the newer HBase IPC introduced in HBase 0.96.
- **AsyncHBaseSink** (`org.apache.flume.sink.hbase.AsyncHBaseSink`) has better performance than HBaseSink, since it can make non-blocking calls to HBase.

**HBaseSink** converts a Flume Event into HBase Increments or Puts: a serializer implements `HBaseEventSerializer`, which is instantiated when the sink starts. For every event, the sink calls t

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
he serializer's `initialize` method, which translates the Flume Event into the HBase increments and puts sent to the HBase cluster.

**AsyncHBaseSink** implements `AsyncHBaseEventSerializer`. Its `initialize` method is called only once, when the sink starts. The sink invokes `setEvent` and then calls `getIncrements` and `getActions`, similar to HBaseSink. When the sink stops, the serializer's `cleanUp` method is called.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-02" number={15} question="Differentiate between FileSink and FileRollSink." difficulty="medium" topic="Sinks">
The major difference is where the events end up: HDFS FileSink writes events into the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS), whereas FileRollSink stores events into the local file system.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
id="flume-sinks-03" number={16} question="Does Apache Flume provide support for third-party plugins?" difficulty="easy" topic="Extensibility">
Yes — most data analysts rely on Apache Flume's plug-in-based architecture, since it can load data from external sources and transfer it to external destinations.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-04" number={17} question="Can Flume distribute data to multiple destinations?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Flow">
Yes. It supports multiplexing flows — an event can flow from one source to multiple channels and multiple destinations. This is achieved by defining a flow multiplexer.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-05" number={18} question="How can a multi-hop agent be set up in Flume?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Flow">

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
The Avro RPC Bridge mechanism is used to set up a multi-hop agent in Apache Flume.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-06" number={19} question="Can you explain configuration files in Flume?" difficulty="easy" topic="Configuration">
The agent's configuration is stored in a local configuration file. It comprises each agent's source, sink, and channel information.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-07" number={20} question="What are Interceptors?" difficulty="medium" topic="Data Flow">
Interceptors are used to filter events between a source and channel, or between a channel and sink. These interceptors can filter out unnecessary or targeted log entries. You can chain as many interceptors as your requirements need.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-sinks-08" numb

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
er={21} question="Is it possible to leverage real-time analysis on the big data collected by Flume directly?" difficulty="medium" topic="Integrations">
Yes — data from Flume can be extracted, transformed, and loaded in real time into Apache Solr servers using the `MorphlineSolrSink`.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
### Flume in the Hadoop Ecosystem

<QuestionList topic="Flume Ecosystem" storagePrefix="flume-ecosystem">

<QuestionCard id="flume-ecosystem-01" number={22} question="What is Flume-NG?" difficulty="easy" topic="Ecosystem">
Flume-NG is a real-time loader for streaming data into Hadoop. It stores data in HDFS and HBase. It's the successor to the original Flume and the version you'll want to get started with, since it improves substantially on the original.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-ecosystem-02" number={23} question="What are the complicated steps in Flume configuration?" difficulty="medium" topic="Configuration">
Flume processes streaming data, so once started there is no stop/end to the process — it asynchronously flows data from source to HDFS via an agent. First, the agent

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
needs to know how its individual components are connected to load data, so configuration is what triggers loading of streaming data. For example, `consumerKey`, `consumerSecret`, `accessToken`, and `accessTokenSecret` are the key factors needed to download data from Twitter.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-ecosystem-03" number={24} question="What are the data extraction tools in Hadoop?" difficulty="easy" topic="Ecosystem">
Sqoop can be used to transfer data between RDBMS and HDFS. Flume can be used to extract streaming data from social media, web logs, and similar sources, and store it on HDFS.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="flume-ecosystem-04" number={25} question="What are the similarities and differences between Apache Flume and Apache Kafka?" difficulty="medium" topic="E

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
cosystem">
Flume pushes messages to their destination via its Sinks. With Kafka, you instead need to consume messages from the Kafka Broker using a Kafka Consumer API — Kafka is pull-based rather than push-based. Both move large volumes of event data, but Flume is purpose-built for ingesting data into Hadoop-ecosystem sinks, while Kafka is a general-purpose distributed log that many different consumers can read from independently.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a Flume agent that ships web server logs into HDFS without losing data during a downstream outage."** Talk through choosing the FILE channel over MEMORY for durability, how channel-based transactions guarantee each hop only advances after a commit, and where backpressure builds up if HDFS becomes slow.
- **"You need the same event stream to go to both HDFS for archival and Solr for real-time search."** Explain the Replicating channel selector for fan-out, versus Multiplexing when different event types need to go to different channels, and mention `MorphlineSolrSink` for the real-time path.
- **"How would you decide between Flume and Kafka for a new ingestion pipeline?"** Walk through the push vs. pull distinction, Flume's built-in Hadoop-ecosystem

title: Flume
description: Apache Flume interview questions covering agents, channels, sinks, reliability, interceptors, and how Flume compares to Kafka for log ingestion pipelines.
content:
sinks vs. Kafka's general-purpose consumer model, and when you might use both together (Flume as a Kafka producer, for example).
