title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
import QuestionCard from '../../../components/mdx/QuestionCard.astro';
import QuestionList from '../../../components/mdx/QuestionList.astro';

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
## Data Observability: Monitoring and Incident Response

**Data observability is the practice of monitoring, alerting, and diagnosing the health of data and data pipelines — catching silent failures before they reach a dashboard, and cutting the time it takes to find root cause once something breaks.**

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
## Key Concepts

Data observability differs from application observability in what it watches: instead of latency and error rates for request/response services, it tracks dataset health over time — **freshness** (is data arriving on schedule), **volume** (are row/byte/partition counts within expected bounds), **schema** (did columns get added, removed, or retyped), and **quality** (nulls, uniqueness, referential integrity). These signals matter because data failures are often silent — a job succeeds, but the data is wrong, missing a subset, or duplicated — so detection depends on dataset-level checks and reconciliation rather than job exit codes alone. When something breaks, **lineage** tells you the blast radius (which downstream tables and dashboards are affected), and a good **runbook**

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
turns an alert into a repeatable recovery process rather than a from-scratch investigation.

---

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="Observability" storagePrefix="observability">

<QuestionCard id="obs-01" number={1} question="What is data observability?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Data observability is the practice of monitoring, alerting, and diagnosing the health of data and data pipelines. It focuses on whether datasets are arriving on time, with correct volume and values, and whether downstream consumers can rely on them. The goal is to reduce "data downtime" and speed up root-cause analysis.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-02" number={2} question="How is data observability different from application observability?" difficulty="easy" topic="Fundamentals">
Application observability focuses on service health (latency, error rates, traces) for request/re

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
sponse systems. Data observability focuses on dataset health and pipeline behavior over time (freshness, completeness, distribution shifts). Data issues can be silent, delayed, and cumulative, so you need different signals and baselines.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-03" number={3} question="What are the key signals you monitor for data pipelines?" difficulty="medium" topic="Signals">
Common signals include:

- **Freshness** — track max event/ingestion time to ensure data is arriving on schedule
- **Volume** — monitor row counts, bytes, and partitions for anomalies
- **Schema changes** — detect added, removed, or typed columns that may break downstream
- **Quality checks** — validate null rates, uniqueness, and referential integrity
- **Pipeline run status** — track job success/fa

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
ilure and execution duration
- **Cost signals** — monitor query time, bytes scanned, and cluster utilization
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-04" number={4} question="How do you define and measure an end-to-end SLA for data?" difficulty="medium" topic="SLAs">
You define the SLA from source event time to availability in the final dataset or BI metric. Measure it with timestamps at each stage (ingest, transform, publish) and compute percentiles (p50/p95) as well as breach counts. SLAs should reflect consumer needs and include ownership and escalation paths.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-05" number={5} question="What should you log for each pipeline run?" difficulty="easy" topic="Logging">
At minimum:

- **Inputs** — source tables/partitions, offsets, watermarks
- **Outputs** —

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
target tables/partitions, row counts written
- **Runtime and resource usage** — CPU, memory, execution time
- **Warnings/errors** — retry attempts, failure details
- **Data quality results** — summary of validation checks

This makes runs reproducible and speeds up debugging.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-06" number={6} question="How do you detect silent failures?" difficulty="hard" topic="Failure Detection">
Silent failures happen when the job succeeds but data is wrong (missing subset, wrong join, truncation). Detect them with dataset-level checks: freshness, volume bounds, distribution checks, and reconciliations against sources. Monitoring key business KPIs for unexpected changes is also effective.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-07" number={7} question="What is lineag

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
e and how does it help during incidents?" difficulty="medium" topic="Lineage">
Lineage describes upstream/downstream dependencies between datasets, jobs, and dashboards. During incidents, lineage helps you assess blast radius (which reports are impacted) and prioritize fixes. It also helps prevent regressions by making dependencies explicit.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-08" number={8} question="How do you monitor and debug a broken metric in BI?" difficulty="hard" topic="Incident Response">
You start from the metric definition and trace dependencies to the underlying tables. Then check data freshness and recent changes (deploys, schema changes, upstream outages). Use reconciliation queries and sampling to locate where the metric diverged, then confirm whether it is a data issue o

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
r a definition change.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-09" number={9} question="How do you design alerts to avoid alert fatigue?" difficulty="medium" topic="Alerting">
Alerts should be actionable, scoped, and owned. Use severity levels, suppression windows, and baselines that account for seasonality. Prefer multi-signal alerts (freshness + volume) over single noisy checks, and include runbooks with clear next steps.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-10" number={10} question="How do you approach backfills safely?" difficulty="hard" topic="Backfills">
Backfills can be expensive and risky because they rewrite history and can change metrics. A safe approach includes running in smaller batches, validating each batch, and separating "raw" from "published" layers so you can rebuild de

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
rived tables without re-ingesting sources. You also communicate expected metric changes and schedule around peak usage.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-11" number={11} question="What is a runbook and what should it contain?" difficulty="medium" topic="Runbooks">
A runbook is a step-by-step guide for responding to an alert or incident. It should include impact assessment, common root causes, validation queries, mitigation steps, rollback/backfill steps, and escalation contacts. Good runbooks reduce time-to-recovery for on-call engineers.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="obs-12" number={12} question="What are common incident patterns in data platforms?" difficulty="medium" topic="Incident Patterns">
Common patterns include:

- **Upstream outages** — late or missing data from source

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
systems
- **Schema changes** — breaking transforms when columns are added/removed/changed
- **Duplicates** — data duplication from retries or reprocessing
- **Incorrect joins** — metric inflation due to join logic errors
- **Partition/timezone bugs** — incorrect data filtering due to boundary issues
- **Cost spikes** — expensive queries consuming excessive resources
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: Data Observability
description: Data observability interview questions covering monitoring signals, SLAs, lineage, alerting, backfills, runbooks, and incident response for data pipelines.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Freshness on a critical table breaches SLA at 3am — walk through your triage steps."** Tests signal literacy, runbook use, and escalation instincts.
- **"A BI dashboard shows revenue dropped 15% but nothing alerted — how did that slip through, and how do you prevent it next time?"** Tests silent-failure detection and multi-signal alerting.
- **"You need to backfill six months of a core fact table without breaking downstream dashboards — how do you approach it?"** Tests safe backfill strategy and raw/published layer separation.
- **"Design the logging and lineage you'd add to a new pipeline so the next on-call engineer isn't starting from zero."** Tests logging minimums and the practical value of lineage.

---

import QuestionCard from '../../../components/mdx/QuestionCard.astro';
import QuestionList from '../../../components/mdx/QuestionList.astro';

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
## Data System Design: Architecture & Patterns

**Master system design concepts for building scalable data platforms and pipelines — from batch and streaming architecture to reliability, schema evolution, and multi-tenancy.**

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
## Table of Contents
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Interview Questions](#interview-questions)
- [Common Interview Scenarios](#common-interview-scenarios)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

---

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
## Key Concepts

Data system design questions test whether you can turn a vague business requirement into a concrete architecture. Most answers draw from a small set of recurring building blocks: a **layered storage model** (raw/silver/gold) that separates immutable ingestion from cleaned and business-ready data; a **batch vs. streaming** decision driven by latency needs versus complexity and cost; **reliability patterns** — idempotent writes, retries, dead-letter queues, checkpointing — that make pipelines safe to rerun; and handling for the messy realities of production data, like **late-arriving events**, **schema evolution**, and **backfills**. Around all of that sit platform-level concerns — **multi-tenancy**, **observability**, and **cost vs. SLA trade-offs** — that turn a single pip

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
eline into a platform other teams can build on safely.

---

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
## Interview Questions

<QuestionList topic="System Design" storagePrefix="system-design">

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-01" number={1} question="How would you design a batch analytics pipeline?" difficulty="medium" topic="Batch Design">
A typical design ingests data into a raw layer (immutable), transforms it into curated datasets (cleaned and modeled), and publishes it into marts or BI-serving datasets. You define SLAs, data quality checks, and clear ownership for each dataset along the way. Batch pipelines are usually scheduled daily or hourly and optimized for correctness and cost rather than latency.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-02" number={2} question="How would you design a near-real-time ingestion pipeline?" difficulty="hard" topic="Streaming">
You typically use an e

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
vent log or CDC stream (Kafka, Pub/Sub, Kinesis) and write to a raw, append-only store with durable offsets. Downstream, you materialize current-state tables and metrics using windowing and deduplication. The hard parts are designing for replays, ordering guarantees, and late-arriving events, since streaming systems don't get a second batch run to fix mistakes.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-03" number={3} question="How do you ensure idempotency in data pipelines?" difficulty="medium" topic="Reliability">
Idempotency means retries don't change the final result. Common strategies include writing with deterministic keys (upserts or merges instead of blind appends), using exactly-once features where the engine supports them, and keeping raw data immutable while rebuilding derive

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
d layers from it. You also need to store checkpoints and watermarks consistently with the writes they correspond to, so a retry replays from a known-good point.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-04" number={4} question="How do you handle late-arriving events and backfills?" difficulty="hard" topic="Data Quality">
You define an allowed lateness window and design tables keyed by event time, not just ingestion time. For late events, you reprocess the affected windows or partitions and update derived outputs deterministically, so reprocessing produces the same result regardless of when it runs. Backfills specifically should be incremental, validated, and isolated so they don't corrupt current reporting while they're in flight.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-05" number=

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
{5} question="When would you choose streaming over batch processing, and vice versa?" difficulty="medium" topic="Architecture">
Streaming is chosen for low latency and continuous updates, but it adds real complexity — state management, ordering, exactly-once semantics. Batch is simpler and often cheaper for many analytics use cases where near-real-time freshness isn't a hard requirement. Many real systems end up hybrid: streaming ingestion into a raw layer, with batch modeling on top to build marts.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-06" number={6} question="What is the raw/silver/gold layering pattern, and why use it?" difficulty="easy" topic="Data Modeling">
Raw (or bronze) is immutable ingestion with minimal transformation. Silver is cleaned, standardized, and deduplicated dat

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
a with consistent schemas. Gold is business-ready marts and aggregates designed for BI and applications. This layering helps with reprocessing (you can always rebuild silver/gold from raw), governance, and giving each layer a clear contract with its consumers.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-07" number={7} question="How do you design a multi-tenant data platform?" difficulty="hard" topic="Platform Design">
You need isolation — separate compute or quotas per tenant — clear ownership, and strong governance. Provide shared standards for naming, testing, and documentation, plus a catalog for discovery so tenants can find and trust each other's datasets. Multi-tenancy also requires controlling cost and preventing one team's workloads from impacting others, which usually means resou

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
rce quotas and workload separation.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-08" number={8} question="How do you handle schema evolution in a data platform?" difficulty="medium" topic="Schema">
You define compatibility rules, version schemas or contracts, and enforce them in CI and at ingestion. Downstream transforms should tolerate additive fields but fail fast on breaking changes rather than silently producing wrong data. You also maintain backward-compatible outputs wherever multiple consumers depend on a stable schema, since you can't force every consumer to upgrade at once.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-09" number={9} question="What reliability patterns do you use for production data pipelines?" difficulty="medium" topic="Reliability">
Common patterns include idempo

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
tent writes, retries with backoff, dead-letter queues for records that repeatedly fail, exactly-once or effectively-once processing, and separating raw from derived layers so derived data can always be rebuilt. You also rely on checkpointing and consistent watermark management to avoid gaps and duplicates across restarts.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-10" number={10} question="What does platform observability look like for a data system?" difficulty="medium" topic="Observability">
You monitor freshness, volume, quality, and lineage, plus pipeline runtime and failure rates. Alerts should be actionable and tied to runbooks, not just noise that gets ignored. Observability should be end-to-end — from source systems all the way through to the final BI datasets and the metrics sta

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
keholders actually look at.
</QuestionCard>

<QuestionCard id="sysdesign-11" number={11} question="How do you manage cost vs. SLA trade-offs in system design?" difficulty="medium" topic="Cost">
You optimize storage layout (partitioning, file sizes), avoid unnecessary recomputation, and use selective materialization rather than materializing everything. You also schedule heavy jobs off-peak, enforce resource quotas, and track cost per dataset or pipeline so spend is visible and attributable. Cost has to be part of the design from the start, not an afterthought bolted on once the bill is too high.
</QuestionCard>

</QuestionList>

---

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
## Common Interview Scenarios

- **"Design a data platform that ingests clickstream events and serves near-real-time dashboards."** Walk through the streaming ingestion choice, the raw/silver/gold layering, windowing and deduplication, and how you'd handle late events without corrupting already-served metrics.
- **"A backfill you ran overwrote correct data with stale data — how do you prevent that next time?"** Talk through isolating backfills from live pipelines, validating output before promoting it, and designing idempotent, deterministic reprocessing keyed by event time.
- **"Multiple teams want to share one data platform — how do you design for that?"** Cover isolation and quotas, a shared catalog and naming standards, and cost/SLA guardrails that stop one tenant's workload from degra

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:
ding everyone else's.
- **"How would you detect and diagnose a silent data quality problem before a stakeholder does?"** Describe the observability layer — freshness, volume, and quality monitors with actionable alerts — spanning from source ingestion through to the gold-layer datasets people actually query.

---

title: System Design
description: Data platform system design interview questions covering pipeline architecture, streaming, idempotency, layering, multi-tenancy, and reliability patterns.
content:

- [Observability](/level-4-advanced/observability/) — a deeper look at monitoring freshness, quality, and pipeline health
- [Streaming](/level-3-technologies/streaming/) — the tools and patterns behind the near-real-time ingestion designs above
